Cup products, the Heisenberg group, and codimension two algebraic cycles

Ettore Aldrovandi and Niranjan Ramachandran Ettore Aldrovandi, Department of Mathematics, Florida State University, Tallahassee, FL 32306-4510 USA. aldrovandi@math.fsu.edu http://www.math.fsu.edu/ ealdrov Niranjan Ramachandran, Department of Mathematics, University of Maryland, College Park, MD 20742 USA. atma@math.umd.edu http://www2.math.umd.edu/ atma/
(Date: May 2, 2026)
Abstract.

We define higher categorical invariants (gerbes) of codimension two algebraic cycles and provide a categorical interpretation of the intersection of divisors on a smooth algebraic variety. This generalization of the classical relation between divisors and line bundles furnishes a new perspective on the Bloch-Quillen formula.

Key words and phrases:
Algebraic cycles, gerbes, Heisenberg group, higher categories
2010 Mathematics Subject Classification:
14C25, 14F42, 55P20, 55N15
The second author’s research was supported by the 2015-2016 “Research and Scholarship Award” from the Graduate School, University of Maryland.
Contents

  2. 2 Preliminaries
    1. 2.1 Abelian Gerbes [21, 16, 7]
    2. 2.2 The gerbe of lifts associated with a central extension
    3. 2.3 Four-term complexes
  3. 3 The Heisenberg group
    1. 3.1 The Heisenberg group
    2. 3.2 Extension to sheaves
    3. 3.3 Simplicial computations
    4. 3.4 The cup product
  4. 4 Examples and connections to prior results
    1. 4.1 Self-cup products of Poonen-Rains
    2. 4.2 Brylinski’s work on regulators and étale analogues
    3. 4.3 Finite flat group schemes
    4. 4.4 The gerbe associated with a pair of divisors
    5. 4.5 Adjunction formula
    6. 4.6 Parshin’s adelic groups
  5. 5 Algebraic cycles of codimension two
    1. 5.1 Bloch-Quillen formula
    2. 5.2 Divisors
    3. 5.3 The Gersten gerbe of a codimension two cycle
    4. 5.4 Gerbes and cohomology with support
    5. 5.5 Geometric interpretation of some results of Bloch
    6. 5.6 The two gerbes associated with an intersection of divisors

Des buissons lumineux fusaient comme des gerbes;
Mille insectes, tels des prismes, vibraient dans l’air;
–Emile Verhaeren (1855-1916),

Le paradis (Les rythmes souverains)

1. Introduction

This aim of this paper is to define higher categorical invariants (gerbes) of codimension two algebraic cycles and provide a categorical interpretation of the intersection of divisors on a smooth proper algebraic variety. This generalization of the classical relation between divisors and line bundles furnishes a new perspective on the classical Bloch-Quillen formula relating Chow groups and algebraic K-theory.

Our work is motivated by the following three basic questions.

  1. (i)

    Let A𝐴A𝐴 and B𝐵B𝐵 be sheaves of abelian groups on a manifold (or algebraic variety) X𝑋X𝑋. Given α∈H1⁢(X,A)𝛼superscript𝐻1𝑋𝐴\alpha\in H^{1}(X,A)𝛼∈𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐴) and β∈H1⁢(X,B)𝛽superscript𝐻1𝑋𝐵\beta\in H^{1}(X,B)𝛽∈𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐵), one has their cup-product α∪β∈H2⁢(X,A⊗B)𝛼𝛽superscript𝐻2𝑋tensor-product𝐴𝐵\alpha\cup\beta\in H^{2}(X,A\otimes B)𝛼∪𝛽∈𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝐴⊗𝐵). We recall that H1superscript𝐻1H^{1}𝐻¹ and H2superscript𝐻2H^{2}𝐻² classify equivalence classes of torsors and gerbes111For us, the term ”gerbe” signifies a stack in groupoids which is locally non-empty and locally connected (§2.1). It is slightly different from the ancient gerbes of Acids, alkalies and salts: their manufacture and applications, Volume 2 (1865) by Thomas Richardson and Henry Watts, pp. 567-569: §4. Gerbes: This firework is made in various ways, generally throwing up a luminous and sparkling jet of fire, somewhat resembling a water-spout: hence its name. Gerbes consist of a straight, cylindrical case, sometime made with wrought iron (if the gerbe is of large dimensions). … Mr. Darby has invented an entirely novel and beautiful gerbe, called the Italian gerbe…” :

    H1⁢(X,A)superscript𝐻1𝑋𝐴\displaystyle H^{1}(X,A)\quad𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐴) ⟷Isomorphism classes of A-torsors⟷Isomorphism classes of A-torsors\displaystyle\longleftrightarrow\quad\text{Isomorphism classes of $A$-torsors}⟷"Isomorphism classes of A-torsors"
    H2⁢(X,A)superscript𝐻2𝑋𝐴\displaystyle H^{2}(X,A)\quad𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝐴) ⟷Isomorphism classes of A-gerbes;⟷Isomorphism classes of A-gerbes\displaystyle\longleftrightarrow\quad\text{Isomorphism classes of $A$-gerbes};⟷"Isomorphism classes of A-gerbes";

    we may pick torsors P𝑃P𝑃 and Q𝑄Q𝑄 representing α𝛼\alpha𝛼 and β𝛽\beta𝛽 and ask

    Question 1.1.

    Given P𝑃PP and Q𝑄QQ, is there a natural construction of a gerbe GP,Qsubscript𝐺𝑃𝑄G_{P,Q}G_{P,Q} which manifests the cohomology class α∪β=[P]∪[Q]𝛼𝛽delimited-[]𝑃delimited-[]𝑄\alpha\cup\beta=[P]\cup[Q]α∪β=[P]∪[Q]?

    The above question admits the following algebraic-geometric analogue.

  2. (ii)

    Let X𝑋X𝑋 be a smooth proper222Actually, our results which depend on the Gersten resolution (5.1.1) are valid for any separated smooth scheme of finite type over a field. variety over a field F𝐹F𝐹. Let Zi⁢(X)superscript𝑍𝑖𝑋Z^{i}(X)𝑍^𝑖⁢(𝑋) be the abelian group of algebraic cycles of codimension i𝑖i𝑖 on X𝑋X𝑋 and let C⁢Hi⁢(X)𝐶superscript𝐻𝑖𝑋CH^{i}(X)𝐶⁢𝐻^𝑖⁢(𝑋) be the Chow group of algebraic cycles of codimension i𝑖i𝑖 modulo rational equivalence. The isomorphism

    C⁢H1⁢(X)⁢⟶∼⁢H1⁢(X,𝒪∗)𝐶superscript𝐻1𝑋similar-to⟶superscript𝐻1𝑋superscript𝒪CH^{1}(X)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}H^{1}(X,\mathcal{O}^{*})𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝑋)⁢⟶┬∼⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝒪^∗)

    connects (Weil) divisors and invertible sheaves (or 𝔾msubscript𝔾𝑚\mathbb{G}_{m}𝔾_𝑚-torsors). While divisors form a group, 𝔾msubscript𝔾𝑚\mathbb{G}_{m}𝔾_𝑚-torsors on X𝑋X𝑋 form a Picard category TorsX⁡(𝔾m)subscriptTors𝑋subscript𝔾𝑚\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}_{X}(\mathbb{G}_{m})"Tors"_𝑋(𝔾_𝑚) with the monoidal structure provided by the Baer sum of torsors. Any divisor D𝐷D𝐷 determines a 𝔾msubscript𝔾𝑚\mathbb{G}_{m}𝔾_𝑚-torsor LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷 -see §5.2; the torsor LD+D′subscript𝐿𝐷superscript𝐷′L_{D+D^{\prime}}𝐿_{𝐷+𝐷'} is isomorphic to the Baer sum of LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷 and LD′subscript𝐿superscript𝐷′L_{D^{\prime}}𝐿_𝐷'. In other words, one has an additive map [23, II, Proposition 6.13]

    (1.0.1) Z1⁢(X)→TorsX⁡(𝔾m)D↦LD.formulae-sequence→superscript𝑍1𝑋subscriptTors𝑋subscript𝔾𝑚maps-to𝐷subscript𝐿𝐷Z^{1}(X)\to\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}_{X}(\mathbb{G}_{m})\qquad D\mapsto L_{% D}.𝑍¹⁢(𝑋)→"Tors"_𝑋(𝔾_𝑚)𝐷↦𝐿_𝐷.
    Question 1.2.

    What is a natural generalization of (1.0.1) to higher codimension cycles?

    Since TorsX⁡(𝔾m)subscriptTors𝑋subscript𝔾𝑚\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}_{X}(\mathbb{G}_{m})"Tors"_𝑋(𝔾_𝑚) is a Picard category, one could expect the putative additive maps on Zi⁢(X)superscript𝑍𝑖𝑋Z^{i}(X)𝑍^𝑖⁢(𝑋) to land in Picard categories or their generalizations.

    Question 1.3.

    Is there a categorification of the intersection pairing

    (1.0.2) C⁢H1⁢(X)×C⁢H1⁢(X)→C⁢H2⁢(X)⁢?→𝐶superscript𝐻1𝑋𝐶superscript𝐻1𝑋𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋?CH^{1}(X)\times CH^{1}(X)\to CH^{2}(X)?𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝑋)×𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝑋)→𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋)⁢?

    More generally, one can ask for a categorical interpretation of the entire Chow ring of X𝑋X𝑋.

Main results

Our first result is an affirmative answer to Question 1.1; the key observation is that a certain Heisenberg group animates the cup-product.

Theorem 1.4.

Let A,B𝐴𝐵A,BA,B be sheaves of abelian groups on a topological space or scheme X𝑋XX.

  1. (i)

    There is a canonical functorial Heisenberg333For A=B=ℤ𝐴𝐵ℤA=B=\mathbb{Z}𝐴=𝐵=ℤ we obtain the usual Heisenberg group over ℤℤ\mathbb{Z}ℤ. sheaf HA,Bsubscript𝐻𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵} on X𝑋X𝑋 which sits in an exact sequence

    0→A⊗B→HA,B→A×B→0;→0tensor-product𝐴𝐵→subscript𝐻𝐴𝐵→𝐴𝐵→00\to A\otimes B\to H_{A,B}\to A\times B\to 0;0→𝐴⊗𝐵→𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵}→𝐴×𝐵→0;

    the sheaf HA,Bsubscript𝐻𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵} (of non-abelian groups) is a central extension of A×B𝐴𝐵A\times B𝐴×𝐵 by A⊗Btensor-product𝐴𝐵A\otimes B𝐴⊗𝐵.

  2. (ii)

    The associated boundary map

    ∂:H1⁢(X,A)×H1⁢(X,B)=H1⁢(X,A×B)→H2⁢(X,A⊗B):superscript𝐻1𝑋𝐴superscript𝐻1𝑋𝐵superscript𝐻1𝑋𝐴𝐵→superscript𝐻2𝑋tensor-product𝐴𝐵\partial:H^{1}(X,A)\times H^{1}(X,B)=H^{1}(X,A\times B)\to H^{2}(X,A\otimes B)∂:{𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐴)×𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐵)=𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐴×𝐵)→𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝐴⊗𝐵)}

    sends the class (γ,δ)𝛾𝛿(\gamma,\delta)(𝛾,𝛿) to the cup-product γ∪δ𝛾𝛿\gamma\cup\delta𝛾∪𝛿.

  3. (iii)

    Given torsors P𝑃P𝑃 and Q𝑄Q𝑄 for A𝐴A𝐴 and B𝐵B𝐵, view P×Q𝑃𝑄P\times Q𝑃×𝑄 as a A×B𝐴𝐵A\times B𝐴×𝐵-torsor on X𝑋X𝑋. Let 𝒢P,Qsubscript𝒢𝑃𝑄\mathcal{G}_{P,Q}𝒢_{𝑃,𝑄} be the gerbe of local liftings (see §2.2) of P×Q𝑃𝑄P\times Q𝑃×𝑄 to a HA,Bsubscript𝐻𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵}-torsor; its band is A⊗Btensor-product𝐴𝐵A\otimes B𝐴⊗𝐵 and its class in H2⁢(X,A⊗B)superscript𝐻2𝑋tensor-product𝐴𝐵H^{2}(X,A\otimes B)𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝐴⊗𝐵) is [P]∪[Q]delimited-[]𝑃delimited-[]𝑄[P]\cup[Q][𝑃]∪[𝑄].

  4. (iv)

    The gerbe 𝒢P,Qsubscript𝒢𝑃𝑄\mathcal{G}_{P,Q}𝒢_{𝑃,𝑄} is covariant functorial in A𝐴A𝐴 and B𝐵B𝐵 and contravariant functorial in X𝑋X𝑋.

  5. (v)

    The gerbe 𝒢P,Qsubscript𝒢𝑃𝑄\mathcal{G}_{P,Q}𝒢_{𝑃,𝑄} is trivial (equivalent to the stack of A⊗Btensor-product𝐴𝐵A\otimes B𝐴⊗𝐵-torsors) if either P𝑃P𝑃 or Q𝑄Q𝑄 is trivial.

We prove this theorem over a general site 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢. We also provide a natural interpretation of the (class of the) Heisenberg sheaf in terms of maps of Eilenberg-Mac Lane objects in §3.4.

Here is another rephrasing of Theorem 1.4: For abelian sheaves A𝐴A𝐴 and B𝐵B𝐵 on a site 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢, there is a natural bimonoidal functor

(1.0.3) Tors𝖢⁡(A)×Tors𝖢⁡(B)⟶Gerbes𝖢⁡(A⊗B)(P,Q)↦𝒢P,Qformulae-sequence⟶subscriptTors𝖢𝐴subscriptTors𝖢𝐵subscriptGerbes𝖢tensor-product𝐴𝐵maps-to𝑃𝑄subscript𝒢𝑃𝑄\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}_{\mathsf{C}}(A)\times\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}% _{\mathsf{C}}(B)\longrightarrow\operatorname{\textsc{Gerbes}}_{\mathsf{C}}(A% \otimes B)\qquad(P,Q)\mapsto\mathcal{G}_{P,Q}"Tors"_𝖢(𝐴)×"Tors"_𝖢(𝐵)⟶"Gerbes"_𝖢(𝐴⊗𝐵)(𝑃,𝑄)↦𝒢_{𝑃,𝑄}

where Tors𝖢⁡(A)subscriptTors𝖢𝐴\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}_{\mathsf{C}}(A)"Tors"_𝖢(𝐴), Tors𝖢⁡(B)subscriptTors𝖢𝐵\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}_{\mathsf{C}}(B)"Tors"_𝖢(𝐵) are the Picard categories of A𝐴A𝐴 and B𝐵B𝐵-torsors on 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢 and Gerbes𝖢⁡(A⊗B)subscriptGerbes𝖢tensor-product𝐴𝐵\operatorname{\textsc{Gerbes}}_{\mathsf{C}}(A\otimes B)"Gerbes"_𝖢(𝐴⊗𝐵) is the Picard 2222-category of A⊗Btensor-product𝐴𝐵A\otimes B𝐴⊗𝐵-gerbes on 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢. Thus, Theorem 1.4 constitutes a categorification of the cup-product map

(1.0.4) ∪:H1(A)×H1(B)→H2(A⊗B).\cup:H^{1}(A)\times H^{1}(B)\to H^{2}(A\otimes B).∪:𝐻¹(𝐴)×𝐻¹(𝐵)→𝐻²(𝐴⊗𝐵).

Let us turn to Questions 1.2 and 1.3. Let 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢 be the Zariski site on the smooth proper variety X𝑋X𝑋.

Suppose that D𝐷D𝐷 and D′superscript𝐷′D^{\prime}𝐷' are divisors on X𝑋X𝑋 which intersect in the codimension-two cycle D.D′formulae-sequence𝐷superscript𝐷′D.D^{\prime}𝐷.𝐷'. Applying Theorem 1.4 to 𝒪Dsubscript𝒪𝐷\mathcal{O}_{D}𝒪_𝐷 and 𝒪D′subscript𝒪superscript𝐷′\mathcal{O}_{D^{\prime}}𝒪_𝐷' with A=B=𝔾m𝐴𝐵subscript𝔾𝑚A=B=\mathbb{G}_{m}𝐴=𝐵=𝔾_𝑚, one has a 𝔾m⊗𝔾mtensor-productsubscript𝔾𝑚subscript𝔾𝑚\mathbb{G}_{m}\otimes\mathbb{G}_{m}𝔾_𝑚⊗𝔾_𝑚-gerbe 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} on X𝑋X𝑋. We now invoke the isomorphisms (the second is the fundamental Bloch-Quillen isomorphism)

𝔾m⁢⟶∼⁢𝒦1,C⁢Hi⁢(X)⁢⟶(⁢5.1.3⁢)∼⁢Hi⁢(X,𝒦i)subscript𝔾𝑚similar-to⟶subscript𝒦1𝐶superscript𝐻𝑖𝑋italic-(5.1.3italic-)similar-to⟶superscript𝐻𝑖𝑋subscript𝒦𝑖\mathbb{G}_{m}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathcal{K}_{1},\qquad CH^{i}(X)% \underset{\eqref{BQ}}{\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}}H^{i}(X,\mathcal{K}_{i})𝔾_𝑚⁢⟶┬∼⁢𝒦₁,𝐶⁢𝐻^𝑖⁢(𝑋)⁢(⟶┬∼)┬⁢(5.1.3)⁢𝐻^𝑖⁢(𝑋,𝒦_𝑖)

where 𝒦isubscript𝒦𝑖\mathcal{K}_{i}𝒦_𝑖 is the Zariski sheaf associated with the presheaf U↦Ki⁢(U)maps-to𝑈subscript𝐾𝑖𝑈U\mapsto K_{i}(U)𝑈↦𝐾_𝑖⁢(𝑈).

Pushforward of 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} along 𝒦1×𝒦1→𝒦2→subscript𝒦1subscript𝒦1subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{1}\times\mathcal{K}_{1}\to\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₁×𝒦₁→𝒦₂ gives a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe still denoted 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'}; we call this the Heisenberg gerbe attached to the codimension-two cycle D.D′formulae-sequence𝐷superscript𝐷′D.D^{\prime}𝐷.𝐷'. This raises the possibility of relating 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbes and codimension-two cycles on X𝑋X𝑋, implicit in (5.1.3). Since a general codimension-two cycle need not be an intersection of divisiors (or even rationally equivalent to an intersection of divisors), a new idea is necessary to generalize the above construction of the Heisenberg gerbe to all codimension-two cycles. Our approach proceeds via the Gersten sequence (5.1.1).

Theorem 1.5.

(i) Any codimension-two cycle α∈Z2⁢(X)𝛼superscript𝑍2𝑋\alpha\in Z^{2}(X)α∈Z²⁢(X) determines a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_α on X𝑋XX.

(ii) the class of 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_α in H2⁢(X,𝒦2)superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})H²⁢(X,𝒦₂) corresponds to α∈C⁢H2⁢(X)𝛼𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋\alpha\in CH^{2}(X)α∈C⁢H²⁢(X) under the Bloch-Quillen map (5.1.3).

(iii) the gerbe 𝒞α+α′subscript𝒞𝛼superscript𝛼′\mathcal{C}_{\alpha+\alpha^{\prime}}𝒞_{α+α'} is equivalent to the Baer sum of 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_α and 𝒞α′subscript𝒞superscript𝛼′\mathcal{C}_{\alpha^{\prime}}𝒞_α'.

(iv) 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_α and 𝒞α′subscript𝒞superscript𝛼′\mathcal{C}_{\alpha^{\prime}}𝒞_α' are equivalent as 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbes if and only if α=α′𝛼superscript𝛼′\alpha=\alpha^{\prime}α=α' in C⁢H2⁢(X)𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋CH^{2}(X)C⁢H²⁢(X).

Since 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 uses the Gersten sequence (5.1.1) in a crucial way, we call 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 the Gersten gerbe of α𝛼\alpha𝛼; it admits a geometric description, closely analogous to that of the 𝔾msubscript𝔾𝑚\mathbb{G}_{m}𝔾_𝑚-torsor LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷 of a divisor D𝐷D𝐷 of §5.2; see Remark 5.6. One has an additive map

(1.0.5) Z2⁢(X)→GerbesX⁡(𝒦2)α↦𝒞α.formulae-sequence→superscript𝑍2𝑋subscriptGerbes𝑋subscript𝒦2maps-to𝛼subscript𝒞𝛼Z^{2}(X)\to\operatorname{\textsc{Gerbes}}_{X}(\mathcal{K}_{2})\qquad\alpha% \mapsto\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}.𝑍²⁢(𝑋)→"Gerbes"_𝑋(𝒦₂)𝛼↦𝒞_𝛼.

When α=D.D′formulae-sequence𝛼𝐷superscript𝐷′\alpha=D.D^{\prime}𝛼=𝐷.𝐷' is the intersection of two divisors, there are two 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbes attached to it: the Heisenberg gerbe 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} and the Gersten gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼; these are abstractly equivalent as their classes in H2⁢(X,𝒦2)superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂) correspond to α𝛼\alpha𝛼. More is possible.

Theorem 1.6.

If α∈Z2⁢(X)𝛼superscript𝑍2𝑋\alpha\in Z^{2}(X)α∈Z²⁢(X) is the intersection D.D′formulae-sequence𝐷superscript𝐷′D.D^{\prime}D.D' of divisors D,D′∈Z1⁢(X)𝐷superscript𝐷′superscript𝑍1𝑋D,D^{\prime}\in Z^{1}(X){D,D'}∈Z¹⁢(X), then there is a natural equivalence Θ:𝒞α→𝒢D,D′:Θ→subscript𝒞𝛼subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\Theta:\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}\to\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}Θ:𝒞_α→𝒢_{D,D'} between the Gersten and Heisenberg 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbes attached to α=D.D′formulae-sequence𝛼𝐷superscript𝐷′\alpha=D.D^{\prime}α=D.D'.

Thus, Theorems 1.4, 1.5, 1.6 together provide the following commutative diagram thereby answering Question 1.3:

Since the Heisenberg gerbe 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} arises from a gerbe with band 𝔾m⊗𝔾mtensor-productsubscript𝔾𝑚subscript𝔾𝑚\mathbb{G}_{m}\otimes\mathbb{G}_{m}𝔾_𝑚⊗𝔾_𝑚, we obtain:

Proposition.

A necessary condition for a codimension-two cycle α𝛼\alphaα on X𝑋XX to be an intersection α=D.D′formulae-sequence𝛼𝐷superscript𝐷′\alpha=D.D^{\prime}α=D.D' of divisors D,D′𝐷superscript𝐷′D,D^{\prime}D,D' is that the Gersten gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_α with band 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂ lifts to a 𝔾m⊗𝔾mtensor-productsubscript𝔾𝑚subscript𝔾𝑚\mathbb{G}_{m}\otimes\mathbb{G}_{m}𝔾ₘ⊗𝔾ₘ-gerbe.

We begin with a review of the basic notions and tools (lifting gerbe, four-term complexes) in §2 and then present the construction and properties of the Heisenberg group in §3 before proving Theorem 1.4. After a quick discussion of various examples in §4, we turn to codimension-two algebraic cycles in §5 and construct the Gersten gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 and prove Theorems 1.5, 1.6 using the tools in §2.

Dictionary for codimension two cycles

The above results indicate the viability of viewing 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbes as natural invariants of codimension-two cycles on X𝑋X𝑋. Additional evidence is given by the following points: 444Let η:Spec⁢FX→X:𝜂→Specsubscript𝐹𝑋𝑋\eta\colon\textrm{Spec}~{}F_{X}\to X𝜂:"Spec"⁢𝐹_𝑋→𝑋 be the generic point of X𝑋X𝑋 and write Kiηsuperscriptsubscript𝐾𝑖𝜂K_{i}^{\eta}𝐾_𝑖^𝜂 for the sheaf η∗⁢Ki⁢(FX)subscript𝜂subscript𝐾𝑖subscript𝐹𝑋\eta_{*}K_{i}(F_{X})𝜂_∗⁢𝐾_𝑖⁢(𝐹_𝑋); one has the map 𝒦i→Kiη→subscript𝒦𝑖superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑖𝜂\mathcal{K}_{i}\to K_{i}^{\eta}𝒦_𝑖→𝐾_𝑖^𝜂.

  • •

    𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbes are present (albeit implicitly) in the Bloch-Quillen formula (5.1.3) for i=2𝑖2i=2𝑖=2.

  • •

    The Picard category 𝔓=TorsX⁡(𝔾m)𝔓subscriptTors𝑋subscript𝔾𝑚\mathfrak{P}=\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}_{X}(\mathbb{G}_{m})𝔓="Tors"_𝑋(𝔾_𝑚) of 𝔾msubscript𝔾𝑚\mathbb{G}_{m}𝔾_𝑚-torsors on X𝑋X𝑋 satisfies

    π1⁢(𝔓)=H0⁢(X,𝒪∗)=C⁢H1⁢(X,1),π0⁢(𝔓)=H1⁢(X,𝒪∗)=C⁢H1⁢(X).formulae-sequencesubscript𝜋1𝔓superscript𝐻0𝑋superscript𝒪𝐶superscript𝐻1𝑋1subscript𝜋0𝔓superscript𝐻1𝑋superscript𝒪𝐶superscript𝐻1𝑋\pi_{1}(\mathfrak{P})=H^{0}(X,\mathcal{O}^{*})=CH^{1}(X,1),\qquad\pi_{0}(% \mathfrak{P})=H^{1}(X,\mathcal{O}^{*})=CH^{1}(X).𝜋₁⁢(𝔓)=𝐻⁰⁢(𝑋,𝒪^∗)=𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,1),𝜋₀⁢(𝔓)=𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝒪^∗)=𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝑋).

    Similarly, the Picard 2222-category ℭ=GerbesX⁡(𝒦2)ℭsubscriptGerbes𝑋subscript𝒦2\mathfrak{C}=\operatorname{\textsc{Gerbes}}_{X}(\mathcal{K}_{2})ℭ="Gerbes"_𝑋(𝒦₂) of 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbes is closely related to the higher Chow groups C⁢H2⁢(X,−)𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋CH^{2}(X,-)𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋,-) of Bloch [4] in codimension two, generalizing the Chow groups C⁢Hj⁢(X,0)=C⁢Hj⁢(X)𝐶superscript𝐻𝑗𝑋0𝐶superscript𝐻𝑗𝑋CH^{j}(X,0)=CH^{j}(X)𝐶⁢𝐻^𝑗⁢(𝑋,0)=𝐶⁢𝐻^𝑗⁢(𝑋):

    π2⁢(ℭ)=H0⁢(X,𝒦2)=C⁢H2⁢(X,2),π1⁢(ℭ)=H1⁢(X,𝒦2)=C⁢H2⁢(X,1),π0⁢(ℭ)=H2⁢(X,𝒦2)⁢=(⁢5.1.3⁢)⁢C⁢H2⁢(X).formulae-sequencesubscript𝜋2ℭsuperscript𝐻0𝑋subscript𝒦2𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋2subscript𝜋1ℭsuperscript𝐻1𝑋subscript𝒦2𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋1subscript𝜋0ℭsuperscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2italic-(5.1.3italic-)𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋\pi_{2}(\mathfrak{C})=H^{0}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})=CH^{2}(X,2),\quad\pi_{1}(% \mathfrak{C})=H^{1}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})=CH^{2}(X,1),\quad\pi_{0}(\mathfrak{C})=% H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})\overset{\eqref{BQ}}{=}CH^{2}(X).𝜋₂⁢(ℭ)=𝐻⁰⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂)=𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋,2),𝜋₁⁢(ℭ)=𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂)=𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋,1),𝜋₀⁢(ℭ)=𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂)⁢=┬⁢(5.1.3)⁢𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋).
  • •

    The additive map arising from Theorem 1.5

    Z2⁢(X)→GerbesX⁡(𝒦2),α↦𝒞αformulae-sequence→superscript𝑍2𝑋subscriptGerbes𝑋subscript𝒦2maps-to𝛼subscript𝒞𝛼Z^{2}(X)\to\operatorname{\textsc{Gerbes}}_{X}(\mathcal{K}_{2}),\qquad\alpha% \mapsto\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝑍²⁢(𝑋)→"Gerbes"_𝑋(𝒦₂),𝛼↦𝒞_𝛼

    gives the Bloch-Quillen isomorphism (5.1.3) on the level of π0subscript𝜋0\pi_{0}𝜋₀. It provides an answer to Question 1.2 for codimension two cycles.

  • •

    The Gersten gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 admits a simple algebro-geometric description (Remark 5.5): Any α𝛼\alpha𝛼 determines a K2η/𝒦2superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂subscript𝒦2K_{2}^{\eta}/{\mathcal{K}_{2}}𝐾₂^𝜂/𝒦₂-torsor; then 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 is the gerbe of liftings of this torsor to a K2ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂K_{2}^{\eta}𝐾₂^𝜂-torsor on X𝑋X𝑋.

  • •

    The gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 is canonically trivial outside of the support of α𝛼\alpha𝛼 (Remark 5.5).

  • •

    Pushing the Gersten gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 along the map 𝒦2→Ω2→subscript𝒦2superscriptΩ2\mathcal{K}_{2}\to\Omega^{2}𝒦₂→Ω² produces an Ω2superscriptΩ2\Omega^{2}Ω²-gerbe which manifests the (de Rham) cycle class of α𝛼\alpha𝛼 in H2⁢(X,Ω2)superscript𝐻2𝑋superscriptΩ2H^{2}(X,\Omega^{2})𝐻²⁢(𝑋,Ω²).

The map (1.0.1) is a part of the marvellous dictionary [23, II, §6] arising from the divisor sequence (5.2.1):

(1.0.6) Divisors⟷Cartier divisors⟷𝒦1-torsors⟷Line bundles⟷Invertible sheaves.⟷DivisorsCartier divisors⟷𝒦1-torsors⟷Line bundles⟷Invertible sheaves\text{Divisors}\longleftrightarrow\text{Cartier~{}divisors}\longleftrightarrow% \text{$\mathcal{K}_{1}$-torsors}\longleftrightarrow\text{Line~{}bundles}% \longleftrightarrow\text{Invertible~{}sheaves}."Divisors"⟷"Cartier divisors"⟷"K1-torsors"⟷"Line bundles"⟷"Invertible sheaves".

More generally, from the Gersten sequence (5.1.1) we obtain the following:

Z1⁢(X)⁢⟶≅⁢H0⁢(X,K1η/𝒦1)(=Cartier⁢divisors)↠H1⁢(X,𝒦1)≅C⁢H1⁢(X)↠annotatedsuperscript𝑍1𝑋⟶superscript𝐻0𝑋superscriptsubscript𝐾1𝜂subscript𝒦1absentCartierdivisorssuperscript𝐻1𝑋subscript𝒦1𝐶superscript𝐻1𝑋\displaystyle Z^{1}(X)\overset{\cong}{\longrightarrow}H^{0}(X,K_{1}^{\eta}/% \mathcal{K}_{1})(=~{}\mathrm{Cartier~{}divisors})\twoheadrightarrow H^{1}(X,% \mathcal{K}_{1})\cong CH^{1}(X)annotated{𝑍¹⁢(𝑋)⁢⟶┬≅⁢𝐻⁰⁢(𝑋,𝐾₁^𝜂/𝒦₁)}(=Cartier⁢divisors)↠𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝒦₁)≅𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝑋)
Z2⁢(X)↠H1⁢(X,K2η/𝒦2)⁢⟶≅⁢H2⁢(X,𝒦2)≅C⁢H2⁢(X).↠superscript𝑍2𝑋superscript𝐻1𝑋superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂subscript𝒦2⟶superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋\displaystyle Z^{2}(X)\twoheadrightarrow H^{1}(X,K_{2}^{\eta}/\mathcal{K}_{2})% \overset{\cong}{\longrightarrow}H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})\cong CH^{2}(X).𝑍²⁢(𝑋)↠𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐾₂^𝜂/𝒦₂)⁢⟶┬≅⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂)≅𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋).

Inspired by this and [3, Definition 3.2], we call K2η/𝒦2superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂subscript𝒦2K_{2}^{\eta}/\mathcal{K}_{2}𝐾₂^𝜂/𝒦₂-torsors as codimension-two Cartier cycles on X𝑋X𝑋. Thus the analog for codimension two cycles of the dictionary (1.0.6) reads

Codimension two cycles⟷Cartier cycles⟷𝒦2-gerbes.Codimension two cycles⟷Cartier cycles⟷𝒦2-gerbes\text{Codimension two cycles}\quad\longleftrightarrow\quad\text{Cartier~{}% cycles}\quad\longleftrightarrow\quad\text{$\mathcal{K}_{2}$-gerbes}."Codimension two cycles"⟷"Cartier cycles"⟷"K2-gerbes".

Since the Gersten sequence (5.1.1) exists for all 𝒦isubscript𝒦𝑖\mathcal{K}_{i}𝒦_𝑖, it should be possible to generalize Theorem 1.5 to higher codimensions thereby answering Question 1.2; however, this would involve higher gerbes. Any cycle of codimension i>2𝑖2i>2𝑖>2 determines a higher gerbe [7] with band 𝒦isubscript𝒦𝑖\mathcal{K}_{i}𝒦_𝑖 (an example in the context of Parshin chains is presented below); this provides a new perspective on the Bloch-Quillen formula (5.1.3). The higher dimensional analogues of (1.0.3), (1.0.2), and Theorem 1.5 are still in progress.

Higher gerbes attached to smooth Parshin chains

By Gersten’s conjecture, the localization sequence [32, §7 Proposition 3.2] breaks up into short exact sequences

0⟶Ki⁢(V)⟶Ki⁢(V−Y)⟶Ki−1⁢(Y)⟶0,(i>0)formulae-sequence⟶0subscript𝐾𝑖𝑉⟶subscript𝐾𝑖𝑉𝑌⟶subscript𝐾𝑖1𝑌⟶0𝑖00\longrightarrow K_{i}(V)\longrightarrow K_{i}(V-Y)\longrightarrow K_{i-1}(Y)% \longrightarrow 0,\qquad(i>0)0⟶𝐾_𝑖⁢(𝑉)⟶𝐾_𝑖⁢(𝑉-𝑌)⟶𝐾_{𝑖-1}⁢(𝑌)⟶0,(𝑖>0)

for any smooth variety V𝑉V𝑉 over F𝐹F𝐹 and a closed smooth subvariety Y𝑌Y𝑌 of V𝑉V𝑉. Let j:D→X:𝑗→𝐷𝑋j:D\to X𝑗:𝐷→𝑋 be a smooth closed subvariety of codimension one of X𝑋X𝑋; write ι:X−D→X:𝜄→𝑋𝐷𝑋\iota:X-D\to X𝜄:𝑋-𝐷→𝑋 for the open complement of D𝐷D𝐷. Any divisor α𝛼\alpha𝛼 of D𝐷D𝐷 is a codimension-two cycle on X𝑋X𝑋; one has a map Pic⁢(D)→C⁢H2⁢(X)→Pic𝐷𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋{\rm Pic}(D)\to CH^{2}(X)Pic⁢(𝐷)→𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋) [4, (iii), p. 269]. This gives the exact sequence (for i>0𝑖0i>0𝑖>0)

0⟶𝒦i⟶ℱi⟶j∗⁢𝒦i−1D⟶0⟶0subscript𝒦𝑖⟶subscriptℱ𝑖⟶subscript𝑗subscriptsuperscript𝒦𝐷𝑖1⟶00\longrightarrow\mathcal{K}_{i}\longrightarrow\mathcal{F}_{i}\longrightarrow j% _{*}\mathcal{K}^{D}_{i-1}\longrightarrow 00⟶𝒦_𝑖⟶ℱ_𝑖⟶𝑗_∗⁢𝒦^𝐷_{𝑖-1}⟶0

of sheaves on X𝑋X𝑋 where ℱi=ι∗⁢𝒦iUsubscriptℱ𝑖subscript𝜄subscriptsuperscript𝒦𝑈𝑖\mathcal{F}_{i}=\iota_{*}\mathcal{K}^{U}_{i}ℱ_𝑖=𝜄_∗⁢𝒦^𝑈_𝑖 is the sheaf associated with the presheaf U↦Ki⁢(U−D)maps-to𝑈subscript𝐾𝑖𝑈𝐷U\mapsto K_{i}(U-D)𝑈↦𝐾_𝑖⁢(𝑈-𝐷). We write 𝒦iDsuperscriptsubscript𝒦𝑖𝐷\mathcal{K}_{i}^{D}𝒦_𝑖^𝐷 and 𝒦iUsuperscriptsubscript𝒦𝑖𝑈\mathcal{K}_{i}^{U}𝒦_𝑖^𝑈 for the usual K-theory sheaves on D𝐷D𝐷 and U𝑈U𝑈 since the notation 𝒦isubscript𝒦𝑖\mathcal{K}_{i}𝒦_𝑖 is already reserved for the sheaf on X𝑋X𝑋. The boundary map

H1⁢(D,𝒦1D)=H1⁢(X,j∗⁢𝒦1D)⟶H2⁢(X,𝒦2)superscript𝐻1𝐷subscriptsuperscript𝒦𝐷1superscript𝐻1𝑋subscript𝑗subscriptsuperscript𝒦𝐷1⟶superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2H^{1}(D,\mathcal{K}^{D}_{1})=H^{1}(X,j_{*}\mathcal{K}^{D}_{1})\longrightarrow H% ^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝐻¹⁢(𝐷,𝒦^𝐷₁)=𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝑗_∗⁢𝒦^𝐷₁)⟶𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂)

is the map C⁢H1⁢(D)→C⁢H2⁢(X)→𝐶superscript𝐻1𝐷𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋CH^{1}(D)\to CH^{2}(X)𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝐷)→𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋). For any divisor α𝛼\alpha𝛼 of D𝐷D𝐷, the 𝒦1Dsubscriptsuperscript𝒦𝐷1\mathcal{K}^{D}_{1}𝒦^𝐷₁-torsor 𝒪αsubscript𝒪𝛼\mathcal{O}_{\alpha}𝒪_𝛼 determines a unique j∗⁢𝒦1Dsubscript𝑗subscriptsuperscript𝒦𝐷1j_{*}\mathcal{K}^{D}_{1}𝑗_∗⁢𝒦^𝐷₁-torsor Lαsubscript𝐿𝛼L_{\alpha}𝐿_𝛼 on X𝑋X𝑋. The 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 (viewing α𝛼\alpha𝛼 as a codimension two cycle on X𝑋X𝑋) is the lifting gerbe of the j∗⁢𝒦1Dsubscript𝑗subscriptsuperscript𝒦𝐷1j_{*}\mathcal{K}^{D}_{1}𝑗_∗⁢𝒦^𝐷₁-torsor Lαsubscript𝐿𝛼L_{\alpha}𝐿_𝛼 (obstructions to lifting to a ℱ2subscriptℱ2\mathcal{F}_{2}ℱ₂-torsor).

This generalizes to higher codimensions (and pursued in forthcoming work):

  • •

    (codimension three) If β𝛽\beta𝛽 is a codimension-two cycle of D𝐷D𝐷, then the gerbe 𝒞βsubscript𝒞𝛽\mathcal{C}_{\beta}𝒞_𝛽 on D𝐷D𝐷 determines a unique gerbe Lβsubscript𝐿𝛽L_{\beta}𝐿_𝛽 on X𝑋X𝑋 (with band j∗⁢𝒦2Dsubscript𝑗subscriptsuperscript𝒦𝐷2j_{*}\mathcal{K}^{D}_{2}𝑗_∗⁢𝒦^𝐷₂). The obstructions to lifting Lβsubscript𝐿𝛽L_{\beta}𝐿_𝛽 to a ℱ3subscriptℱ3\mathcal{F}_{3}ℱ₃-gerbe is a 2222-gerbe 𝒢βsubscript𝒢𝛽\mathcal{G}_{\beta}𝒢_𝛽 [7] with band 𝒦3subscript𝒦3\mathcal{K}_{3}𝒦₃ on X𝑋X𝑋. This gives an example of a higher gerbe invariant of a codimension three cycle on X𝑋X𝑋. Gerbes with band K3η/𝒦3superscriptsubscript𝐾3𝜂subscript𝒦3K_{3}^{\eta}/{\mathcal{K}_{3}}𝐾₃^𝜂/𝒦₃ provide the codimension-three analog of Cartier divisors H0⁢(X,K1η/𝒦1)superscript𝐻0𝑋superscriptsubscript𝐾1𝜂subscript𝒦1H^{0}(X,K_{1}^{\eta}/{\mathcal{K}_{1}})𝐻⁰⁢(𝑋,𝐾₁^𝜂/𝒦₁).

  • •

    (Parshin chains) Recall that a chain of subvarieties

    X0↪X1↪X2↪X3↪⋯↪Xn=X↪subscript𝑋0subscript𝑋1↪subscript𝑋2↪subscript𝑋3↪⋯↪subscript𝑋𝑛𝑋X_{0}\hookrightarrow X_{1}\hookrightarrow X_{2}\hookrightarrow X_{3}% \hookrightarrow\cdots\hookrightarrow X_{n}=X𝑋₀↪𝑋₁↪𝑋₂↪𝑋₃↪⋯↪𝑋_𝑛=𝑋

    where each Xisubscript𝑋𝑖X_{i}𝑋_𝑖 is a divisor of Xi+1subscript𝑋𝑖1X_{i+1}𝑋_{𝑖+1} gives rise to a Parshin chain on X𝑋X𝑋. We will call a Parshin chain smooth if all the subvarieties Xisubscript𝑋𝑖X_{i}𝑋_𝑖 are smooth. Iterating the previous construction provides a higher gerbe on Xn=Xsubscript𝑋𝑛𝑋X_{n}=X𝑋_𝑛=𝑋 with band 𝒦jsubscript𝒦𝑗\mathcal{K}_{j}𝒦_𝑗 attached to Xn−jsubscript𝑋𝑛𝑗X_{n-j}𝑋_{𝑛-𝑗} (a codimension j𝑗j𝑗 cycle of Xnsubscript𝑋𝑛X_{n}𝑋_𝑛).

Other than the classical Hartshorne-Serre correspondence between certain codimension-two cycles and certain rank two vector bundles, we are not aware of any generalizations of the dictionary (1.0.6) to higher codimension. In particular, our idea of attaching a higher-categorical invariant to a higher codimension cycle seems new in the literature. We expect that Picard n𝑛n𝑛-categories play a role in the functorial Riemann-Roch program of Deligne [15].

Our results are related to and inspired by the beautiful work of S. Bloch [3], L. Breen [8], J.-L. Brylinski [10], P. Gajer [18, 19], A. N. Parshin [30], B. Poonen - E. Rains [31], and D. Ramakrishnan [33] (see §4). Brylinski’s hope555“In principle such ideas will lead to a geometric description of all regulator maps, once the categorical aspects have been cleared up. Hopefully this would lead to a better understanding of algebraic K-theory itself.” [10, Introduction] for a higher-categorical geometrical interpretation of the regulator maps from algebraic K-theory to Deligne cohomology was a major catalyst. In a forthcoming paper, we will investigate the relations between the Gersten gerbe and Deligne cohomology.

Acknowledgements.

The second author’s research was supported by the 2015-2016 “Research and Scholarship Award” from the Graduate School, University of Maryland. We would like to thank O. Bräunling, L. Breen, P. Cartier, S. Lichtenbaum, J. Rosenberg, J. Schafer, and H. Tamvakis for very useful comments and suggestions. We are grateful to the referee for her/his comprehensive report with detailed suggestions and comments.

Notations and conventions

Let 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢 be a site. We write 𝖢~superscript𝖢~\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖢^~ for the topos of sheaves over 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢, 𝖢ab~subscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}𝖢^~_ab the abelian group objects of 𝖢~superscript𝖢~\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖢^~, namely the abelian sheaves on 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢, and by 𝖢grp~subscriptsuperscript𝖢~grp\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{grp}}𝖢^~_grp the sheaves of groups on 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢. Our notation for cohomology is as follows. For an abelian object A𝐴A𝐴 of a topos 𝖳𝖳\mathsf{T}𝖳, Hi⁢(A)superscript𝐻𝑖𝐴H^{i}(A)𝐻^𝑖⁢(𝐴) denotes the cohomology of the terminal object e∈𝖳𝑒𝖳e\in\mathsf{T}𝑒∈𝖳 with coefficients in A𝐴A𝐴, namely ithsuperscript𝑖thi^{\mathrm{th}}𝑖ᵗʰ derived functor of Hom𝖳⁡(e,A)subscriptHom𝖳𝑒𝐴\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{T}}(e,A)Hom_𝖳(𝑒,𝐴). This is the same as Ext𝖳abi⁡(ℤ,A)subscriptsuperscriptExt𝑖subscript𝖳abℤ𝐴\operatorname{Ext}^{i}_{\mathsf{T}_{\mathrm{ab}}}(\mathbb{Z},A)Ext^𝑖_𝖳_ab(ℤ,𝐴). More generally, Hi⁢(X,A)superscript𝐻𝑖𝑋𝐴H^{i}(X,A)𝐻^𝑖⁢(𝑋,𝐴) denotes the cohomology of A𝐴A𝐴 in the topos 𝖳/X𝖳𝑋\mathsf{T}/X𝖳/𝑋. We use 𝐇𝐇\mathbf{H}𝐇 for hypercohomology.

A variety over a field F𝐹F𝐹 is an integral scheme of finite type over F𝐹F𝐹.

2. Preliminaries

2.1. Abelian Gerbes [21, 16, 7]

A gerbe 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 over a site 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢 is a stack in groupoids which is locally non-empty and locally connected.

𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 is locally nonempty if for every object U𝑈U𝑈 of 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢 there is a cover, say a local epimorphism, V→U→𝑉𝑈V\to U𝑉→𝑈 such that the category 𝒢⁢(V)𝒢𝑉\mathcal{G}(V)𝒢⁢(𝑉) is nonempty; it is locally connected if given objects x,y∈𝒢⁢(U)𝑥𝑦𝒢𝑈x,y\in\mathcal{G}(U){𝑥,𝑦}∈𝒢⁢(𝑈) as above, then, locally on U𝑈U𝑈, the sheaf Hom⁡(x,y)Hom𝑥𝑦\operatorname{Hom}(x,y)Hom(𝑥,𝑦) defined above has sections. For each object x𝑥x𝑥 over U𝑈U𝑈 we can introduce the automorphism sheaf Aut𝒢⁡(x)subscriptAut𝒢𝑥\operatorname{Aut}_{\mathcal{G}}(x)Aut_𝒢(𝑥), and by local connectedness all these automorphism sheaves are (non canonically) isomorphic.

In the sequel we will only work with abelian gerbes, where there is a coherent identification between the automorphism sheaves Aut𝒢⁡(x)subscriptAut𝒢𝑥\operatorname{Aut}_{\mathcal{G}}(x)Aut_𝒢(𝑥), for any choice of an object x𝑥x𝑥 of 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢, and a fixed sheaf of groups G𝐺G𝐺. In this case G𝐺G𝐺 is necessarily abelian666The automorphisms in Aut⁡(G)Aut𝐺\operatorname{Aut}(G)Aut(𝐺) completely decouple, hence play no role., and the class of 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 determines an element in H2⁢(G)superscript𝐻2𝐺H^{2}(G)𝐻²⁢(𝐺), [7, §2] (and also [26]), where Hi⁢(G)=Ext𝖢ab~i⁡(ℤ,G)superscript𝐻𝑖𝐺subscriptsuperscriptExt𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝖢~abℤ𝐺H^{i}(G)=\operatorname{Ext}^{i}_{\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}}(\mathbb{Z},G)𝐻^𝑖⁢(𝐺)=Ext^𝑖_𝖢^~_ab(ℤ,𝐺) denotes the standard cohomology with coefficients in the abelian sheaf G𝐺G𝐺 in the topos 𝖢~superscript𝖢~\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖢^~ of sheaves over 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢.

Let us briefly recall how the class of 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 is obtained using a Čech type argument. Assume for simplicity that the site 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢 has pullbacks. Let 𝒰={Ui}𝒰subscript𝑈𝑖\mathcal{U}=\{U_{i}\}𝒰={𝑈_𝑖} be a cover of an object X𝑋X𝑋 of 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢. Let xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i}𝑥_𝑖 be a choice of an object of 𝒢⁢(Ui)𝒢subscript𝑈𝑖\mathcal{G}(U_{i})𝒢⁢(𝑈_𝑖). For simplicity, let us assume that we can find morphisms αi⁢j:xj|Ui⁢j→xi|Ui⁢j\alpha_{ij}\colon x_{j}\rvert_{U_{ij}}\to x_{i}\rvert_{U_{ij}}{𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑗}:𝑥_𝑗|}_𝑈_{𝑖⁢𝑗}→𝑥_𝑖|_𝑈_{𝑖⁢𝑗}. The class of 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 will be represented by the 2-cocycle {ci⁢j⁢k}subscript𝑐𝑖𝑗𝑘\{c_{ijk}\}{𝑐_{𝑖⁢𝑗⁢𝑘}} of 𝒰𝒰\mathcal{U}𝒰 with values in G𝐺G𝐺 obtained in the standard way as the deviation for {αi⁢j}subscript𝛼𝑖𝑗\{\alpha_{ij}\}{𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑗}} from satisfying the cocycle condition:

αi⁢j∘αj⁢k=ci⁢j⁢k∘αi⁢k.subscript𝛼𝑖𝑗subscript𝛼𝑗𝑘subscript𝑐𝑖𝑗𝑘subscript𝛼𝑖𝑘\alpha_{ij}\circ\alpha_{jk}=c_{ijk}\circ\alpha_{ik}.𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑗}∘𝛼_{𝑗⁢𝑘}=𝑐_{𝑖⁢𝑗⁢𝑘}∘𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑘}.

In the above identity—which defines it—ci⁢j⁢k∈Aut(xi|Ui⁢j⁢k)≅G|Ui⁢j⁢kc_{ijk}\in\operatorname{Aut}(x_{i}\rvert_{U_{ijk}})\cong G\rvert_{U_{ijk}}𝑐_{𝑖⁢𝑗⁢𝑘}∈Aut(𝑥_𝑖|_𝑈_{𝑖⁢𝑗⁢𝑘})≅𝐺|_𝑈_{𝑖⁢𝑗⁢𝑘}. It is obvious that {ci⁢j⁢k}subscript𝑐𝑖𝑗𝑘\{c_{ijk}\}{𝑐_{𝑖⁢𝑗⁢𝑘}} is a cocycle.

Returning to stacks for a moment, a stack 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 determines an object π0⁢(𝒢)subscript𝜋0𝒢\pi_{0}(\mathcal{G})𝜋₀⁢(𝒢), defined as the sheaf associated to the presheaf of connected components of 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢, where the latter is the presheaf that to each object U𝑈U𝑈 of 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢 assigns the set of isomorphism classes of objects of 𝒢⁢(U)𝒢𝑈\mathcal{G}(U)𝒢⁢(𝑈). By definition, if 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 is a gerbe, then π0⁢(𝒢)=∗subscript𝜋0𝒢\pi_{0}(\mathcal{G})=*𝜋₀⁢(𝒢)=∗. In general, writing just π0subscript𝜋0\pi_{0}𝜋₀ in place of π0⁢(𝒢)subscript𝜋0𝒢\pi_{0}(\mathcal{G})𝜋₀⁢(𝒢), by base changing to π0subscript𝜋0\pi_{0}𝜋₀, namely considering the site 𝖢/π0𝖢subscript𝜋0\mathsf{C}/\pi_{0}𝖢/𝜋₀, every stack 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 is (tautologically) a gerbe over π0subscript𝜋0\pi_{0}𝜋₀ [27].

Example 2.1.
  1. (i)

    The trivial gerbe with band G𝐺G𝐺 is the stack Tors⁡(G)Tors𝐺\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(G)"Tors"(𝐺) of G𝐺G𝐺-torsors. Moreover, for any gerbe 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢, the choice of an object x𝑥x𝑥 in 𝒢⁢(U)𝒢𝑈\mathcal{G}(U)𝒢⁢(𝑈) determines an equivalence of gerbes 𝒢|U≅Tors(G|U)\mathcal{G}\rvert_{U}\cong\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(G\rvert_{U}){𝒢|}_𝑈≅"Tors"(𝐺|_𝑈), over 𝖢/U𝖢𝑈\mathsf{C}/U𝖢/𝑈, where G=Aut𝒢⁡(x)𝐺subscriptAut𝒢𝑥G=\operatorname{Aut}_{\mathcal{G}}(x)𝐺=Aut_𝒢(𝑥). There is an equivalence Tors⁡(G)≅BGTors𝐺subscriptB𝐺\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(G)\cong\mathrm{B}_{G}"Tors"(𝐺)≅B_𝐺, the topos of (left) G𝐺G𝐺-objects of 𝖢~superscript𝖢~\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖢^~ ([21]).

  2. (ii)

    Any line bundle L𝐿L𝐿 over an algebraic variety X𝑋X𝑋 over ℚℚ\mathbb{Q}ℚ determines a gerbe 𝒢nsubscript𝒢𝑛\mathcal{G}_{n}𝒢_𝑛 with band 𝝁nsubscript𝝁𝑛\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n}𝝁_𝑛 (the sheaf of n𝑛n𝑛th roots of unity) for any n>1𝑛1n>1𝑛>1 as follows: Over any open set U𝑈U𝑈, consider the category of pairs (ℒ,α)ℒ𝛼(\mathcal{L},\alpha)(ℒ,𝛼) where ℒℒ\mathcal{L}ℒ is a line bundle on U𝑈U𝑈 and α:ℒ⊗n→∼L:𝛼similar-to→superscriptℒtensor-productabsent𝑛𝐿\alpha:\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\xrightarrow{\sim}L𝛼:ℒ^{⊗𝑛}→┬∼𝐿 is an isomorphism of line bundles over U𝑈U𝑈. These assemble to the gerbe Gnsubscript𝐺𝑛G_{n}𝐺_𝑛 of n𝑛n𝑛th roots of L𝐿L𝐿. This is an example of a lifting gerbe §2.2.

Remark.

One also has the following interpretation, which shows that, in a fairly precise sense, a gerbe is the categorical analog of a torsor. Let 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 be a gerbe over 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢, let {Ui}subscript𝑈𝑖\{U_{i}\}{𝑈_𝑖} be a cover of U∈Ob⁢(𝖢)𝑈Ob𝖢U\in\mathrm{Ob}(\mathsf{C})𝑈∈Ob⁢(𝖢), and let {xi}subscript𝑥𝑖\{x_{i}\}{𝑥_𝑖} be a collection of objects xi∈𝒢⁢(Ui)subscript𝑥𝑖𝒢subscript𝑈𝑖x_{i}\in\mathcal{G}(U_{i})𝑥_𝑖∈𝒢⁢(𝑈_𝑖). The G𝐺G𝐺-torsors Ei⁢j=Hom⁡(xj,xi)subscript𝐸𝑖𝑗Homsubscript𝑥𝑗subscript𝑥𝑖E_{ij}=\operatorname{Hom}(x_{j},x_{i})𝐸_{𝑖⁢𝑗}=Hom(𝑥_𝑗,𝑥_𝑖) are part of a “torsor cocycle” γi⁢j⁢k:Ei⁢j⊗Ej⁢k→Ei⁢k:subscript𝛾𝑖𝑗𝑘→tensor-productsubscript𝐸𝑖𝑗subscript𝐸𝑗𝑘subscript𝐸𝑖𝑘\gamma_{ijk}\colon E_{ij}\otimes E_{jk}\to E_{ik}𝛾_{𝑖⁢𝑗⁢𝑘}:𝐸_{𝑖⁢𝑗}⊗𝐸_{𝑗⁢𝑘}→𝐸_{𝑖⁢𝑘}, locally given by ci⁢j⁢ksubscript𝑐𝑖𝑗𝑘c_{ijk}𝑐_{𝑖⁢𝑗⁢𝑘}, above, and subject to the obvious 2-cocycle identity [9, (5-10) on p. 201]. Let Tors⁡(G)Tors𝐺\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(G)"Tors"(𝐺) be the stack of G𝐺G𝐺-torsors over X𝑋X𝑋. Since G𝐺G𝐺 is assumed abelian, Tors⁡(G)Tors𝐺\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(G)"Tors"(𝐺) has a group-like composition law given by the standard Baer sum. The fact that 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 itself is locally equivalent to Tors⁡(G)Tors𝐺\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(G)"Tors"(𝐺), plus the datum of the torsor cocycle {Ei⁢j}subscript𝐸𝑖𝑗\{E_{ij}\}{𝐸_{𝑖⁢𝑗}}, show that 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 is equivalent to a Tors⁡(G)Tors𝐺\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(G)"Tors"(𝐺)-torsor.

The primary examples of abelian gerbes occurring in this paper are the gerbe of local lifts associated to a central extension and four-term complexes, described in the next two sections.

2.2. The gerbe of lifts associated with a central extension

(See [21, 7, 9].) A central extension

(2.2.1) 0⟶A⁢⟶ı⁢E⁢⟶𝑝⁢G⟶0⟶0𝐴italic-ı⟶𝐸𝑝⟶𝐺⟶00\longrightarrow A\overset{\imath}{\longrightarrow}E\overset{p}{% \longrightarrow}G\longrightarrow 00⟶𝐴⁢⟶┬ı⁢𝐸⁢⟶┬p⁢𝐺⟶0

of sheaves of groups determines a homotopy-exact sequence

Tors⁡(A)⟶Tors⁡(E)⟶Tors⁡(G),⟶Tors𝐴Tors𝐸⟶Tors𝐺\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(A)\longrightarrow\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(E)% \longrightarrow\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(G),"Tors"(𝐴)⟶"Tors"(𝐸)⟶"Tors"(𝐺),

which is an extension of topoi with characteristic class c∈H2⁢(BG,A)𝑐superscript𝐻2subscriptB𝐺𝐴c\in H^{2}(\mathrm{B}_{G},A)𝑐∈𝐻²⁢(B_𝐺,𝐴). (Recall that A𝐴A𝐴 is abelian and that Tors⁡(G)Tors𝐺\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(G)"Tors"(𝐺) is equivalent to BGsubscriptB𝐺\mathrm{B}_{G}B_𝐺.) If 𝖷𝖷\mathsf{X}𝖷 is any topos over Tors⁡(G)≅BGTors𝐺subscriptB𝐺\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(G)\cong\mathrm{B}_{G}"Tors"(𝐺)≅B_𝐺, the gerbe of lifts is the gerbe with band A𝐴A𝐴

ℰ=𝖧𝗈𝗆BG⁡(𝖷,BE),ℰsubscript𝖧𝗈𝗆subscriptB𝐺𝖷subscriptB𝐸\mathcal{E}=\operatorname{\mathsf{Hom}}_{\mathrm{B}_{G}}(\mathsf{X},\mathrm{B}% _{E}),ℰ=𝖧𝗈𝗆_B_𝐺(𝖷,B_𝐸),

where 𝖧𝗈𝗆𝖧𝗈𝗆\operatorname{\mathsf{Hom}}𝖧𝗈𝗆 denotes the cartesian morphisms. The class c⁢(ℰ)∈H2⁢(𝖷,A)𝑐ℰsuperscript𝐻2𝖷𝐴c(\mathcal{E})\in H^{2}(\mathsf{X},A)𝑐⁢(ℰ)∈𝐻²⁢(𝖷,𝐴) is the pullback of c𝑐c𝑐 along the map 𝖷→BG→𝖷subscriptB𝐺\mathsf{X}\to\mathrm{B}_{G}𝖷→B_𝐺. By the universal property of BGsubscriptB𝐺\mathrm{B}_{G}B_𝐺, the morphism 𝖷→BG→𝖷subscriptB𝐺\mathsf{X}\to\mathrm{B}_{G}𝖷→B_𝐺 corresponds to a G𝐺G𝐺-torsor P𝑃P𝑃 of 𝖷𝖷\mathsf{X}𝖷, hence the A𝐴A𝐴-gerbe ℰℰ\mathcal{E}ℰ is the gerbe whose objects are (locally) pairs of the form (Q,λ)𝑄𝜆(Q,\lambda)(𝑄,𝜆), where Q𝑄Q𝑄 is an E𝐸E𝐸-torsor and λ:Q→P:𝜆→𝑄𝑃\lambda\colon Q\to P𝜆:𝑄→𝑃 an equivariant map. It is easy to see that an automorphism of an object (Q,λ)𝑄𝜆(Q,\lambda)(𝑄,𝜆) can be identified with an element of A𝐴A𝐴, so that A𝐴A𝐴 is indeed the band of ℰℰ\mathcal{E}ℰ.

Let us take 𝖷=𝖢~𝖷superscript𝖢~\mathsf{X}=\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖷=𝖢^~, and let P𝑃P𝑃 be a G𝐺G𝐺-torsor. With the same assumptions as the end of § 2.1, let X𝑋X𝑋 be an object of 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢 with a cover {Ui}subscript𝑈𝑖\{U_{i}\}{𝑈_𝑖}. In this case, the class of ℰℰ\mathcal{E}ℰ is computed by choosing E|UiE\rvert_{U_{i}}𝐸|_𝑈_𝑖-torsors Qisubscript𝑄𝑖Q_{i}𝑄_𝑖 and equivariant maps λi:Qi→P|Ui\lambda_{i}\colon Q_{i}\to P\rvert_{U_{i}}𝜆_𝑖:𝑄_𝑖→𝑃|_𝑈_𝑖. Up to refining the cover, let αi⁢j:Qj→Qi:subscript𝛼𝑖𝑗→subscript𝑄𝑗subscript𝑄𝑖\alpha_{ij}\colon Q_{j}\to Q_{i}𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑗}:𝑄_𝑗→𝑄_𝑖 be an E𝐸E𝐸-torsor isomorphism such that λi∘αi⁢j=λjsubscript𝜆𝑖subscript𝛼𝑖𝑗subscript𝜆𝑗\lambda_{i}\circ\alpha_{ij}=\lambda_{j}𝜆_𝑖∘𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑗}=𝜆_𝑗. With these choices the class of ℰℰ\mathcal{E}ℰ is given by the cocycle αi⁢j∘αj⁢k∘αi⁢k−1subscript𝛼𝑖𝑗subscript𝛼𝑗𝑘superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑖𝑘1\alpha_{ij}\circ\alpha_{jk}\circ\alpha_{ik}^{-1}𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑗}∘𝛼_{𝑗⁢𝑘}∘𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑘}⁻¹.

Remark 2.2.

The above argument gives the well known boundary map [21, Proposition 4.3.4]

∂1:H1⁢(G)⟶H2⁢(A):superscript1⟶superscript𝐻1𝐺superscript𝐻2𝐴\partial^{1}\colon H^{1}(G)\longrightarrow H^{2}(A)∂¹:𝐻¹⁢(𝐺)⟶𝐻²⁢(𝐴)

(where we have omitted 𝖷𝖷\mathsf{X}𝖷 from the notation). Dropping down one degree we get [ibid., Proposition 3.3.1]

∂0:H0⁢(G)⟶H1⁢(A).:superscript0⟶superscript𝐻0𝐺superscript𝐻1𝐴\partial^{0}\colon H^{0}(G)\longrightarrow H^{1}(A).∂⁰:𝐻⁰⁢(𝐺)⟶𝐻¹⁢(𝐴).

In fact these are just the boundary maps determined by the above short exact sequence when all objects are abelian. The latter can be specialized even further: if g:∗→Gg\colon*\to G𝑔:∗→𝐺, then by pullback the fiber Egsubscript𝐸𝑔E_{g}𝐸_𝑔 is an A𝐴A𝐴-torsor [22].

2.3. Four-term complexes

Let 𝖢ab~subscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}𝖢^~_ab be the category of abelian sheaves over the site 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢. Below we shall be interested in four-term exact sequences of the form:

(2.3.1) 0⟶A⁢⟶ı⁢L1⁢⟶∂⁢L0⁢⟶𝑝⁢B⟶0.⟶0𝐴italic-ı⟶subscript𝐿1⟶subscript𝐿0𝑝⟶𝐵⟶00\longrightarrow A\overset{\imath}{\longrightarrow}L_{1}\overset{\partial}{% \longrightarrow}L_{0}\overset{p}{\longrightarrow}B\longrightarrow 0.0⟶𝐴⁢⟶┬ı⁢𝐿₁⁢⟶┬∂⁢𝐿₀⁢⟶┬p⁢𝐵⟶0.

Let Ch+⁢(𝖢ab~)subscriptChsubscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathrm{Ch}_{+}(\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}})Ch₊⁢(𝖢^~_ab) be the category of positively graded homological complexes of abelian sheaves. The above sequence can be thought of as a (non-exact) sequence

0⟶A⁢[1]⟶[L1⟶L0]⟶B⟶0⟶0𝐴delimited-[]1⟶delimited-[]⟶subscript𝐿1subscript𝐿0⟶𝐵⟶00\longrightarrow A[1]\longrightarrow[L_{1}\longrightarrow L_{0}]% \longrightarrow B\longrightarrow 00⟶𝐴⁢[1]⟶[𝐿₁⟶𝐿₀]⟶𝐵⟶0

of morphisms of Ch+⁢(𝖢ab~)subscriptChsubscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathrm{Ch}_{+}(\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}})Ch₊⁢(𝖢^~_ab). This sequence is short-exact in the sense of Picard categories, namely as a short exact sequence of Picard stacks

0⟶Tors⁡(A)⟶ℒ⁢⟶𝑝⁢B⟶0,⟶0Tors𝐴⟶ℒ𝑝⟶𝐵⟶00\longrightarrow\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(A)\longrightarrow\mathcal{L}% \overset{p}{\longrightarrow}B\longrightarrow 0,0⟶"Tors"(𝐴)⟶ℒ⁢⟶┬p⁢𝐵⟶0,

where ℒℒ\mathcal{L}ℒ is the strictly commutative Picard stack associated to the complex L1→L0→subscript𝐿1subscript𝐿0L_{1}\to L_{0}𝐿₁→𝐿₀ and the abelian object B𝐵B𝐵 is considered as a discrete stack in the obvious way. We have isomorphisms A≅π1⁢(ℒ)𝐴subscript𝜋1ℒA\cong\pi_{1}(\mathcal{L})𝐴≅𝜋₁⁢(ℒ) and B≅π0⁢(ℒ)𝐵subscript𝜋0ℒB\cong\pi_{0}(\mathcal{L})𝐵≅𝜋₀⁢(ℒ), where the former is the automorphism sheaf of the object 0∈ℒ0ℒ0\in\mathcal{L}0∈ℒ and the latter the sheaf of connected components (see [7, 8, 13]). It is also well known that the projection p:ℒ→B:𝑝→ℒ𝐵p\colon\mathcal{L}\to B𝑝:ℒ→𝐵 makes ℒℒ\mathcal{L}ℒ a gerbe over B𝐵B𝐵. In this case the band of ℒℒ\mathcal{L}ℒ over B𝐵B𝐵 is ABsubscript𝐴𝐵A_{B}𝐴_𝐵, thereby determining a class in H2⁢(B,A)superscript𝐻2𝐵𝐴H^{2}(B,A)𝐻²⁢(𝐵,𝐴).777This is part of the invariant classifying the four-term sequence, see the remarks in [8, §6].

Rather than considering ℒℒ\mathcal{L}ℒ itself as a gerbe over B𝐵B𝐵, we shall be interested in its fibers above generalized points β:∗→B\beta\colon*\to B𝛽:∗→𝐵. Let us put 𝒜=Tors⁡(A)𝒜Tors𝐴\mathcal{A}=\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(A)𝒜="Tors"(𝐴). By a categorification of the arguments in [22], the fiber ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽 above β𝛽\beta𝛽 is an 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A}𝒜-torsor, hence an abelian A𝐴A𝐴-gerbe, by the observation at the end of § 2.1 (see also the equivalence described in [6]). ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽 is canonically equivalent to 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A}𝒜 whenever β=0𝛽0\beta=0𝛽=0. Writing

Hom𝖢~⁡(∗,B)≅Hom𝖢ab~⁡(ℤ,B)=H0⁢(B),subscriptHomsuperscript𝖢~𝐵subscriptHomsubscriptsuperscript𝖢~abℤ𝐵superscript𝐻0𝐵\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{C}^{~}}(*,B)\cong\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{C}^{~% }_{\mathrm{ab}}}(\mathbb{Z},B)=H^{0}(B),Hom_𝖢^~(∗,𝐵)≅Hom_𝖢^~_ab(ℤ,𝐵)=𝐻⁰⁢(𝐵),

we have the homomorphism

(2.3.2) ∂2:H0⁢(B)⟶H2⁢(A),:superscript2⟶superscript𝐻0𝐵superscript𝐻2𝐴\partial^{2}:H^{0}(B)\longrightarrow H^{2}(A),∂²:𝐻⁰⁢(𝐵)⟶𝐻²⁢(𝐴),

which sends β𝛽\beta𝛽 to the class of ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽 in H2⁢(A)superscript𝐻2𝐴H^{2}(A)𝐻²⁢(𝐴). The sum of β𝛽\beta𝛽 and β′superscript𝛽′\beta^{\prime}𝛽' is sent to the Baer sum of ℒβ+ℒβ′subscriptℒ𝛽subscriptℒsuperscript𝛽′\mathcal{L}_{\beta}+\mathcal{L}_{\beta^{\prime}}ℒ_𝛽+ℒ_𝛽', and the characteristic class is additive. In the following Lemma we show this map is the same as the one described in [21, Théorème 3.4.2].

Lemma 2.3.
  1. (i)

    The map ∂2superscript2\partial^{2}∂² in (2.3.2) is the canonical cohomological map (iterated boundary map) [21, Théorème 3.4.2]

    d2:H0⁢(B)⟶H1⁢(C)⟶H2⁢(A):superscript𝑑2⟶superscript𝐻0𝐵superscript𝐻1𝐶⟶superscript𝐻2𝐴d^{2}:H^{0}(B)\longrightarrow H^{1}(C)\longrightarrow H^{2}(A)𝑑²:{𝐻⁰⁢(𝐵)⟶𝐻¹⁢(𝐶)⟶𝐻²⁢(𝐴)}

    (C𝐶C𝐶 is defined below) arising from the four-term complex (2.3.1).

  2. (ii)

    The image of β𝛽\beta𝛽 under d2superscript𝑑2d^{2}𝑑² is the class of the gerbe ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽.

Proof.

We keep the same notation as above. Let us split (2.3.1) as

with C=Im⁢∂𝐶ImC=\operatorname{Im}\partial𝐶=Im⁢∂. By Grothendieck’s theory of extensions [22], with β:∗→B\beta\colon*\to B𝛽:∗→𝐵, the fiber (L0)βsubscriptsubscript𝐿0𝛽(L_{0})_{\beta}(𝐿₀)_𝛽 is a C𝐶C𝐶-torsor (see the end of Remark 2.2). According to section 2.2, we have a morphism Tors⁡(L1)→Tors⁡(C)→Torssubscript𝐿1Tors𝐶\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(L_{1})\to\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(C)"Tors"(𝐿₁)→"Tors"(𝐶), and the object (L0)βsubscriptsubscript𝐿0𝛽(L_{0})_{\beta}(𝐿₀)_𝛽 of Tors⁡(C)Tors𝐶\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(C)"Tors"(𝐶) gives rise to the gerbe of lifts ℰβ≡ℰL0,βsubscriptℰ𝛽subscriptℰsubscript𝐿0𝛽\mathcal{E}_{\beta}\equiv\mathcal{E}_{L_{0},\beta}ℰ_𝛽≡ℰ_{𝐿₀,𝛽}, which is an A𝐴A𝐴-gerbe. Now, consider the map assigning to β∈H0⁢(B)𝛽superscript𝐻0𝐵\beta\in H^{0}(B)𝛽∈𝐻⁰⁢(𝐵) the class of ℰβ∈H2⁢(A)subscriptℰ𝛽superscript𝐻2𝐴\mathcal{E}_{\beta}\in H^{2}(A)ℰ_𝛽∈𝐻²⁢(𝐴). By construction, this map factors through H1⁢(C)superscript𝐻1𝐶H^{1}(C)𝐻¹⁢(𝐶) by sending β𝛽\beta𝛽 to the class of the torsor (L0)βsubscriptsubscript𝐿0𝛽(L_{0})_{\beta}(𝐿₀)_𝛽. We then lift that to the class of the gerbe of lifts in H2⁢(A)superscript𝐻2𝐴H^{2}(A)𝐻²⁢(𝐴). All stages are compatible with the abelian group structures. This is the homomorphism described in [21, Théorème 3.4.2].

It is straightforward that this is just the classical lift of β𝛽\beta𝛽 through the four-term sequence (2.3.1). Indeed, this is again easily seen in terms of a Čech cover {Ui}subscript𝑈𝑖\{U_{i}\}{𝑈_𝑖} of ∗*∗. Lifts xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i}𝑥_𝑖 of β|Ui\beta\rvert_{U_{i}}𝛽|_𝑈_𝑖 are sections of the C𝐶C𝐶-torsor (L0)βsubscriptsubscript𝐿0𝛽(L_{0})_{\beta}(𝐿₀)_𝛽, therefore determining a standard C𝐶C𝐶-valued 1-cocycle {ci⁢j}subscript𝑐𝑖𝑗\{c_{ij}\}{𝑐_{𝑖⁢𝑗}}. From section 2.2 we then obtain an A𝐴A𝐴-valued 2-cocycle {ai⁢j⁢k}subscript𝑎𝑖𝑗𝑘\{a_{ijk}\}{𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑗⁢𝑘}} arising from the choice of local L1subscript𝐿1L_{1}𝐿₁-torsors Xisubscript𝑋𝑖X_{i}𝑋_𝑖 such that Xi→(L0)β|UiX_{i}\to(L_{0})_{\beta}\rvert_{U_{i}}𝑋_𝑖→(𝐿₀)_𝛽|_𝑈_𝑖 is (L1→C→subscript𝐿1𝐶L_{1}\to C𝐿₁→𝐶)-equivariant. Note that in the case at hand, π:L1→C:𝜋→subscript𝐿1𝐶\pi\colon L_{1}\to C𝜋:𝐿₁→𝐶 being an epimorphism, the lifting of the torsor (L0)βsubscriptsubscript𝐿0𝛽(L_{0})_{\beta}(𝐿₀)_𝛽 is done by choosing local trivializations, i.e. the xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i}𝑥_𝑖 above, and then choosing Xi=L1|UiX_{i}=L_{1}\rvert_{U_{i}}𝑋_𝑖=𝐿₁|_𝑈_𝑖.

The same argument shows that the class of ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽, introduced earlier, is the same as that of ℰβsubscriptℰ𝛽\mathcal{E}_{\beta}ℰ_𝛽. This is a consequence of the following well known facts: objects of ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽 are locally lifts of β𝛽\beta𝛽 to L0subscript𝐿0L_{0}𝐿₀; morphisms between them are given by elements of L1subscript𝐿1L_{1}𝐿₁ acting through ∂\partial∂. As a result, automorphisms are sections of A𝐴A𝐴 and clearly the class so obtained coincides with that of ℰβsubscriptℰ𝛽\mathcal{E}_{\beta}ℰ_𝛽. Therefore ℰβsubscriptℰ𝛽\mathcal{E}_{\beta}ℰ_𝛽 and ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽 are equivalent and the homomorphism of [21, Théorème 3.4.2] is equal to (2.3.2), as required. ∎

From the proof of the above lemma, we obtain the following two descriptions of the A𝐴A𝐴-gerbe ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽.

Corollary 2.4.

(i) For any four-term complex (2.3.1) and any generalized point β𝛽\betaβ of B𝐵BB, the fiber ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒᵦ is a gerbe. Explicitly, it is the stack associated with the prestack which attaches to U𝑈UU the groupoid ℒβ⁢(U)subscriptℒ𝛽𝑈\mathcal{L}_{\beta}(U)ℒᵦ⁢(U) whose objects are elements g∈L0⁢(U)𝑔subscript𝐿0𝑈g\in L_{0}(U)g∈L₀⁢(U) with p⁢(g)=β𝑝𝑔𝛽p(g)=\betap⁢(g)=β and morphisms between g𝑔gg and g′superscript𝑔′g^{\prime}g' given by elements hℎhh of L1⁢(U)subscript𝐿1𝑈L_{1}(U)L₁⁢(U) satisfying ∂(h)=g−g′ℎ𝑔superscript𝑔′\partial(h)=g-g^{\prime}∂(h)=g-g'.

(ii) The A𝐴AA-gerbe ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒᵦ is the lifting gerbe of the C𝐶CC-torsor (L0)βsubscriptsubscript𝐿0𝛽(L_{0})_{\beta}(L₀)ᵦ to a L1subscript𝐿1L_{1}L₁-torsor. ∎

We will use both descriptions in §5 especially in the comparison of the Gersten and the Heisenberg gerbe of a codimension two cycle, in the case that it is an intersection of divisors.

A slightly different point of view is the following. Recast the sequence (2.3.1) as a quasi-isomorphism

A⁢[2]⁢⟶≅⁢[L1⟶L0⟶B]𝐴delimited-[]2⟶delimited-[]⟶subscript𝐿1subscript𝐿0⟶𝐵A[2]\overset{\cong}{\longrightarrow}\bigl{[}L_{1}\longrightarrow L_{0}% \longrightarrow B\bigr{]}𝐴⁢[2]⁢⟶┬≅⁢[𝐿₁⟶𝐿₀⟶𝐵]

of three-term complexes of Ch+⁢(𝖢ab~)subscriptChsubscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathrm{Ch}_{+}(\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}})Ch₊⁢(𝖢^~_ab), where now A𝐴A𝐴 has been shifted two places to the left. Also, relabel the right hand side as L2′→L1′→L0′→subscriptsuperscript𝐿′2subscriptsuperscript𝐿′1→subscriptsuperscript𝐿′0L^{\prime}_{2}\to L^{\prime}_{1}\to L^{\prime}_{0}𝐿'₂→𝐿'₁→𝐿'₀ (where again we employ homological degrees) for convenience. By [35], the above morphism of complexes of Ch+⁢(𝖢ab~)subscriptChsubscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathrm{Ch}_{+}(\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}})Ch₊⁢(𝖢^~_ab), placed in degrees [−2,0]20[-2,0][-2,0], gives an equivalence between the corresponding associated strictly commutative Picard 2-stacks

𝔄⁢⟶≅⁢𝔏𝔄⟶𝔏\mathfrak{A}\overset{\cong}{\longrightarrow}\mathfrak{L}𝔄⁢⟶┬≅⁢𝔏

over 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢. Here 𝔏=[L2′→L1′→L0′]~𝔏superscriptdelimited-[]→subscriptsuperscript𝐿′2subscriptsuperscript𝐿′1→subscriptsuperscript𝐿′0~\mathfrak{L}=[L^{\prime}_{2}\to L^{\prime}_{1}\to L^{\prime}_{0}]^{~}𝔏=[𝐿'₂→𝐿'₁→𝐿'₀]^~ and 𝔄=[A→0→0]~≅Tors⁡(𝒜)≅Gerbes⁡(A)𝔄superscriptdelimited-[]→𝐴0→0~Tors𝒜Gerbes𝐴\mathfrak{A}=[A\to 0\to 0]^{~}\cong\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(\mathcal{A})% \cong\operatorname{\textsc{Gerbes}}(A)𝔄=[𝐴→0→0]^~≅"Tors"(𝒜)≅"Gerbes"(𝐴). This time we have π0⁢(𝔏)=π1⁢(𝔏)=0subscript𝜋0𝔏subscript𝜋1𝔏0\pi_{0}(\mathfrak{L})=\pi_{1}(\mathfrak{L})=0𝜋₀⁢(𝔏)=𝜋₁⁢(𝔏)=0, and π2⁢(𝔏)≅Asubscript𝜋2𝔏𝐴\pi_{2}(\mathfrak{L})\cong A𝜋₂⁢(𝔏)≅𝐴, as it follows directly from the quasi-isomorphism above. Thus 𝔏𝔏\mathfrak{L}𝔏 is 2-connected, namely any two objects are locally (i.e. after base change) connected by an arrow; similarly, any two arrows with the same source and target are—again, locally—connected by a 2-arrow.

Locally, any object of 𝔏𝔏\mathfrak{L}𝔏 is a section β∈B=L0′𝛽𝐵subscriptsuperscript𝐿′0\beta\in B=L^{\prime}_{0}𝛽∈𝐵=𝐿'₀. By the preceding argument, the Picard stack ℒβ=Aut𝔏⁡(β)subscriptℒ𝛽subscriptAut𝔏𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}=\operatorname{Aut}_{\mathfrak{L}}(\beta)ℒ_𝛽=Aut_𝔏(𝛽) is an A𝐴A𝐴-gerbe, and the assignment β↦ℒβmaps-to𝛽subscriptℒ𝛽\beta\mapsto\mathcal{L}_{\beta}𝛽↦ℒ_𝛽 realizes (a quasi-inverse of) the equivalence between 𝔄𝔄\mathfrak{A}𝔄 and 𝔏𝔏\mathfrak{L}𝔏. It is easy to see that ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽 is the same as the fiber over β𝛽\beta𝛽 introduced before.

In particular, for the Gersten resolution (5.1.1), (5.1.2), for 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂, we get the equivalence of Picard 2-stacks

(2.3.3) Gerbes⁡(𝒦2)≅[G2X]~.Gerbessubscript𝒦2superscriptdelimited-[]superscriptsubscript𝐺2𝑋~\operatorname{\textsc{Gerbes}}(\mathcal{K}_{2})\cong\bigl{[}G_{2}^{X}\bigr{]}^% {~}."Gerbes"(𝒦₂)≅[𝐺₂^𝑋]^~.

3. The Heisenberg group

The purpose of this section is to describe a functor H:𝖠𝖻×𝖠𝖻→𝖦𝗋𝗉:𝐻→𝖠𝖻𝖠𝖻𝖦𝗋𝗉H\colon\mathsf{Ab}\times\mathsf{Ab}\to\mathsf{Grp}𝐻:𝖠𝖻×𝖠𝖻→𝖦𝗋𝗉, where 𝖠𝖻𝖠𝖻\mathsf{Ab}𝖠𝖻 is the category of abelian groups and 𝖦𝗋𝗉𝖦𝗋𝗉\mathsf{Grp}𝖦𝗋𝗉 that of groups. If 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢 is a site, the method immediately generalizes to the categories of abelian groups and of groups in 𝖢~superscript𝖢~\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖢^~, the topos of sheaves on 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢. For any pair A,B𝐴𝐵A,B𝐴,𝐵 of abelian sheaves on 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢, there is a canonical Heisenberg sheaf HA,Bsubscript𝐻𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵} (of non-commutative groups on 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢), a central extension of A×B𝐴𝐵A\times B𝐴×𝐵 by A⊗Btensor-product𝐴𝐵A\otimes B𝐴⊗𝐵.

The definition of H𝐻H𝐻 is based on a generalization of the Heisenberg group construction due to Brylinski [10, §5]. A pullback along the diagonal map A→A⊗A→𝐴tensor-product𝐴𝐴A\to A\otimes A𝐴→𝐴⊗𝐴 gives the extension constructed by Poonen and Rains [31].

3.1. The Heisenberg group

Let A𝐴A𝐴 and B𝐵B𝐵 be abelian groups. Consider the (central) extension

(3.1.1) 0→A⊗B→HA,B→A×B→0→0tensor-product𝐴𝐵→subscript𝐻𝐴𝐵→𝐴𝐵→00\to A\otimes B\to H_{A,B}\to A\times B\to 00→𝐴⊗𝐵→𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵}→𝐴×𝐵→0

where the group HA,Bsubscript𝐻𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵} is defined by the group law:

(3.1.2) (a,b,t)⁢(a′,b′,t′)=(a⁢a′,b⁢b′,t+t′+a⊗b′).𝑎𝑏𝑡superscript𝑎′superscript𝑏′superscript𝑡′𝑎superscript𝑎′𝑏superscript𝑏′𝑡superscript𝑡′tensor-product𝑎superscript𝑏′(a,b,t)\,(a^{\prime},b^{\prime},t^{\prime})=(aa^{\prime},bb^{\prime},t+t^{% \prime}+a\otimes b^{\prime}).(𝑎,𝑏,𝑡)⁢(𝑎',𝑏',𝑡')=(𝑎⁢𝑎',𝑏⁢𝑏',𝑡+𝑡'+𝑎⊗𝑏').

Here a,a′𝑎superscript𝑎′a,a^{\prime}𝑎,𝑎' are elements of A𝐴A𝐴, b,b′𝑏superscript𝑏′b,b^{\prime}𝑏,𝑏' of B𝐵B𝐵, and t,t′𝑡superscript𝑡′t,t^{\prime}𝑡,𝑡' of A⊗Btensor-product𝐴𝐵A\otimes B𝐴⊗𝐵. The nonabelian group HA,Bsubscript𝐻𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵} is evidently a functor of the pair (A,B)𝐴𝐵(A,B)(𝐴,𝐵), namely a pair of homomorphisms (f:A→A′,g:B→B′):𝑓→𝐴superscript𝐴′𝑔:→𝐵superscript𝐵′(f\colon A\to A^{\prime},g\colon B\to B^{\prime})(𝑓:𝐴→{𝐴',𝑔}:𝐵→𝐵') induces a homomorphism Hf,g:HA,B→HA′,B′:subscript𝐻𝑓𝑔→subscript𝐻𝐴𝐵subscript𝐻superscript𝐴′superscript𝐵′H_{f,g}\colon H_{A,B}\to H_{A^{\prime},B^{\prime}}𝐻_{𝑓,𝑔}:𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵}→𝐻_{𝐴',𝐵'}. The special case A=B=𝝁n𝐴𝐵subscript𝝁𝑛A=B=\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n}𝐴=𝐵=𝝁_𝑛 occurs in Brylinski’s treatment of the regulator map to étale cohomology [10].

The map

(3.1.3) f:(A×B)×(A×B)⟶A⊗B,f⁢(a,b,a′,b′)=a⊗b′,:𝑓formulae-sequence⟶𝐴𝐵𝐴𝐵tensor-product𝐴𝐵𝑓𝑎𝑏superscript𝑎′superscript𝑏′tensor-product𝑎superscript𝑏′f\colon(A\times B)\times(A\times B)\longrightarrow A\otimes B,\qquad f(a,b,a^{% \prime},b^{\prime})=a\otimes b^{\prime},𝑓:{(𝐴×𝐵)×(𝐴×𝐵)⟶𝐴⊗𝐵,𝑓⁢(𝑎,𝑏,𝑎',𝑏')=𝑎⊗𝑏'},

is a cocycle representing the class of the extension (3.1.1) in H2⁢(A×B,A⊗B)superscript𝐻2𝐴𝐵tensor-product𝐴𝐵H^{2}(A\times B,A\otimes B)𝐻²⁢(𝐴×𝐵,𝐴⊗𝐵) (group cohomology). Its alternation

φf:∧ℤ2(A×B)⟶A⊗B,φf⁢((a,b),(a′,b′))=a⊗b′−a′⊗b,:subscript𝜑𝑓formulae-sequence⟶superscriptsubscriptℤ2𝐴𝐵tensor-product𝐴𝐵subscript𝜑𝑓𝑎𝑏superscript𝑎′superscript𝑏′tensor-product𝑎superscript𝑏′tensor-productsuperscript𝑎′𝑏\varphi_{f}\colon\wedge_{\mathbb{Z}}^{2}(A\times B)\longrightarrow A\otimes B,% \qquad\varphi_{f}((a,b),(a^{\prime},b^{\prime}))=a\otimes b^{\prime}-a^{\prime% }\otimes b,𝜑_𝑓:{∧_ℤ²(𝐴×𝐵)⟶𝐴⊗𝐵,𝜑_𝑓⁢((𝑎,𝑏),(𝑎',𝑏'))=𝑎⊗𝑏'-𝑎'⊗𝑏},

coincides with the standard commutator map and represents the value of the projection of the class of f𝑓f𝑓 under the third map in the universal coefficient sequence

0⟶Ext1⁡(A×B,A⊗B)⟶H2⁢(A×B,A⊗B)⟶Hom⁡(∧ℤ2(A×B),A⊗B).⟶0superscriptExt1𝐴𝐵tensor-product𝐴𝐵⟶superscript𝐻2𝐴𝐵tensor-product𝐴𝐵⟶Homsuperscriptsubscriptℤ2𝐴𝐵tensor-product𝐴𝐵0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^{1}(A\times B,A\otimes B)\longrightarrow H^% {2}(A\times B,A\otimes B)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(\wedge_{\mathbb{Z}}% ^{2}(A\times B),A\otimes B).0⟶Ext¹(𝐴×𝐵,𝐴⊗𝐵)⟶𝐻²⁢(𝐴×𝐵,𝐴⊗𝐵)⟶Hom(∧_ℤ²(𝐴×𝐵),𝐴⊗𝐵).

As for the commutator map, it is equal to [s,s]:∧ℤ2(A×B)→A⊗B:𝑠𝑠→superscriptsubscriptℤ2𝐴𝐵tensor-product𝐴𝐵[s,s]\colon\wedge_{\mathbb{Z}}^{2}(A\times B)\to A\otimes B[𝑠,𝑠]:∧_ℤ²(𝐴×𝐵)→𝐴⊗𝐵, where s:A×B→HA,B:𝑠→𝐴𝐵subscript𝐻𝐴𝐵s\colon A\times B\to H_{A,B}𝑠:𝐴×𝐵→𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵} is a set-theoretic lift, but the map actually is independent of the choice of s𝑠s𝑠. (For details see, e.g. the introduction to [8].)

Remark 3.1.

The properties of the class of the extension HA,Bsubscript𝐻𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵}, in particular that it is a cup-product of the fundamental classes of A𝐴A𝐴 and B𝐵B𝐵, as we can already evince from (3.1.3), are best expressed in terms of Eilenberg-Mac Lane spaces. We will do this below working in the topos of sheaves over a site.

3.2. Extension to sheaves

The construction of the Heisenberg group carries over to the sheaf context. Let 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢 be a site, and 𝖢~superscript𝖢~\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖢^~ the topos of sheaves over 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢. Denote by 𝖢ab~subscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}𝖢^~_ab the abelian group objects of 𝖢~superscript𝖢~\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖢^~, namely the abelian sheaves on 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢, and by 𝖢grp~subscriptsuperscript𝖢~grp\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{grp}}𝖢^~_grp the sheaves of groups on 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢.

For all pairs of objects A,B𝐴𝐵A,B𝐴,𝐵 of 𝖢ab~subscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}𝖢^~_ab, it is clear that the above construction of HA,Bsubscript𝐻𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵} carries over to a functor

H:𝖢ab~×𝖢ab~⟶𝖢grp~.:𝐻⟶subscriptsuperscript𝖢~absubscriptsuperscript𝖢~absubscriptsuperscript𝖢~grpH\colon\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}\times\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}% \longrightarrow\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{grp}}.𝐻:𝖢^~_ab×𝖢^~_ab⟶𝖢^~_grp.

In particular, since HA,Bsubscript𝐻𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵} is already a sheaf of sets (isomorphic to A×B×(A⊗B)𝐴𝐵tensor-product𝐴𝐵A\times B\times(A\otimes B)𝐴×𝐵×(𝐴⊗𝐵)), the only question is whether the group law varies nicely, but this is clear from its functoriality. Note further that by definition of HA,Bsubscript𝐻𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵} the resulting epimorphism HA,B→A×B→subscript𝐻𝐴𝐵𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}\to A\times B𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵}→𝐴×𝐵 has a global section s:A×B→HA,B:𝑠→𝐴𝐵subscript𝐻𝐴𝐵s\colon A\times B\to H_{A,B}𝑠:𝐴×𝐵→𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵} as objects of 𝖢~superscript𝖢~\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖢^~, namely s=(idA,idB,0)𝑠subscriptid𝐴subscriptid𝐵0s=(\operatorname{id}_{A},\operatorname{id}_{B},0)𝑠=(id_𝐴,id_𝐵,0), which we can use this to repeat the calculations of § 3.1.

In more detail, from § 2.2, the class of the central extenson (3.1.1) is to be found in H2⁢(BA×B,A⊗B)superscript𝐻2subscriptB𝐴𝐵tensor-product𝐴𝐵H^{2}(\mathrm{B}_{A\times B},A\otimes B)𝐻²⁢(B_{𝐴×𝐵},𝐴⊗𝐵) (A⊗Btensor-product𝐴𝐵A\otimes B𝐴⊗𝐵 is a trivial A×B𝐴𝐵A\times B𝐴×𝐵-module). This replaces the group cohomology of § 3.1 with its appropriate topos equivalent. By pulling back to the ambient topos, say 𝖷=𝖢~𝖷superscript𝖢~\mathsf{X}=\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖷=𝖢^~, this is the class of the gerbe of lifts from BA×Bsubscript𝐵𝐴𝐵B_{A\times B}𝐵_{𝐴×𝐵} to BHsubscript𝐵𝐻B_{H}𝐵_𝐻. We are ready to give a proof of Theorem 1.4. This proof is computational.

Proof of Theorem 1.4.

Let us go back to the cocycle calculations at the end of § 2.2, where X𝑋X𝑋 is an object of 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢 equipped with a cover 𝒰={Ui}𝒰subscript𝑈𝑖\mathcal{U}=\{U_{i}\}𝒰={𝑈_𝑖}. An A×B𝐴𝐵A\times B𝐴×𝐵-torsor (P,Q)𝑃𝑄(P,Q)(𝑃,𝑄) over X𝑋X𝑋 would be represented by a Čech cocycle (ai⁢j,bi⁢j)subscript𝑎𝑖𝑗subscript𝑏𝑖𝑗(a_{ij},b_{ij})(𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑗},𝑏_{𝑖⁢𝑗}) relative to 𝒰𝒰\mathcal{U}𝒰. The cocycle is determined by the choice of isomorphisms (P,Q)|Ui≅(A×B)|Ui(P,Q)\rvert_{U_{i}}\cong(A\times B)\rvert_{U_{i}}{(𝑃,𝑄)|}_𝑈_𝑖≅(𝐴×𝐵)|_𝑈_𝑖. Now, define Ri=HA,B|UiR_{i}=H_{A,B}\rvert_{U_{i}}𝑅_𝑖=𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵}|_𝑈_𝑖 with the trivial HA,Bsubscript𝐻𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵}-torsor structure, and let λi:Ri→(P,Q)|Ui\lambda_{i}\colon R_{i}\to(P,Q)\rvert_{U_{i}}𝜆_𝑖:𝑅_𝑖→(𝑃,𝑄)|_𝑈_𝑖 equal the epimorphism in (3.1.1). Carrying out the calculation described at the end of 2.2 with these data gives αi⁢j∘αj⁢k∘αi⁢k−1=ai⁢j⊗bj⁢ksubscript𝛼𝑖𝑗subscript𝛼𝑗𝑘superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑖𝑘1tensor-productsubscript𝑎𝑖𝑗subscript𝑏𝑗𝑘\alpha_{ij}\circ\alpha_{jk}\circ\alpha_{ik}^{-1}=a_{ij}\otimes b_{jk}𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑗}∘𝛼_{𝑗⁢𝑘}∘𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑘}⁻¹=𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑗}⊗𝑏_{𝑗⁢𝑘}, which is the cup-product in Čech cohomology of the classes corresponding to the A𝐴A𝐴-torsor P𝑃P𝑃 and the B𝐵B𝐵-torsor Q𝑄Q𝑄. In other words, the gerbe of lifts corresponding to the central extension determined by the Heisenberg group incarnates the cup product map

H1⁢(X,A)×H1⁢(X,B)⁢⟶∪⁢H2⁢(X,A⊗B).superscript𝐻1𝑋𝐴superscript𝐻1𝑋𝐵⟶superscript𝐻2𝑋tensor-product𝐴𝐵H^{1}(X,A)\times H^{1}(X,B)\overset{\cup}{\longrightarrow}H^{2}(X,A\otimes B).𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐴)×𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐵)⁢⟶┬∪⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝐴⊗𝐵).

For the choice αi⁢j=(ai⁢j,bi⁢j,0)subscript𝛼𝑖𝑗subscript𝑎𝑖𝑗subscript𝑏𝑖𝑗0\alpha_{ij}=(a_{ij},b_{ij},0)𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑗}=(𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑗},𝑏_{𝑖⁢𝑗},0), one has the following explicit calculation in the Heisenberg group

αi⁢j∘αj⁢k∘αi⁢k−1subscript𝛼𝑖𝑗subscript𝛼𝑗𝑘superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑖𝑘1\displaystyle\alpha_{ij}\circ\alpha_{jk}\circ\alpha_{ik}^{-1}𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑗}∘𝛼_{𝑗⁢𝑘}∘𝛼_{𝑖⁢𝑘}⁻¹ =(ai⁢j,bi⁢j,0)⁢(aj⁢k,bj⁢k,0)⁢(ai⁢k,bi⁢k,0)−1absentsubscript𝑎𝑖𝑗subscript𝑏𝑖𝑗0subscript𝑎𝑗𝑘subscript𝑏𝑗𝑘0superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑖𝑘subscript𝑏𝑖𝑘01\displaystyle=(a_{ij},b_{ij},0)(a_{jk},b_{jk},0)(a_{ik},b_{ik},0)^{-1}=(𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑗},𝑏_{𝑖⁢𝑗},0)⁢(𝑎_{𝑗⁢𝑘},𝑏_{𝑗⁢𝑘},0)⁢(𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑘},𝑏_{𝑖⁢𝑘},0)⁻¹
=(ai⁢k,bi⁢k,ai⁢j⊗bj⁢k)⁢(ai⁢k−1,bi⁢k−1,ai⁢k⊗bi⁢k)absentsubscript𝑎𝑖𝑘subscript𝑏𝑖𝑘tensor-productsubscript𝑎𝑖𝑗subscript𝑏𝑗𝑘subscriptsuperscript𝑎1𝑖𝑘subscriptsuperscript𝑏1𝑖𝑘tensor-productsubscript𝑎𝑖𝑘subscript𝑏𝑖𝑘\displaystyle=(a_{ik},b_{ik},a_{ij}\otimes b_{jk})(a^{-1}_{ik},b^{-1}_{ik},a_{% ik}\otimes b_{ik})=(𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑘},𝑏_{𝑖⁢𝑘},𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑗}⊗𝑏_{𝑗⁢𝑘})⁢(𝑎⁻¹_{𝑖⁢𝑘},𝑏⁻¹_{𝑖⁢𝑘},𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑘}⊗𝑏_{𝑖⁢𝑘})
=(1,1,ai⁢j⊗bj⁢k+ai⁢k⊗bi⁢k−ai⁢k⊗bi⁢k)absent11tensor-productsubscript𝑎𝑖𝑗subscript𝑏𝑗𝑘tensor-productsubscript𝑎𝑖𝑘subscript𝑏𝑖𝑘tensor-productsubscript𝑎𝑖𝑘subscript𝑏𝑖𝑘\displaystyle=(1,1,a_{ij}\otimes b_{jk}+a_{ik}\otimes b_{ik}-a_{ik}\otimes b_{% ik})=(1,1,𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑗}⊗𝑏_{𝑗⁢𝑘}+𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑘}⊗𝑏_{𝑖⁢𝑘}-𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑘}⊗𝑏_{𝑖⁢𝑘})
=(1,1,ai⁢j⊗bj⁢k);absent11tensor-productsubscript𝑎𝑖𝑗subscript𝑏𝑗𝑘\displaystyle=(1,1,a_{ij}\otimes b_{jk});=(1,1,𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑗}⊗𝑏_{𝑗⁢𝑘});

We used that the inverse of (a,b,t)𝑎𝑏𝑡(a,b,t)(𝑎,𝑏,𝑡) in the Heisenberg group is (a−1,b−1,−t+a⊗b)superscript𝑎1superscript𝑏1𝑡tensor-product𝑎𝑏(a^{-1},b^{-1},-t+a\otimes b)(𝑎⁻¹,𝑏⁻¹,-𝑡+𝑎⊗𝑏):

(a,b,t)⁢(a−1,b−1,−t+a⊗b)=(1,1,a⊗b−1+t−t+a⊗b)=(1,1,0).𝑎𝑏𝑡superscript𝑎1superscript𝑏1𝑡tensor-product𝑎𝑏11tensor-product𝑎superscript𝑏1𝑡𝑡tensor-product𝑎𝑏110(a,b,t)(a^{-1},b^{-1},-t+a\otimes b)=(1,1,a\otimes b^{-1}+t-t+a\otimes b)=(1,1% ,0).(𝑎,𝑏,𝑡)⁢(𝑎⁻¹,𝑏⁻¹,-𝑡+𝑎⊗𝑏)=(1,1,𝑎⊗𝑏⁻¹+𝑡-𝑡+𝑎⊗𝑏)=(1,1,0).

It is well known [9, Chapter 1, §1.3, Equation (1-18), p. 29] that the Čech cup-product of a={ai⁢j}𝑎subscript𝑎𝑖𝑗a=\{a_{ij}\}𝑎={𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑗}} and b={bi⁢j}𝑏subscript𝑏𝑖𝑗b=\{b_{ij}\}𝑏={𝑏_{𝑖⁢𝑗}} is given by the two-cocycle

{a∪b}i⁢j⁢k={ai⁢j⊗bj⁢k}.subscript𝑎𝑏𝑖𝑗𝑘tensor-productsubscript𝑎𝑖𝑗subscript𝑏𝑗𝑘\{a\cup b\}_{ijk}=\{a_{ij}\otimes b_{jk}\}.{𝑎∪𝑏}_{𝑖⁢𝑗⁢𝑘}={𝑎_{𝑖⁢𝑗}⊗𝑏_{𝑗⁢𝑘}}.

This proves the first three points of the statement, whereas the fourth is built-in from the very construction. The fifth follows from the fact that the class of the gerbe of lifts is bilinear: this is evident from the expression computed above. ∎

As hinted above, the cup product has a more intrinsic explanation in terms of maps between Eilenberg-Mac Lane objects in the topos. Passing to Eilenberg-Mac Lane objects in particular “explains” why the cup-product realizes the cup-product pairing. First, we state

Theorem 3.2.

The class of the extension (3.1.1) in 𝖢~superscript𝖢~\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖢^~ corresponds to (the homotopy class of) the cup product map

K⁢(A×B,1)≅K⁢(A,1)×K⁢(B,1)⟶K⁢(A⊗B,2)𝐾𝐴𝐵1𝐾𝐴1𝐾𝐵1⟶𝐾tensor-product𝐴𝐵2K(A\times B,1)\cong K(A,1)\times K(B,1)\longrightarrow K(A\otimes B,2)𝐾⁢(𝐴×𝐵,1)≅𝐾⁢(𝐴,1)×𝐾⁢(𝐵,1)⟶𝐾⁢(𝐴⊗𝐵,2)

between the identity maps of K⁢(A,1)𝐾𝐴1K(A,1)K⁢(A,1) and K⁢(B,1)𝐾𝐵1K(B,1)K⁢(B,1); its expression is given by (3.1.3).

Proof.

Observe the epimorphism HA,B→A×B→subscript𝐻𝐴𝐵𝐴𝐵H_{A,B}\to A\times B𝐻_{𝐴,𝐵}→𝐴×𝐵 has global set-theoretic sections. The statement follows from Propositions 3.3 and 3.4 below. ∎

The two main points, which we now proceed to illustrate, are that Eilenberg-Mac Lane objects represent cohomology (and hypercohomology, once we take into account simplicial objects) in a topos, and that the cohomology of a group object in a topos (such as A×B𝐴𝐵A\times B𝐴×𝐵 in 𝖢~superscript𝖢~\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖢^~) with trivial coefficients can be traded for the hypercohomology of a simplicial model of it. In this way we calculate the class of the extension as a map, and such map is identified with the cup product. We assemble the necessary results to flesh out the proof of Theorem 3.2 in the next two sections.

3.3. Simplicial computations

The class of the central extension (2.2.1) can be computed simplicially. (For the following recollections, see [25, VI.5, VI.6, VI.8] and [5, §2].)

Let 𝖳𝖳\mathsf{T}𝖳 be a topos, G𝐺G𝐺 a group-object of 𝖳𝖳\mathsf{T}𝖳 (for us it will be 𝖳=𝖢~𝖳superscript𝖢~\mathsf{T}=\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖳=𝖢^~) and B⁢G=K⁢(G,1)𝐵𝐺𝐾𝐺1BG=K(G,1)𝐵⁢𝐺=𝐾⁢(𝐺,1) the standard classifying simplicial object with Bn⁢G=Gnsubscript𝐵𝑛𝐺superscript𝐺𝑛B_{n}G=G^{n}𝐵_𝑛⁢𝐺=𝐺^𝑛 [14]. Let A𝐴A𝐴 be a trivial G𝐺G𝐺-module. We will need the following well known fact.888Unfortunately we could not find a specific entry point in the literature to reference, therefore we assemble here the necessary prerequisites. See also [11, §§2,3] for a detailed treatment in the representable case.

Proposition 3.3.

Hi⁢(BG,A)≅𝐇i⁢(B⁢G,A).superscript𝐻𝑖subscriptB𝐺𝐴superscript𝐇𝑖𝐵𝐺𝐴H^{i}(\mathrm{B}_{G},A)\cong\mathbf{H}^{i}(BG,A).𝐻^𝑖⁢(B_𝐺,𝐴)≅𝐇^𝑖⁢(𝐵⁢𝐺,𝐴).

Proof.

The object on the right is the hypercohomology as a simplicial object of 𝖳𝖳\mathsf{T}𝖳. Let X𝑋X𝑋 be a simplicial object in a topos 𝖳𝖳\mathsf{T}𝖳. One defines

𝐇i⁢(X,A)=E⁢xti⁡(ℤ⁢[X]~,A)superscript𝐇𝑖𝑋𝐴superscriptExt𝑖ℤsuperscriptdelimited-[]𝑋~𝐴\mathbf{H}^{i}(X,A)=\operatorname{\textbf{E}xt}^{i}(\mathbb{Z}[X]^{~},A)𝐇^𝑖⁢(𝑋,𝐴)={"E"⁢xt}^𝑖(ℤ⁢[𝑋]^~,𝐴)

where M~superscript𝑀~M^{~}𝑀^~, for any simplicial abelian object M𝑀M𝑀 of 𝖳𝖳\mathsf{T}𝖳, denotes the corresponding chain complex defined by Mn~=Mnsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝑛~subscript𝑀𝑛M_{n}^{~}=M_{n}𝑀_𝑛^~=𝑀_𝑛, and by taking the alternate sum of the face maps. ℤ⁢Xnℤsubscript𝑋𝑛\mathbb{Z}X_{n}ℤ⁢𝑋_𝑛 denotes the abelian object of 𝖳𝖳\mathsf{T}𝖳 generated by Xnsubscript𝑋𝑛X_{n}𝑋_𝑛. Of interest to us is the spectral sequence [5, Example (2.10) and below]:

E1p,q=Hq⁢(Xp,A)⟹𝐇∙⁢(X,A).superscriptsubscript𝐸1𝑝𝑞superscript𝐻𝑞subscript𝑋𝑝𝐴⟹superscript𝐇∙𝑋𝐴E_{1}^{p,q}=H^{q}(X_{p},A)\Longrightarrow\mathbf{H}^{\bullet}(X,A).𝐸₁^{𝑝,𝑞}=𝐻^𝑞⁢(𝑋_𝑝,𝐴)⟹𝐇^∙⁢(𝑋,𝐴).

Let X𝑋X𝑋 be any simplicial object of 𝖳𝖳\mathsf{T}𝖳. The levelwise topoi 𝖳/Xn𝖳subscript𝑋𝑛\mathsf{T}/X_{n}𝖳/𝑋_𝑛, n=0,1,…𝑛01…n=0,1,\dots𝑛={0,1,…}, form a simplicial topos 𝖷=𝖳/X𝖷𝖳𝑋\mathsf{X}=\mathsf{T}/X𝖷=𝖳/𝑋 or equivalently a topos fibered over ΔopsuperscriptΔop\Delta^{\mathrm{op}}Δᵒᵖ, where ΔΔ\DeltaΔ is the simplicial category. The topos B⁢𝖷B𝖷\mathrm{B}\mathsf{X}B⁢𝖷 of 𝖷𝖷\mathsf{X}𝖷-objects essentially consists of descent-like data, that is, objects L𝐿L𝐿 of 𝖷0subscript𝖷0\mathsf{X}_{0}𝖷₀ equipped with an arrow a:d1∗⁢L→d0∗⁢L:𝑎→superscriptsubscript𝑑1𝐿superscriptsubscript𝑑0𝐿a\colon d_{1}^{*}L\to d_{0}^{*}L𝑎:𝑑₁^∗⁢𝐿→𝑑₀^∗⁢𝐿 the cocycle condition d0∗⁢a⁢d2∗⁢a=d1∗⁢asuperscriptsubscript𝑑0𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑑2𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑑1𝑎d_{0}^{*}a\,d_{2}^{*}a=d_{1}^{*}a𝑑₀^∗⁢𝑎⁢𝑑₂^∗⁢𝑎=𝑑₁^∗⁢𝑎 and s0∗⁢a=idsuperscriptsubscript𝑠0𝑎ids_{0}^{*}a=\operatorname{id}𝑠₀^∗⁢𝑎=id (the latter is automatic if a𝑎a𝑎 is an isomorphism). By [25, VI.8.1.3], in the case where X=B⁢G𝑋𝐵𝐺X=BG𝑋=𝐵⁢𝐺, B⁢𝖷B𝖷\mathrm{B}\mathsf{X}B⁢𝖷 is nothing but BGsubscriptB𝐺\mathrm{B}_{G}B_𝐺, the topos of G𝐺G𝐺-objects of 𝖳𝖳\mathsf{T}𝖳. One also forms the topos Tot⁡(𝖷)Tot𝖷\operatorname{Tot}(\mathsf{X})Tot(𝖷), whose objects are collections Fn∈𝖷nsubscript𝐹𝑛subscript𝖷𝑛F_{n}\in\mathsf{X}_{n}𝐹_𝑛∈𝖷_𝑛 such that for each α:[m]→[n]:𝛼→delimited-[]𝑚delimited-[]𝑛\alpha\colon[m]\to[n]𝛼:[𝑚]→[𝑛] in ΔopsuperscriptΔop\Delta^{\mathrm{op}}Δᵒᵖ there is a morphism Fα:α∗⁢Fm→Fn:subscript𝐹𝛼→superscript𝛼subscript𝐹𝑚subscript𝐹𝑛F_{\alpha}\colon\alpha^{*}F_{m}\to F_{n}𝐹_𝛼:𝛼^∗⁢𝐹_𝑚→𝐹_𝑛, where α∗superscript𝛼\alpha^{*}𝛼^∗ is the inverse image corresponding to the morphism α:𝖷n→𝖷m:𝛼→subscript𝖷𝑛subscript𝖷𝑚\alpha\colon\mathsf{X}_{n}\to\mathsf{X}_{m}𝛼:𝖷_𝑛→𝖷_𝑚. There is a functor 𝑛𝑒𝑟:B⁢𝖷→Tot⁡(𝖷):𝑛𝑒𝑟→B𝖷Tot𝖷\mathit{ner}\colon\mathrm{B}\mathsf{X}\to\operatorname{Tot}(\mathsf{X})𝑛𝑒𝑟:B⁢𝖷→Tot(𝖷) sending (L,a)𝐿𝑎(L,a)(𝐿,𝑎) to the object of Tot⁡(𝖷)Tot𝖷\operatorname{Tot}(\mathsf{X})Tot(𝖷) which at level n𝑛n𝑛 equals (d0⁢⋯⁢d0)∗⁢Lsuperscriptsubscript𝑑0⋯subscript𝑑0𝐿(d_{0}\cdots d_{0})^{*}L(𝑑₀⁢⋯⁢𝑑₀)^∗⁢𝐿 (a𝑎a𝑎 enters through the resulting face maps), see loc. cit. for the actual expressions. The functor 𝑛𝑒𝑟𝑛𝑒𝑟\mathit{ner}𝑛𝑒𝑟 is the inverse image functor for a morphism Tot⁡(𝖷)→B⁢𝖷→Tot𝖷B𝖷\operatorname{Tot}(\mathsf{X})\to\mathrm{B}\mathsf{X}Tot(𝖷)→B⁢𝖷, and, 𝖷𝖷\mathsf{X}𝖷 satisfying the conditions of being a “good pseudo-category” ([25, VI 8.2]) we have an isomorphism

R⁢Γ⁢(B⁢𝖷,L)⁢⟶≅⁢R⁢Γ⁢(Tot⁡(𝖷),𝑛𝑒𝑟⁢(L))𝑅ΓB𝖷𝐿⟶𝑅ΓTot𝖷𝑛𝑒𝑟𝐿R\Gamma(\mathrm{B}\mathsf{X},L)\overset{\cong}{\longrightarrow}R\Gamma(% \operatorname{Tot}(\mathsf{X}),\mathit{ner}(L))𝑅⁢Γ⁢(B⁢𝖷,𝐿)⁢⟶┬≅⁢𝑅⁢Γ⁢(Tot(𝖷),𝑛𝑒𝑟⁢(𝐿))

and, in turn, a spectral sequence

E1p,q=Hq⁢(Xp,𝑛𝑒𝑟p⁢(L))⟹H∙⁢(B⁢𝖷,L),superscriptsubscript𝐸1𝑝𝑞superscript𝐻𝑞subscript𝑋𝑝subscript𝑛𝑒𝑟𝑝𝐿⟹superscript𝐻∙B𝖷𝐿E_{1}^{p,q}=H^{q}(X_{p},\mathit{ner}_{p}(L))\Longrightarrow H^{\bullet}(% \mathrm{B}\mathsf{X},L),𝐸₁^{𝑝,𝑞}=𝐻^𝑞⁢(𝑋_𝑝,𝑛𝑒𝑟_𝑝⁢(𝐿))⟹𝐻^∙⁢(B⁢𝖷,𝐿),

[25, VI, Corollaire 8.4.2.2]. On the left hand side we recognize the spectral sequence for the cohomology of a simplicial object in a topos [5, §2.10].

Applying the foregoing to X=B⁢G𝑋𝐵𝐺X=BG𝑋=𝐵⁢𝐺, and L𝐿L𝐿 a left G𝐺G𝐺-object of 𝖳𝖳\mathsf{T}𝖳, we obtain [25, VI.8.4.4.5]

E1p,q=Hq⁢(Gp,L)⟹H∙⁢(BG,L).superscriptsubscript𝐸1𝑝𝑞superscript𝐻𝑞superscript𝐺𝑝𝐿⟹superscript𝐻∙subscriptB𝐺𝐿E_{1}^{p,q}=H^{q}(G^{p},L)\Longrightarrow H^{\bullet}(\mathrm{B}_{G},L).𝐸₁^{𝑝,𝑞}=𝐻^𝑞⁢(𝐺^𝑝,𝐿)⟹𝐻^∙⁢(B_𝐺,𝐿).

(We set Y=e𝑌𝑒Y=e𝑌=𝑒, the terminal object of 𝖳𝖳\mathsf{T}𝖳, in the formulas from loc. cit.)

Thus if L=A𝐿𝐴L=A𝐿=𝐴, the trivial G𝐺G𝐺-module arising from a central extension of G𝐺G𝐺 by A𝐴A𝐴, by comparing the spectral sequences we can trade H2⁢(BG,A)superscript𝐻2subscriptB𝐺𝐴H^{2}(\mathrm{B}_{G},A)𝐻²⁢(B_𝐺,𝐴) for the hypercohomology 𝐇2⁢(K⁢(G,1),A)superscript𝐇2𝐾𝐺1𝐴\mathbf{H}^{2}(K(G,1),A)𝐇²⁢(𝐾⁢(𝐺,1),𝐴). ∎

3.4. The cup product

The class of the extension extension (3.1.1) corresponds to the homotopy class of a map K⁢(A×B,1)→K⁢(A⊗B,2)→𝐾𝐴𝐵1𝐾tensor-product𝐴𝐵2K(A\times B,1)\to K(A\otimes B,2)𝐾⁢(𝐴×𝐵,1)→𝐾⁢(𝐴⊗𝐵,2). We interpret it in terms of cup products of Eilenberg-Mac Lane objects.

Recall that for an object M𝑀M𝑀 of 𝖢ab~subscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}𝖢^~_ab we have K⁢(M,i)=K⁢(M⁢[i])𝐾𝑀𝑖𝐾𝑀delimited-[]𝑖K(M,i)=K(M[i])𝐾⁢(𝑀,𝑖)=𝐾⁢(𝑀⁢[𝑖]), where M⁢[i]𝑀delimited-[]𝑖M[i]𝑀⁢[𝑖] denotes M𝑀M𝑀 placed in homological degree i𝑖i𝑖, and K:Ch+⁢(𝖢ab~)→s⁢𝖢ab~:𝐾→subscriptChsubscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab𝑠subscriptsuperscript𝖢~abK\colon\mathrm{Ch}_{+}(\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}})\to s\mathsf{C}^{~}_{% \mathrm{ab}}𝐾:Ch₊⁢(𝖢^~_ab)→𝑠⁢𝖢^~_ab is the Dold-Kan functor from nonnegative chain complexes of 𝖢ab~subscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}𝖢^~_ab to simplicial abelian sheaves. Explicitly:

K⁢(M,i)n={00≤n<i,⨁s:[n]↠[i]Mn≥i.𝐾subscript𝑀𝑖𝑛cases00𝑛𝑖subscriptdirect-sum:𝑠↠delimited-[]𝑛delimited-[]𝑖𝑀𝑛𝑖K(M,i)_{n}=\begin{cases}0&0\leq n<i,\\ \bigoplus_{s\colon[n]\twoheadrightarrow[i]}M&n\geq i.\end{cases}𝐾⁢(𝑀,𝑖)_𝑛={{■("0"&"0≤n<i,"@"⨁:s↠[n][i]M"&"≥ni.")}

In particular, K⁢(M,i)i=M𝐾subscript𝑀𝑖𝑖𝑀K(M,i)_{i}=M𝐾⁢(𝑀,𝑖)_𝑖=𝑀. K𝐾K𝐾 is a quasi-inverse to the normalized complex functor N:s⁢𝖢ab~→Ch+⁢(𝖢ab~):𝑁→𝑠subscriptsuperscript𝖢~absubscriptChsubscriptsuperscript𝖢~abN\colon s\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}\to\mathrm{Ch}_{+}(\mathsf{C}^{~}_{% \mathrm{ab}})𝑁:𝑠⁢𝖢^~_ab→Ch₊⁢(𝖢^~_ab).

If X𝑋X𝑋 is a simplicial object X𝑋X𝑋 of 𝖢~superscript𝖢~\mathsf{C}^{~}𝖢^~, we have

(3.4.1) 𝐇i⁢(X,M)≅[X,K⁢(M,i)],superscript𝐇𝑖𝑋𝑀𝑋𝐾𝑀𝑖\mathbf{H}^{i}(X,M)\cong[X,K(M,i)],𝐇^𝑖⁢(𝑋,𝑀)≅[𝑋,𝐾⁢(𝑀,𝑖)],

where the right-hand side denotes the hom-set in the homotopy category [24, 5]. In particular, there is a fundamental class ıMn∈𝐇n⁢(K⁢(M,n),M)superscriptsubscriptitalic-ı𝑀𝑛superscript𝐇𝑛𝐾𝑀𝑛𝑀\imath_{M}^{n}\in\mathbf{H}^{n}(K(M,n),M)ı_𝑀^𝑛∈𝐇^𝑛⁢(𝐾⁢(𝑀,𝑛),𝑀), corresponding to the identity map.

Returning to the objects A𝐴A𝐴 and B𝐵B𝐵 of 𝖢ab~subscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}𝖢^~_ab, also recall the morphism [5, Chapter II, Equation (2.22), p. 64]

(3.4.2) δi,j:K⁢(A,i)×K⁢(B,j)⟶K⁢(A⊗B,i+j).:subscript𝛿𝑖𝑗⟶𝐾𝐴𝑖𝐾𝐵𝑗𝐾tensor-product𝐴𝐵𝑖𝑗\delta_{i,j}\colon K(A,i)\times K(B,j)\longrightarrow K(A\otimes B,i+j).𝛿_{𝑖,𝑗}:𝐾⁢(𝐴,𝑖)×𝐾⁢(𝐵,𝑗)⟶𝐾⁢(𝐴⊗𝐵,𝑖+𝑗).

It is the composition of two maps. The first is:

K⁢(A,i)×K⁢(B,j)⟶d⁢((K⁢(A,i)⊠K⁢(B,j))=(K⁢(A,i)⊗K⁢(B,j))),⟶𝐾𝐴𝑖𝐾𝐵𝑗𝑑⊠𝐾𝐴𝑖𝐾𝐵𝑗tensor-product𝐾𝐴𝑖𝐾𝐵𝑗K(A,i)\times K(B,j)\longrightarrow d((K(A,i)\boxtimes K(B,j))=(K(A,i)\otimes K% (B,j))),𝐾⁢(𝐴,𝑖)×𝐾⁢(𝐵,𝑗)⟶𝑑⁢((𝐾⁢(𝐴,𝑖)⊠𝐾⁢(𝐵,𝑗))=(𝐾⁢(𝐴,𝑖)⊗𝐾⁢(𝐵,𝑗))),

where ⊠⊠\boxtimes⊠ denotes the external tensor product of simplicial objects of 𝖢ab~subscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}𝖢^~_ab and d𝑑d𝑑 the diagonal; the second is the map in s⁢𝖢ab~𝑠subscriptsuperscript𝖢~abs\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}}𝑠⁢𝖢^~_ab corresponding to the Alexander-Whitney map under the Dold-Kan correspondence. We have:

Proposition 3.4.

The class of the extension (3.1.1) is equal to ıA1⊗ıB1=δ1,1⁢(ıA1×ıB1)tensor-productsuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ı𝐴1superscriptsubscriptitalic-ı𝐵1subscript𝛿11superscriptsubscriptitalic-ı𝐴1superscriptsubscriptitalic-ı𝐵1\imath_{A}^{1}\otimes\imath_{B}^{1}=\delta_{1,1}(\imath_{A}^{1}\times\imath_{B% }^{1})ı_A¹⊗ı_B¹=δ_{1,1}⁢(ı_A¹×ı_B¹).

Proof.

Observe that any simplicial morphism f:X→K⁢(M,i):𝑓→𝑋𝐾𝑀𝑖f\colon X\to K(M,i)𝑓:𝑋→𝐾⁢(𝑀,𝑖) is determined by fisubscript𝑓𝑖f_{i}𝑓_𝑖, the rest, for n>i𝑛𝑖n>i𝑛>𝑖, being determined by the simplicial identities. Therefore we need to compute:

K⁢(A×B,1)2≅K⁢(A,1)2×K⁢(B,1)2⟶K⁢(A⊗B,2)2,𝐾subscript𝐴𝐵12𝐾subscript𝐴12𝐾subscript𝐵12⟶𝐾subscripttensor-product𝐴𝐵22K(A\times B,1)_{2}\cong K(A,1)_{2}\times K(B,1)_{2}\longrightarrow K(A\otimes B% ,2)_{2},𝐾⁢(𝐴×𝐵,1)₂≅𝐾⁢(𝐴,1)₂×𝐾⁢(𝐵,1)₂⟶𝐾⁢(𝐴⊗𝐵,2)₂,

namely

(A×B)×(A×B)⟶(A×A)×(B×B)⟶A⊗B.⟶𝐴𝐵𝐴𝐵𝐴𝐴𝐵𝐵⟶tensor-product𝐴𝐵(A\times B)\times(A\times B)\longrightarrow(A\times A)\times(B\times B)% \longrightarrow A\otimes B.(𝐴×𝐵)×(𝐴×𝐵)⟶(𝐴×𝐴)×(𝐵×𝐵)⟶𝐴⊗𝐵.

From the expression of the Alexander-Whitney map, in e.g. [24], the image of the second map in Ch+⁢(𝖢ab~)subscriptChsubscriptsuperscript𝖢~ab\mathrm{Ch}_{+}(\mathsf{C}^{~}_{\mathrm{ab}})Ch₊⁢(𝖢^~_ab) is the sum of d0v⁢d0vsuperscriptsubscript𝑑0𝑣superscriptsubscript𝑑0𝑣d_{0}^{v}d_{0}^{v}𝑑₀^𝑣⁢𝑑₀^𝑣, d1h⁢d1hsuperscriptsubscript𝑑1ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑑1ℎd_{1}^{h}d_{1}^{h}𝑑₁^ℎ⁢𝑑₁^ℎ, and d2h⁢d0vsuperscriptsubscript𝑑2ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑑0𝑣d_{2}^{h}d_{0}^{v}𝑑₂^ℎ⁢𝑑₀^𝑣. Only the third one is nonzero, giving ((a,b),(a′,b′))→a⊗b′→𝑎𝑏superscript𝑎′superscript𝑏′tensor-product𝑎superscript𝑏′((a,b),(a^{\prime},b^{\prime}))\to a\otimes b^{\prime}((𝑎,𝑏),(𝑎',𝑏'))→𝑎⊗𝑏', which equals f𝑓f𝑓 in the construction of the extension (3.1.1). Using (3.4.1) we obtain the conclusion. ∎

The morphism (3.4.2) represents the standard cup product in cohomology. By Proposition  3.4, for an object X𝑋X𝑋 of s⁢𝖢~𝑠superscript𝖢~s\mathsf{C}^{~}𝑠⁢𝖢^~, the cup product

𝐇1⁢(X,A)×𝐇1⁢(X,B)⟶𝐇2⁢(X,A⊗B)⟶superscript𝐇1𝑋𝐴superscript𝐇1𝑋𝐵superscript𝐇2𝑋tensor-product𝐴𝐵\mathbf{H}^{1}(X,A)\times\mathbf{H}^{1}(X,B)\longrightarrow\mathbf{H}^{2}(X,A% \otimes B)𝐇¹⁢(𝑋,𝐴)×𝐇¹⁢(𝑋,𝐵)⟶𝐇²⁢(𝑋,𝐴⊗𝐵)

factors through X→K⁢(A,1)×K⁢(B,1)→𝑋𝐾𝐴1𝐾𝐵1X\to K(A,1)\times K(B,1)𝑋→𝐾⁢(𝐴,1)×𝐾⁢(𝐵,1) and the extension (3.1.1).

Remark.

Proposition 3.4 and the above map provide a more conceptual proof of Theorem 1.4.

4. Examples and connections to prior results

In this section, we collect some examples and briefly indicate the connections with earlier results [3, 10, 30, 31, 33].

4.1. Self-cup products of Poonen-Rains

In [31], Poonen and Rains construct, for any abelian group A𝐴A𝐴, a central extension of the form

0→A⊗A→U⁢A→A→0,→0tensor-product𝐴𝐴→𝑈𝐴→𝐴→00\to A\otimes A\to U\!A\to A\to 0,0→𝐴⊗𝐴→𝑈⁢𝐴→𝐴→0,

providing a functor U:𝖠𝖻→𝖦𝗋𝗉:𝑈→𝖠𝖻𝖦𝗋𝗉U\colon\mathsf{Ab}\to\mathsf{Grp}𝑈:𝖠𝖻→𝖦𝗋𝗉. The group law in U⁢A𝑈𝐴U\!A𝑈⁢𝐴 is obtained from (3.1.2) by setting a=a′𝑎superscript𝑎′a=a^{\prime}𝑎=𝑎' and b=b′𝑏superscript𝑏′b=b^{\prime}𝑏=𝑏'. Hence the above extension can be obtained from (3.1.1) by pulling back along the diagonal homomorphism ΔA:A→A×A:subscriptΔ𝐴→𝐴𝐴𝐴\Delta_{A}\colon A\to A\times AΔ_𝐴:𝐴→𝐴×𝐴. Similarly, both the cocycle and its alternation for the extension constructed in loc. cit. are obtained from ours by pullback along ΔAsubscriptΔ𝐴\Delta_{A}Δ_𝐴, for A∈𝖠𝖻𝐴𝖠𝖻A\in\mathsf{Ab}𝐴∈𝖠𝖻. Similar remarks apply over an abelian sheaf A𝐴A𝐴 on any site 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢. They use U⁢A𝑈𝐴U\!A𝑈⁢𝐴 to describe the self-cup product α∪α𝛼𝛼\alpha\cup\alpha𝛼∪𝛼 of any element α∈H1⁢(A)𝛼superscript𝐻1𝐴\alpha\in H^{1}(A)𝛼∈𝐻¹⁢(𝐴).

4.2. Brylinski’s work on regulators and étale analogues

In [10], Brylinski has proved Theorem 1.4 in the case A=B=𝝁n𝐴𝐵subscript𝝁𝑛A=B=\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n}𝐴=𝐵=𝝁_𝑛, the étale sheaf 𝝁nsubscript𝝁𝑛\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n}𝝁_𝑛 of n𝑛n𝑛th roots of unity on a scheme X𝑋X𝑋 over Spec⁡ℤ⁢[1n]Specℤdelimited-[]1𝑛\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[\frac{1}{n}]Specℤ⁢[1/𝑛] using the Heisenberg group H𝝁n,𝝁nsubscript𝐻subscript𝝁𝑛subscript𝝁𝑛H_{\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n},\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n}}𝐻_{𝝁_𝑛,𝝁_𝑛} (in our notation). The gerbe from Theorem 1.4 in this particular case is related to the Bloch-Deligne line bundle [15, 33], in a sense made precise in [10, Proposition 5.1 and after]. Brylinski has used this special case of Theorem 1.4 to provide a geometric interpretation of certain regulator maps.

4.3. Finite flat group schemes

Let X𝑋X𝑋 be any variety over a perfect field F𝐹F𝐹 of characteristic p>0𝑝0p>0𝑝>0. For any commutative finite flat group scheme N𝑁N𝑁 killed by pnsuperscript𝑝𝑛p^{n}𝑝^𝑛, consider the cup product pairing

H1⁢(X,N)×H1⁢(X,ND)→H2⁢(C,𝝁pn)→superscript𝐻1𝑋𝑁superscript𝐻1𝑋superscript𝑁𝐷superscript𝐻2𝐶subscript𝝁superscript𝑝𝑛H^{1}(X,N)\times H^{1}(X,N^{D})\to H^{2}(C,\boldsymbol{\mu}_{p^{n}})𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝑁)×𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝑁^𝐷)→𝐻²⁢(𝐶,𝝁_𝑝^𝑛)

of flat cohomology groups where NDsuperscript𝑁𝐷N^{D}𝑁^𝐷 is the Cartier dual of N𝑁N𝑁. Theorem 1.4 provides a 𝝁pnsubscript𝝁superscript𝑝𝑛\boldsymbol{\mu}_{p^{n}}𝝁_𝑝^𝑛-gerbe on X𝑋X𝑋 given a N𝑁N𝑁-torsor and a NDsuperscript𝑁𝐷N^{D}𝑁^𝐷-torsor. When N𝑁N𝑁 is the kernel of pnsuperscript𝑝𝑛p^{n}𝑝^𝑛 on an abelian scheme A𝐴A𝐴 so that NDsuperscript𝑁𝐷N^{D}𝑁^𝐷 is the kernel of pnsuperscript𝑝𝑛p^{n}𝑝^𝑛 on the dual abelian scheme ADsuperscript𝐴𝐷A^{D}𝐴^𝐷 of A𝐴A𝐴, the cup-product pairing is related to the Néron-Tate pairing [28, p. 19].

4.4. The gerbe associated with a pair of divisors

Let X𝑋X𝑋 be a smooth variety over a field F𝐹F𝐹. Let D𝐷D𝐷 and D′superscript𝐷′D^{\prime}𝐷' be divisors on X𝑋X𝑋. Consider the non-abelian sheaf H𝐻H𝐻 on X𝑋X𝑋 obtained by pushing the Heisenberg group H𝒦1,𝒦1subscript𝐻subscript𝒦1subscript𝒦1H_{\mathcal{K}_{1},\mathcal{K}_{1}}𝐻_{𝒦₁,𝒦₁} along the multiplication map m:𝒦1⊗𝒦1→𝒦2:𝑚→tensor-productsubscript𝒦1subscript𝒦1subscript𝒦2m:\mathcal{K}_{1}\otimes\mathcal{K}_{1}\to\mathcal{K}_{2}𝑚:𝒦₁⊗𝒦₁→𝒦₂. So H𝐻H𝐻 is a central extension of 𝒦1×K1subscript𝒦1subscript𝐾1\mathcal{K}_{1}\times K_{1}𝒦₁×𝐾₁ by 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂ which we write

(4.4.1) 0⟶𝒦2⟶H⁢⟶𝜋⁢𝒦1×𝒦1⟶0.⟶0subscript𝒦2⟶𝐻𝜋⟶subscript𝒦1subscript𝒦1⟶00\longrightarrow\mathcal{K}_{2}\longrightarrow H\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}% \mathcal{K}_{1}\times\mathcal{K}_{1}\longrightarrow 0.0⟶𝒦₂⟶𝐻⁢⟶┬π⁢𝒦₁×𝒦₁⟶0.

Let L=LD,D′𝐿subscript𝐿𝐷superscript𝐷′L=L_{D,D^{\prime}}𝐿=𝐿_{𝐷,𝐷'} denote the 𝒦1×𝒦1subscript𝒦1subscript𝒦1\mathcal{K}_{1}\times\mathcal{K}_{1}𝒦₁×𝒦₁-torsor defined by the pair D,D′𝐷superscript𝐷′D,D^{\prime}𝐷,𝐷'. Applying Theorem 1.4 gives a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe on X𝑋X𝑋 as follows. Since H𝐻H𝐻 is a central extension (so 𝒦1×𝒦1subscript𝒦1subscript𝒦1\mathcal{K}_{1}\times\mathcal{K}_{1}𝒦₁×𝒦₁ acts trivially on 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂), the category of local liftings of L𝐿L𝐿 to a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-torsor provide (§2.2, [21, IV, 4.2.2]) a canonical 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'}.

Definition 4.1.

The Heisenberg gerbe 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{D,D'} with band 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂ is the following: For each open set U𝑈UU, the category 𝒢D,D′⁢(U)subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′𝑈\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}(U)𝒢_{D,D'}⁢(U) has objects pairs (P,ρ)𝑃𝜌(P,\rho)(P,ρ) where P𝑃PP is a H𝐻HH-torsor on U𝑈UU and

ρ:P×π(𝒦1×𝒦1)⁢⟶∼⁢L:𝜌subscript𝜋𝑃subscript𝒦1subscript𝒦1similar-to⟶𝐿\rho\colon P\times_{\pi}(\mathcal{K}_{1}\times\mathcal{K}_{1})\overset{\sim}{% \longrightarrow}L𝜌:𝑃×_𝜋(𝒦₁×𝒦₁)⁢⟶┬∼⁢𝐿

is an isomorphism of 𝒦1×𝒦1subscript𝒦1subscript𝒦1\mathcal{K}_{1}\times\mathcal{K}_{1}𝒦₁×𝒦₁-torsors; a morphism from (P,ρ)𝑃𝜌(P,\rho)(P,ρ) to (P′,ρ′)superscript𝑃′superscript𝜌′(P^{\prime},\rho^{\prime})(P',ρ') is a map f:P→P′:𝑓→𝑃superscript𝑃′f:P\to P^{\prime}f:P→P' of H𝐻HH-torsors satisfying ρ=ρ′∘f𝜌superscript𝜌′𝑓\rho=\rho^{\prime}\circ fρ=ρ'∘f. It is clear that the set of morphisms from (P,ρ)𝑃𝜌(P,\rho)(P,ρ) to (P′,ρ′)superscript𝑃′superscript𝜌′(P^{\prime},\rho^{\prime})(P',ρ') is a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-torsor.

Example 4.2.

Assume X𝑋X𝑋 is a curve (smooth proper) and put Y=X×X𝑌𝑋𝑋Y=X\times X𝑌=𝑋×𝑋.
