(i) Assume F=𝔽q𝐹subscript𝔽𝑞F=\mathbb{F}_{q}𝐹=𝔽_𝑞 is a finite field. Let D𝐷D𝐷 be the graph on Y𝑌Y𝑌 of the Frobenius morphism π:X→X:𝜋→𝑋𝑋\pi:X\to X𝜋:𝑋→𝑋 and D′superscript𝐷′D^{\prime}𝐷' be the diagonal, the image of X𝑋X𝑋 under the map Δ:X→X×X:Δ→𝑋𝑋𝑋\Delta:X\to X\times XΔ:𝑋→𝑋×𝑋. Theorem 1.4 attaches a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe on Y𝑌Y𝑌 to the zero-cycle D.D′formulae-sequence𝐷superscript𝐷′D.D^{\prime}𝐷.𝐷', the intersection of the divisors D𝐷D𝐷 and D′superscript𝐷′D^{\prime}𝐷'. Since the zero cycle D.D′formulae-sequence𝐷superscript𝐷′D.D^{\prime}𝐷.𝐷' is the pushforward Δ∗⁢βsubscriptΔ𝛽\Delta_{*}\betaΔ_∗⁢𝛽 of β=∑x∈X⁢(𝔽q)x𝛽subscript𝑥𝑋subscript𝔽𝑞𝑥\beta=\displaystyle\sum_{x\in X(\mathbb{F}_{q})}x𝛽=∑┬{𝑥∈𝑋⁢(𝔽_𝑞)}𝑥 on X𝑋X𝑋, we obtain that the set of rational points on X𝑋X𝑋 determines a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe on X×X𝑋𝑋X\times X𝑋×𝑋.

(ii) Note that the diagonal ΔYsubscriptΔ𝑌\Delta_{Y}Δ_𝑌 (a codimension-two cycle on Y×Y𝑌𝑌Y\times Y𝑌×𝑌) can be written as an intersection of divisors V𝑉V𝑉 and V′superscript𝑉′V^{\prime}𝑉' on Y×Y=X×X×X×X𝑌𝑌𝑋𝑋𝑋𝑋Y\times Y=X\times X\times X\times X𝑌×𝑌=𝑋×𝑋×𝑋×𝑋 where V𝑉V𝑉 (resp. V′superscript𝑉′V^{\prime}𝑉') are the set of points of the latter of the form {(a,b,a,c)}𝑎𝑏𝑎𝑐\{(a,b,a,c)\}{(𝑎,𝑏,𝑎,𝑐)} (resp. {(a,b,d,b)}𝑎𝑏𝑑𝑏\{(a,b,d,b)\}{(𝑎,𝑏,𝑑,𝑏)}). Theorem 1.4 says that ΔYsubscriptΔ𝑌\Delta_{Y}Δ_𝑌 determines a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe on Y×Y𝑌𝑌Y\times Y𝑌×𝑌.

4.5. Adjunction formula

Let X𝑋X𝑋 be a smooth proper variety and D𝐷D𝐷 be a smooth divisor of X𝑋X𝑋. The classical adjunction formula states:

The restriction of the line bundle LD−1superscriptsubscript𝐿𝐷1L_{D}^{-1}L_D⁻¹ to D𝐷DD is the conormal bundle NDsubscript𝑁𝐷N_{D}N_D (a line bundle on D𝐷DD).

Given a pair of smooth divisors D,D′𝐷superscript𝐷′D,D^{\prime}𝐷,𝐷' with E=D∩D′𝐸𝐷superscript𝐷′E=D\cap D^{\prime}𝐸=𝐷∩𝐷' smooth of pure codimension two, write ι:E↪X:𝜄↪𝐸𝑋\iota:E\hookrightarrow X𝜄:𝐸↪𝑋 for the inclusion. There is a map π:ι∗⁢𝒦2→𝒦2E:𝜋→superscript𝜄subscript𝒦2superscriptsubscript𝒦2𝐸\pi:\iota^{*}\mathcal{K}_{2}\to\mathcal{K}_{2}^{E}𝜋:𝜄^∗⁢𝒦₂→𝒦₂^𝐸, where 𝒦2Esuperscriptsubscript𝒦2𝐸\mathcal{K}_{2}^{E}𝒦₂^𝐸 indicates the usual K-theory sheaf 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂ on E𝐸E𝐸. An analogue of the adjunction formula for E𝐸E𝐸 would be a description of the 𝒦2Esuperscriptsubscript𝒦2𝐸\mathcal{K}_{2}^{E}𝒦₂^𝐸-gerbe π∗⁢ι∗⁢𝒢D,D′subscript𝜋superscript𝜄subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\pi_{*}\iota^{*}\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝜋_∗⁢𝜄^∗⁢𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} obtained from the 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} on X𝑋X𝑋.

Proposition 4.3.

Let D𝐷DD and D′superscript𝐷′D^{\prime}D' be smooth divisors of X𝑋XX with E=D∩D′𝐸𝐷superscript𝐷′E=D\cap D^{\prime}E=D∩D' smooth of pure codimension two. Consider the line bundles V=(ND)|E𝑉evaluated-atsubscript𝑁𝐷𝐸V=(N_{D})|_{E}V={(N_D)|}_E and V′=(ND′)|Esuperscript𝑉′evaluated-atsubscript𝑁superscript𝐷′𝐸V^{\prime}=(N_{D^{\prime}})|_{E}V'={(N_D')|}_E on E𝐸EE. Then, π∗⁢ι∗⁢𝒢D,D′subscript𝜋superscript𝜄subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\pi_{*}\iota^{*}\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}π_∗⁢ι^∗⁢𝒢_{D,D'} is equivalent to the 𝒦2Esubscriptsuperscript𝒦𝐸2\mathcal{K}^{E}_{2}𝒦ᴱ₂-gerbe 𝒢V,V′subscript𝒢𝑉superscript𝑉′\mathcal{G}_{V,V^{\prime}}𝒢_{V,V'}.

Proof.

Since the restriction map H∗⁢(X,𝒦i)→H∗⁢(E,𝒦iE)→superscript𝐻𝑋subscript𝒦𝑖superscript𝐻𝐸subscriptsuperscript𝒦𝐸𝑖H^{*}(X,\mathcal{K}_{i})\to H^{*}(E,\mathcal{K}^{E}_{i})𝐻^∗⁢(𝑋,𝒦_𝑖)→𝐻^∗⁢(𝐸,𝒦^𝐸_𝑖) respects cup-product, this follows from the classical adjunction formula for D𝐷D𝐷 and D′superscript𝐷′D^{\prime}𝐷'. ∎

4.6. Parshin’s adelic groups

Let S𝑆S𝑆 be a smooth proper surface over a field F𝐹F𝐹. For any choice of a curve C𝐶C𝐶 in S𝑆S𝑆 and a point P𝑃P𝑃 on C𝐶C𝐶, Parshin [30, (18)] has introduced a discrete Heisenberg group

0→ℤ→Γ~P,C→ΓP,C→0,→0ℤ→subscript~Γ𝑃𝐶→subscriptΓ𝑃𝐶→00\to\mathbb{Z}\to\tilde{\Gamma}_{P,C}\to\Gamma_{P,C}\to 0,0→ℤ→Γ̃_{𝑃,𝐶}→Γ_{𝑃,𝐶}→0,

where ΓP,CsubscriptΓ𝑃𝐶\Gamma_{P,C}Γ_{𝑃,𝐶} is isomorphic (non-canonically) to ℤ⊕ℤdirect-sumℤℤ\mathbb{Z}\oplus\mathbb{Z}ℤ⊕ℤ; he has shown [30, end of §3] how a suitable product of these groups leads to an adelic description of C⁢H2⁢(S)𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑆CH^{2}(S)𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑆) and the intersection pairing (1.0.2). His constructions are closely related to an adelic resolution of the sheaf H𝒦1,𝒦1subscript𝐻subscript𝒦1subscript𝒦1H_{\mathcal{K}_{1},\mathcal{K}_{1}}𝐻_{𝒦₁,𝒦₁} on S𝑆S𝑆.

5. Algebraic cycles of codimension two

Throughout this section, X𝑋X𝑋 is a smooth proper variety over a field F𝐹F𝐹. Let η:Spec⁢FX→X:𝜂→Specsubscript𝐹𝑋𝑋\eta\colon\textrm{Spec}~{}F_{X}\to X𝜂:"Spec"⁢𝐹_𝑋→𝑋 be the generic point of X𝑋X𝑋 and write Kiηsuperscriptsubscript𝐾𝑖𝜂K_{i}^{\eta}𝐾_𝑖^𝜂 for the sheaf j∗⁢Ki⁢(FX)subscript𝑗subscript𝐾𝑖subscript𝐹𝑋j_{*}K_{i}(F_{X})𝑗_∗⁢𝐾_𝑖⁢(𝐹_𝑋).

In this section, we construct the Gersten gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 for any codimension two cycle α𝛼\alpha𝛼 on X𝑋X𝑋, provide various equivalent descriptions of 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 and use them to prove Theorems 5.4, 5.10. As a consequence, we obtain Theorems 1.5 and 1.6 of the introduction.

5.1. Bloch-Quillen formula

Recall the (flasque) Gersten resolution999This resolution exists for any separated smooth scheme of finite type over F𝐹F𝐹. [32, §7] [17, p. 276] [20] of the Zariski sheaf 𝒦isubscript𝒦𝑖\mathcal{K}_{i}𝒦_𝑖 associated with the presheaf U↦Ki⁢(U)maps-to𝑈subscript𝐾𝑖𝑈U\mapsto K_{i}(U)𝑈↦𝐾_𝑖⁢(𝑈):

(5.1.1) 0⟶𝒦i⟶⨁x∈X(0)j∗⁢Ki⁢(x)⟶⨁x∈X(1)j∗⁢Ki−1⁢(x)⟶⋯⁢⨁x∈X(i−1)j∗⁢K1⁢(x)→δi−1⨁x∈X(i)j∗⁢K0⁢(x);⟶0subscript𝒦𝑖⟶subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋0subscript𝑗subscript𝐾𝑖𝑥⟶subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋1subscript𝑗subscript𝐾𝑖1𝑥⟶⋯subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋𝑖1subscript𝑗subscript𝐾1𝑥subscript𝛿𝑖1→subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋𝑖subscript𝑗subscript𝐾0𝑥0\longrightarrow\mathcal{K}_{i}\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{x\in X^{(0)}}j_{*}K_{% i}(x)\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{x\in X^{(1)}}j_{*}K_{i-1}(x)\longrightarrow% \cdots\bigoplus_{x\in X^{(i-1)}}j_{*}K_{1}(x)\xrightarrow{\delta_{i-1}}% \bigoplus_{x\in X^{(i)}}~{}j_{*}K_{0}(x);0⟶𝒦_𝑖⟶⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋⁽⁰⁾}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾_𝑖⁢(𝑥)}⟶⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋⁽¹⁾}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾_{𝑖-1}⁢(𝑥)}⟶⋯⁢⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋^(𝑖-1)}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾₁⁢(𝑥)}→┬δ-i1⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋^(𝑖)}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾₀⁢(𝑥)};

here, any point x∈X(m)𝑥superscript𝑋𝑚x\in X^{(m)}𝑥∈𝑋^(𝑚) corresponds to a subvariety of codimension m𝑚m𝑚 and the map j𝑗j𝑗 is the canonical inclusion x↪X↪𝑥𝑋x\hookrightarrow X𝑥↪𝑋. So 𝒦isubscript𝒦𝑖\mathcal{K}_{i}𝒦_𝑖 is quasi-isomorphic to the complex

(5.1.2) GiX=[Kiη⟶⨁x∈X(1)j∗⁢Ki−1⁢(x)⟶⋯⁢⨁x∈X(i−1)j∗⁢K1⁢(x)→δi−1⨁x∈X(i)j∗⁢K0⁢(x)].superscriptsubscript𝐺𝑖𝑋delimited-[]⟶superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑖𝜂subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋1subscript𝑗subscript𝐾𝑖1𝑥⟶⋯subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋𝑖1subscript𝑗subscript𝐾1𝑥subscript𝛿𝑖1→subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋𝑖subscript𝑗subscript𝐾0𝑥G_{i}^{X}=\bigl{[}K_{i}^{\eta}\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{x\in X^{(1)}}j_{*}K_{i% -1}(x)\longrightarrow\cdots\bigoplus_{x\in X^{(i-1)}}j_{*}K_{1}(x)\xrightarrow% {\delta_{i-1}}\bigoplus_{x\in X^{(i)}}j_{*}K_{0}(x)\bigr{]}.𝐺_𝑖^𝑋=[𝐾_𝑖^𝜂⟶⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋⁽¹⁾}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾_{𝑖-1}⁢(𝑥)}⟶⋯⁢⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋^(𝑖-1)}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾₁⁢(𝑥)}→┬δ-i1⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋^(𝑖)}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾₀⁢(𝑥)}].

By (5.1.1), there is a functorial isomorphism [32, §7, Theorem 5.19] [17, Corollary 72, p. 276]

(5.1.3) ⨁iC⁢Hi⁢(X)→∼⨁iHi⁢(X,𝒦i);(Bloch-Quillen formula)similar-to→subscriptdirect-sum𝑖𝐶superscript𝐻𝑖𝑋subscriptdirect-sum𝑖superscript𝐻𝑖𝑋subscript𝒦𝑖(Bloch-Quillen formula)\bigoplus_{i}CH^{i}(X)\xrightarrow{\sim}\bigoplus_{i}H^{i}(X,\mathcal{K}_{i});% \qquad\text{\makebox[0.0pt][l]{(Bloch-Quillen formula)}}⨁┬𝑖{𝐶⁢𝐻^𝑖⁢(𝑋)}→┬∼{⨁┬𝑖{𝐻^𝑖⁢(𝑋,𝒦_𝑖)};"(Bloch-Quillen formula)"}

this is an isomorphism of graded rings: D. Grayson has proved that the intersection product on C⁢H⁢(X)=⊕iC⁢Hi⁢(X)𝐶𝐻𝑋subscriptdirect-sum𝑖𝐶superscript𝐻𝑖𝑋CH(X)=\oplus_{i}CH^{i}(X)𝐶⁢𝐻⁢(𝑋)=⊕_𝑖𝐶⁢𝐻^𝑖⁢(𝑋) corresponds to the cup-product in cohomology [17, Theorem 77, p.278]. Thus, algebraic cycles of codimension n𝑛n𝑛 give n𝑛n𝑛-cocycles of the sheaf 𝒦nsubscript𝒦𝑛\mathcal{K}_{n}𝒦_𝑛 on X𝑋X𝑋 and that two such cocycles are cohomologous exactly when the algebraic cycles are rationally equivalent.

The final two maps in (5.1.1) arise essentially from the valuation and the tame symbol map [3, pp.351-2]. Let R𝑅R𝑅 be a discrete valuation ring, with fraction field L𝐿L𝐿; let ord:L×→ℤ:ord→superscript𝐿ℤ{\rm ord}:L^{\times}\to\mathbb{Z}ord:𝐿^×→ℤ be the valuation and let l𝑙l𝑙 be the residue field. The boundary maps from the localization sequence for Spec⁡RSpec𝑅\operatorname{Spec}RSpec𝑅 are known explicitly: the map L×=K1⁢(L)→K0⁢(l)=ℤsuperscript𝐿subscript𝐾1𝐿→subscript𝐾0𝑙ℤL^{\times}=K_{1}(L)\to K_{0}(l)=\mathbb{Z}𝐿^×=𝐾₁⁢(𝐿)→𝐾₀⁢(𝑙)=ℤ is the map ordord{\rm ord}ord and the map K2⁢(L)→K1⁢(l)=l×→subscript𝐾2𝐿subscript𝐾1𝑙superscript𝑙K_{2}(L)\to K_{1}(l)=l^{\times}𝐾₂⁢(𝐿)→𝐾₁⁢(𝑙)=𝑙^× is the tame symbol. This applies for any normal subvariety V𝑉V𝑉 (corresponds to a y∈X(i)𝑦superscript𝑋𝑖y\in X^{(i)}𝑦∈𝑋^(𝑖)) and a divisor x𝑥x𝑥 of V𝑉V𝑉 (corresponding to a x∈X(i+1)𝑥superscript𝑋𝑖1x\in X^{(i+1)}𝑥∈𝑋^(𝑖+1)).

5.2. Divisors

We recall certain well known results about divisors and line bundles for comparison with the results below for the 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbes attached to codimension two cycles. See [23, Chapter II §6] and [17, Vol. 1, II.2] for details.

If A𝐴A𝐴 is a sheaf of abelian groups on X𝑋X𝑋, then ExtX1⁢(ℤ,A)=H1⁢(X,A)superscriptsubscriptExt𝑋1ℤ𝐴superscript𝐻1𝑋𝐴{\rm Ext}_{X}^{1}(\mathbb{Z},A)=H^{1}(X,A)Ext_𝑋¹⁢(ℤ,𝐴)=𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐴) classifies A𝐴A𝐴-torsors on X𝑋X𝑋. Given an extension E𝐸E𝐸

0⟶A⟶E⁢⟶𝜋⁢ℤ⟶0⟶0𝐴⟶𝐸𝜋⟶ℤ⟶00\longrightarrow A\longrightarrow E\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\mathbb{Z}\longrightarrow 00⟶𝐴⟶𝐸⁢⟶┬π⁢ℤ⟶0

of abelian sheaves on X𝑋X𝑋, the corresponding A𝐴A𝐴-torsor is simply π−1⁢(1)superscript𝜋11\pi^{-1}(1)𝜋⁻¹⁢(1) (a sheaf of sets). When X𝑋X𝑋 is a point, then π−1⁢(1)superscript𝜋11\pi^{-1}(1)𝜋⁻¹⁢(1) is a coset of π−1⁢(0)=Asuperscript𝜋10𝐴\pi^{-1}(0)=A𝜋⁻¹⁢(0)=𝐴, i.e., a A𝐴A𝐴-torsor. The classical correspondence [23] between Weil divisors (codimension-one algebraic cycles) D𝐷D𝐷 on X𝑋X𝑋, Cartier divisors, line bundles ℒDsubscriptℒ𝐷\mathcal{L}_{D}ℒ_𝐷, and torsors LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷 over 𝒪X∗=𝔾m=𝒦1superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑋subscript𝔾𝑚subscript𝒦1\mathcal{O}_{X}^{*}=\mathbb{G}_{m}=\mathcal{K}_{1}𝒪_𝑋^∗=𝔾_𝑚=𝒦₁ comes from the Gersten sequence (5.1.1) for 𝒦1subscript𝒦1\mathcal{K}_{1}𝒦₁ (see also [20, 2.2]):

(5.2.1) 0⟶𝒪X∗⟶FX×⁢⟶𝑑⁢⨁x∈X(1)j∗⁢ℤ→0,⟶0superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑋⟶superscriptsubscript𝐹𝑋𝑑⟶subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋1subscript𝑗ℤ→00\longrightarrow\mathcal{O}_{X}^{*}\longrightarrow F_{X}^{\times}\overset{d}{% \longrightarrow}\bigoplus_{x\in X^{(1)}}j_{*}\mathbb{Z}\to 0,0⟶𝒪_𝑋^∗⟶𝐹_𝑋^×⁢⟶┬d⁢⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋⁽¹⁾}{𝑗_∗⁢ℤ}→0,

where FXsubscript𝐹𝑋F_{X}𝐹_𝑋 is the constant sheaf of rational functions on X𝑋X𝑋 and the sum is over all irreducible effective divisors on X𝑋X𝑋, using that K0⁢(L)≅ℤsubscript𝐾0𝐿ℤK_{0}(L)\cong\mathbb{Z}𝐾₀⁢(𝐿)≅ℤ and K1⁢(L)=L×subscript𝐾1𝐿superscript𝐿K_{1}(L)=L^{\times}𝐾₁⁢(𝐿)=𝐿^× for any field L𝐿L𝐿.

We recall that the categories of line bundles and 𝒪X∗superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑋\mathcal{O}_{X}^{*}𝒪_𝑋^∗-torsors are equivalent: given a line bundle ℒDsubscriptℒ𝐷\mathcal{L}_{D}ℒ_𝐷, the 𝒪X∗superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑋\mathcal{O}_{X}^{*}𝒪_𝑋^∗-torsor LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷 is the complement of the zero section of the line bundle. The functor LD↦ℒDmaps-tosubscript𝐿𝐷subscriptℒ𝐷L_{D}\mapsto\mathcal{L}_{D}𝐿_𝐷↦ℒ_𝐷 is induced by the natural action of 𝔾msubscript𝔾𝑚\mathbb{G}_{m}𝔾_𝑚 on the affine line 𝔸1superscript𝔸1\mathbb{A}^{1}𝔸¹. Pushing the principal 𝒪X∗superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑋\mathcal{O}_{X}^{*}𝒪_𝑋^∗-torsor along this action gives an 𝔸1superscript𝔸1\mathbb{A}^{1}𝔸¹-bundle.

As a Weil divisor D=Σx∈X1⁢nx⁢x𝐷subscriptΣ𝑥superscript𝑋1subscript𝑛𝑥𝑥D=\Sigma_{x\in X^{1}}~{}n_{x}x𝐷=Σ_{𝑥∈𝑋¹}⁢𝑛_𝑥⁢𝑥 is a formal combination with integer coefficients of subvarieties of codimension one of X𝑋X𝑋, it determines a map of sheaves

ψ:ℤ⟶⨁x∈X(1)j∗⁢ℤ;:𝜓⟶ℤsubscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋1subscript𝑗ℤ\psi\colon\mathbb{Z}\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{x\in X^{(1)}}j_{*}\mathbb{Z};𝜓:ℤ⟶⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋⁽¹⁾}{𝑗_∗⁢ℤ};

ψ⁢(1)𝜓1\psi(1)𝜓⁢(1) is the section with components nxsubscript𝑛𝑥n_{x}𝑛_𝑥. The 𝒪X∗superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑋\mathcal{O}_{X}^{*}𝒪_𝑋^∗-torsor LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷 attached to D𝐷D𝐷 is given as the subset

(5.2.2) d−1⁢(ψ⁢(1))⊂FX×.superscript𝑑1𝜓1superscriptsubscript𝐹𝑋{d}^{-1}(\psi(1))\subset F_{X}^{\times}.𝑑⁻¹⁢(𝜓⁢(1))⊂𝐹_𝑋^×.

A Čech description of LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷 relative to an Zariski open cover {Ui}subscript𝑈𝑖\{U_{i}\}{𝑈_𝑖} of X𝑋X𝑋 is as follows. Pick a rational function fisubscript𝑓𝑖f_{i}𝑓_𝑖 on Uisubscript𝑈𝑖U_{i}𝑈_𝑖 with pole of order nxsubscript𝑛𝑥n_{x}𝑛_𝑥 along x𝑥x𝑥 for all x∈Ui(1)𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝑈1𝑖x\in U^{(1)}_{i}𝑥∈𝑈⁽¹⁾_𝑖 (so x𝑥x𝑥 is a irreducible subvariety of codimension one of Uisubscript𝑈𝑖U_{i}𝑈_𝑖); we view fi∈FX×subscript𝑓𝑖superscriptsubscript𝐹𝑋f_{i}\in F_{X}^{\times}𝑓_𝑖∈𝐹_𝑋^×. On Ui∩Ujsubscript𝑈𝑖subscript𝑈𝑗U_{i}\cap U_{j}𝑈_𝑖∩𝑈_𝑗, one has fi=gi⁢j⁢fjsubscript𝑓𝑖subscript𝑔𝑖𝑗subscript𝑓𝑗f_{i}=g_{ij}f_{j}𝑓_𝑖=𝑔_{𝑖⁢𝑗}⁢𝑓_𝑗 for unique gi⁢j∈𝒪X∗⁢(Ui∩Uj)subscript𝑔𝑖𝑗superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑋subscript𝑈𝑖subscript𝑈𝑗g_{ij}\in\mathcal{O}_{X}^{*}(U_{i}\cap U_{j})𝑔_{𝑖⁢𝑗}∈𝒪_𝑋^∗⁢(𝑈_𝑖∩𝑈_𝑗); the collection {gi⁢j}subscript𝑔𝑖𝑗\{g_{ij}\}{𝑔_{𝑖⁢𝑗}} is a Čech one-cocycle with values in 𝒪X∗superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑋\mathcal{O}_{X}^{*}𝒪_𝑋^∗ representing LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷.

For any D𝐷D𝐷, ℒDsubscriptℒ𝐷\mathcal{L}_{D}ℒ_𝐷 (and LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷) is trivial on the complement of the support of D𝐷D𝐷.

Remark 5.1.

For each open U𝑈U𝑈 of X𝑋X𝑋, one has the Picard category TorsU⁡(𝒪∗)subscriptTors𝑈superscript𝒪\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}_{U}(\mathcal{O}^{*})"Tors"_𝑈(𝒪^∗) of 𝒪∗superscript𝒪\mathcal{O}^{*}𝒪^∗-torsors on U𝑈U𝑈. These combine to the Picard stack Tors⁡(𝒪∗)Torssuperscript𝒪\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(\mathcal{O}^{*})"Tors"(𝒪^∗) of 𝒪∗superscript𝒪\mathcal{O}^{*}𝒪^∗-torsors on X𝑋X𝑋. The Gersten sequence incarnates this Picard stack [12, 1.10]. ∎

5.3. The Gersten gerbe of a codimension two cycle

Our next task is to show that every cycle α𝛼\alpha𝛼 of codimension two on X𝑋X𝑋 determines a gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 with band 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂. The Gersten complex (5.1.1) enables us to give a geometric description of 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼; see Remark 5.5 below.

First, let us make the map in (5.1.3) explicit for codimension-two cycles by showing how a codimension-two cycle determines a class in H2⁢(X,𝒦2)superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂). The cycle α𝛼\alpha𝛼 can be viewed as an element 𝟙αsubscript1𝛼\mathbbm{1}_{\alpha}𝟙_𝛼 of H0⁢(X,⨁x∈X(2)j∗⁢K0⁢(x))superscript𝐻0𝑋subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋2subscript𝑗subscript𝐾0𝑥H^{0}(X,\bigoplus\limits_{x\in X^{(2)}}j_{*}K_{0}(x))𝐻⁰⁢(𝑋,⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋⁽²⁾}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾₀⁢(𝑥)}). Writing α=∑xnx⁢[x]𝛼subscript𝑥subscript𝑛𝑥delimited-[]𝑥\alpha=\sum_{x}n_{x}[x]𝛼=∑_𝑥{𝑛_𝑥⁢[𝑥]} as a sum over x∈X(2)𝑥superscript𝑋2x\in X^{(2)}𝑥∈𝑋⁽²⁾ (irreducible codimension two subvarieties), then the x𝑥x𝑥-component of 𝟙αsubscript1𝛼\mathbbm{1}_{\alpha}𝟙_𝛼 corresponds to nxsubscript𝑛𝑥n_{x}𝑛_𝑥 under the canonical isomorphism K0⁢(x)≅ℤsubscript𝐾0𝑥ℤK_{0}(x)\cong\mathbb{Z}𝐾₀⁢(𝑥)≅ℤ. This provides a natural map ϕitalic-ϕ\phiϕ determined by ϕ⁢(1)=𝟙αitalic-ϕ1subscript1𝛼\phi(1)={\mathbbm{1}}_{\alpha}ϕ⁢(1)=𝟙_𝛼:

(5.3.1)

and an exact sequence (by pullback)

(5.3.2) 0⟶𝒦2⟶K2η⁢⟶𝜈⁢T⁢⟶𝛿⁢ℤ⟶0.⟶0subscript𝒦2⟶superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂𝜈⟶𝑇𝛿⟶ℤ⟶00\longrightarrow\mathcal{K}_{2}\longrightarrow K_{2}^{\eta}\overset{\nu}{% \longrightarrow}T\overset{\delta}{\longrightarrow}\mathbb{Z}\longrightarrow 0.0⟶𝒦₂⟶𝐾₂^𝜂⁢⟶┬ν⁢𝑇⁢⟶┬δ⁢ℤ⟶0.

This two-extension of ℤℤ\mathbb{Z}ℤ by 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂ gives a class in Ext2⁡(ℤ,𝒦2)=H2⁢(X,𝒦2)superscriptExt2ℤsubscript𝒦2superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2\operatorname{Ext}^{2}(\mathbb{Z},\mathcal{K}_{2})=H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})Ext²(ℤ,𝒦₂)=𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂) and so a map Z2⁢(X)→H2⁢(X,𝒦2)→superscript𝑍2𝑋superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2Z^{2}(X)\to H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝑍²⁢(𝑋)→𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂).

The maps δ𝛿\delta𝛿 and ν𝜈\nu𝜈 are essentially given by the valuation (or ordord{\rm ord}ord) and tame symbol maps; see §5.1.

Since the Gersten sequence

is a four-term complex, one can apply the results of §2.3, specifically, Corollary 2.4 to it

Definition 5.2.

For any codimension-two cycle α𝛼\alphaα on X𝑋XX, the gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_α is the gerbe ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒᵦ of Corollary 2.4 from §2.3 applied to the four-term Gersten sequence (5.3.1) - see above - with β=ϕ⁢(1)=𝟙α𝛽italic-ϕ1subscript1𝛼\beta=\phi(1)=\mathbbm{1}_{\alpha}β=ϕ⁢(1)=𝟙_α; and ℒℒ\mathcal{L}ℒ is the Picard stack associated to the complex [K2η→⨁x∈X(1)j∗⁢K1⁢(x)]delimited-[]→superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋1subscript𝑗subscript𝐾1𝑥[K_{2}^{\eta}\to\bigoplus\limits_{x\in X^{(1)}}j_{*}K_{1}(x)][K₂^η→⨁┬{x∈X⁽¹⁾}{j_∗⁢K₁⁢(x)}].

Remark 5.3.

Corollary 2.4 provides two descriptions of ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽 (and so of 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼). It should be emphasized that both descriptions are useful. One description of 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 is the succinct description given in Remark 5.5. The other explicit description, crucial for the comparison with the Heisenberg gerbe (Theorem 5.10), consists of the following main points:

  1. (i)

    For any open set U𝑈U𝑈 of X𝑋X𝑋, the category 𝒞α⁢(U)subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈) has objects u∈⨁x∈X(1)j∗⁢K1⁢(x)𝑢subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋1subscript𝑗subscript𝐾1𝑥u\in\bigoplus\limits_{x\in X^{(1)}}j_{*}K_{1}(x)𝑢∈⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋⁽¹⁾}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾₁⁢(𝑥)} with δ⁢u=ϕ⁢(1)𝛿𝑢italic-ϕ1\delta{u}=\phi(1)𝛿⁢𝑢=ϕ⁢(1) and morphisms from u𝑢u𝑢 to u′superscript𝑢′u^{\prime}𝑢' are elements a∈K2η𝑎superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂a\in K_{2}^{\eta}𝑎∈𝐾₂^𝜂 with ν⁢(a)=u′−u𝜈𝑎superscript𝑢′𝑢\nu(a)=u^{\prime}-u𝜈⁢(𝑎)=𝑢'-𝑢.

  2. (ii)

    Any Hom-set Hom𝒞α⁢(u,u′)subscriptHomsubscript𝒞𝛼𝑢superscript𝑢′{\rm Hom}_{\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}}(u,u^{\prime})Hom_𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑢,𝑢') is a K2⁢(U)subscript𝐾2𝑈K_{2}(U)𝐾₂⁢(𝑈)-torsor.

  3. (iii)

    The category 𝒞α⁢(U)subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈) can be described geometrically in terms of the ordord{\rm ord}ord and tame maps. For instance, let X𝑋X𝑋 be a surface. Write the zero-cycle α𝛼\alpha𝛼 as a finite sum ∑i∈Ini⁢xisubscript𝑖𝐼subscript𝑛𝑖subscript𝑥𝑖\sum_{i\in I}n_{i}x_{i}∑_{𝑖∈𝐼}{𝑛_𝑖⁢𝑥_𝑖} of points xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i}𝑥_𝑖 of X𝑋X𝑋. We assume ni≠0subscript𝑛𝑖0n_{i}\neq 0𝑛_𝑖≠0 and write V𝑉V𝑉 for the complement of the support of α𝛼\alpha𝛼. Any non-zero rational function f𝑓f𝑓 on a curve C𝐶C𝐶 defines an object of 𝒞α⁢(U)subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈) if f𝑓f𝑓 is invertible on C∩U∩V𝐶𝑈𝑉C\cap U\cap V𝐶∩𝑈∩𝑉 and satisfies ordxi⁢f=nisubscriptordsubscript𝑥𝑖𝑓subscript𝑛𝑖{\rm ord}_{x_{i}}f=n_{i}ord_𝑥_𝑖⁢𝑓=𝑛_𝑖 for each xi∈Usubscript𝑥𝑖𝑈x_{i}\in U𝑥_𝑖∈𝑈 (assuming, for simplicity, that xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i}𝑥_𝑖 is a smooth point of C𝐶C𝐶). A general object of 𝒞α⁢(U)subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈) is a finite collection u={Cj,fj}𝑢subscript𝐶𝑗subscript𝑓𝑗u=\{C_{j},f_{j}\}𝑢={𝐶_𝑗,𝑓_𝑗} of curves Cjsubscript𝐶𝑗C_{j}𝐶_𝑗 and non-zero rational functions fjsubscript𝑓𝑗f_{j}𝑓_𝑗 on Cjsubscript𝐶𝑗C_{j}𝐶_𝑗 such that fjsubscript𝑓𝑗f_{j}𝑓_𝑗 is invertible on Cj∩U∩Vsubscript𝐶𝑗𝑈𝑉C_{j}\cap U\cap V𝐶_𝑗∩𝑈∩𝑉 and ∑ordxi⁢fj=nisubscriptordsubscript𝑥𝑖subscript𝑓𝑗subscript𝑛𝑖\sum{\rm ord}_{x_{i}}f_{j}=n_{i}∑{ord_𝑥_𝑖⁢𝑓_𝑗}=𝑛_𝑖 (an index j𝑗j𝑗 occurs in the sum if xi∈Cjsubscript𝑥𝑖subscript𝐶𝑗x_{i}\in C_{j}𝑥_𝑖∈𝐶_𝑗) for each xi∈Usubscript𝑥𝑖𝑈x_{i}\in U𝑥_𝑖∈𝑈. A morphism from u𝑢u𝑢 to u′superscript𝑢′u^{\prime}𝑢' is an element a∈K2η𝑎superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂a\in K_{2}^{\eta}𝑎∈𝐾₂^𝜂 whose tame symbol is u′−usuperscript𝑢′𝑢u^{\prime}-u𝑢'-𝑢. ∎

Theorem 5.4.

(i) 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_α is a gerbe on X𝑋XX with band 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂.

(ii) Under (5.1.3), the class of 𝒞α∈H2⁢(X,𝒦2)subscript𝒞𝛼superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}\in H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝒞_α∈H²⁢(X,𝒦₂) corresponds to α∈C⁢H2⁢(X)𝛼𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋\alpha\in CH^{2}(X)α∈C⁢H²⁢(X).

(iii) 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_α is equivalent to 𝒞α′subscript𝒞superscript𝛼′\mathcal{C}_{\alpha^{\prime}}𝒞_α' (as gerbes) if and only if the cycles α𝛼\alphaα and α′superscript𝛼′\alpha^{\prime}α' are rationally equivalent.

Proof.

(i) The Gersten sequence (5.3.1) is an example of a four-term complex, discussed in §2.3. As the stack 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 is a special case of the gerbe ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽 constructed in §2.3, (i) is obvious.

In more detail: We first observe that (5.3.2) provides a quasi-isomorphism between 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂ (sheaf) and the complex (concentrated in degree zero and one)

(5.3.3) η:𝒦2→[K2η→𝜈K⁢e⁢r⁢(δ)].:𝜂→subscript𝒦2delimited-[]𝜈→superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂𝐾𝑒𝑟𝛿\eta:\mathcal{K}_{2}\to[K_{2}^{\eta}\xrightarrow{\nu}Ker(\delta)].𝜂:𝒦₂→[𝐾₂^𝜂→┬ν𝐾⁢𝑒⁢𝑟⁢(𝛿)].

Now, suppose U𝑈U𝑈 is disjoint from the support of α𝛼\alpha𝛼. On such an open set U𝑈U𝑈, the map ϕitalic-ϕ\phiϕ is zero. This means that the objects u𝑢u𝑢 of the category 𝒞α⁢(U)subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈) are elements of Ker⁢(δ)Ker𝛿{\rm Ker}(\delta)Ker⁢(𝛿). The gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼, when restricted to U𝑈U𝑈, is equivalent to the Picard stack of 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-torsors [1, Expose XVIII, 1.4.15]: in the complex [K2η→𝜈Ker⁢(δ)]delimited-[]𝜈→superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂Ker𝛿[K_{2}^{\eta}\xrightarrow{\nu}{\rm Ker}(\delta)][𝐾₂^𝜂→┬νKer⁢(𝛿)], one has Coker⁢(ν)=0Coker𝜈0{\rm Coker}(\nu)=0Coker⁢(𝜈)=0 and Ker⁢(ν)=𝒦2|UKer𝜈evaluated-atsubscript𝒦2𝑈{\rm Ker}(\nu)=\mathcal{K}_{2}|_{U}Ker⁢(𝜈)={𝒦₂|}_𝑈. Since for any abelian sheaf G𝐺G𝐺, the category Tors⁡(G)Tors𝐺\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(G)"Tors"(𝐺) is the trivial G𝐺G𝐺-gerbe, 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 is the trivial gerbe with band 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂ on the complement of the support of α𝛼\alpha𝛼.

Now, consider an arbitrary open set V𝑉V𝑉 of X𝑋X𝑋. By the exactness of (5.3.2), there is an open covering {Ui}subscript𝑈𝑖\{U_{i}\}{𝑈_𝑖} of V𝑉V𝑉 and sections ui∈T⁢(Ui)subscript𝑢𝑖𝑇subscript𝑈𝑖u_{i}\in T(U_{i})𝑢_𝑖∈𝑇⁢(𝑈_𝑖) with ti=ϕ⁢(1)subscript𝑡𝑖italic-ϕ1t_{i}=\phi(1)𝑡_𝑖=ϕ⁢(1). Fix i𝑖i𝑖 and let U𝑈U𝑈 be an open set contained in Uisubscript𝑈𝑖U_{i}𝑈_𝑖. Then the category 𝒞α⁢(U)subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈) is non-empty. The category D𝐷D𝐷 with objects d∈Ker⁢(δ)⊂T⁢(U)𝑑Ker𝛿𝑇𝑈d\in{\rm Ker}(\delta)\subset T(U)𝑑∈Ker⁢(𝛿)⊂𝑇⁢(𝑈) and morphisms HomD⁢(d,d′)=subscriptHom𝐷𝑑superscript𝑑′absent\mathrm{Hom}_{D}(d,d^{\prime})=Hom_𝐷⁢(𝑑,𝑑')= elements a∈K2η𝑎superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂a\in K_{2}^{\eta}𝑎∈𝐾₂^𝜂 with ν⁢(a)=d′−d𝜈𝑎superscript𝑑′𝑑\nu(a)=d^{\prime}-d𝜈⁢(𝑎)=𝑑'-𝑑. The category D𝐷D𝐷 is clearly equivalent to the category of K2⁢(U)subscript𝐾2𝑈K_{2}(U)𝐾₂⁢(𝑈)-torsors. The functor which sends d𝑑d𝑑 to d+ui𝑑subscript𝑢𝑖d+u_{i}𝑑+𝑢_𝑖 is easily seen to be an equivalence of categories between D𝐷D𝐷 and 𝒞α⁢(U)subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈). Thus 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 is a gerbe with band 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂.

(ii) The Bloch-Quillen formula (5.1.3) arises from the canonical map

d2:Z2⁢(X)→H2⁢(X,𝒦2):superscript𝑑2→superscript𝑍2𝑋superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2d^{2}:Z^{2}(X)\to H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝑑²:𝑍²⁢(𝑋)→𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂)

of Lemma 2.3 applied to the four-term complex (5.3.1). As 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 is a gerbe of the form ℒβsubscriptℒ𝛽\mathcal{L}_{\beta}ℒ_𝛽, (ii) follows from Lemma 2.3.

(iii) This is a simple consequence of the Bloch-Quillen formula (5.1.3). ∎

Remark 5.5.

(i) Split the sequence (5.3.1) into

0⟶𝒦2⟶K2η⟶K2η/𝒦2⟶0⟶0subscript𝒦2⟶superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂⟶superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂subscript𝒦2⟶00\longrightarrow\mathcal{K}_{2}\longrightarrow K_{2}^{\eta}\longrightarrow K_{% 2}^{\eta}/{\mathcal{K}_{2}}\longrightarrow 00⟶𝒦₂⟶𝐾₂^𝜂⟶𝐾₂^𝜂/𝒦₂⟶0

and

0⟶K2η/𝒦2⟶⨁x∈X(1)j∗⁢K1⁢(x)⟶⨁x∈X(2)j∗⁢K0⁢(x)⟶0.⟶0superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂subscript𝒦2⟶subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋1subscript𝑗subscript𝐾1𝑥⟶subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋2subscript𝑗subscript𝐾0𝑥⟶00\longrightarrow K_{2}^{\eta}/{\mathcal{K}_{2}}\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{x\in X% ^{(1)}}j_{*}K_{1}(x)\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{x\in X^{(2)}}j_{*}K_{0}(x)% \longrightarrow 0.0⟶𝐾₂^𝜂/𝒦₂⟶⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋⁽¹⁾}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾₁⁢(𝑥)}⟶⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋⁽²⁾}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾₀⁢(𝑥)}⟶0.

Since the Gersten resolution is by flasque sheaves, one has H1⁢(X,K2η/𝒦2)→∼H2⁢(X,𝒦2)similar-to→superscript𝐻1𝑋superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂subscript𝒦2superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2H^{1}(X,K_{2}^{\eta}/{\mathcal{K}_{2}})\xrightarrow{\sim}H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{% 2})𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐾₂^𝜂/𝒦₂)→┬∼𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂). Just as Cartier divisors are elements of H0⁢(X,K1η/𝒦1)superscript𝐻0𝑋superscriptsubscript𝐾1𝜂subscript𝒦1H^{0}(X,K_{1}^{\eta}/{\mathcal{K}_{1}})𝐻⁰⁢(𝑋,𝐾₁^𝜂/𝒦₁), we will now view elements of H1⁢(X,K2η/𝒦2)superscript𝐻1𝑋superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂subscript𝒦2H^{1}(X,K_{2}^{\eta}/{\mathcal{K}_{2}})𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐾₂^𝜂/𝒦₂) as Cartier cycles of codimension two. The map

Z2⁢(X)=H0⁢(X,⨁x∈X(2)j∗⁢K0⁢(x))→H1⁢(X,K2η/𝒦2)superscript𝑍2𝑋superscript𝐻0𝑋subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋2subscript𝑗subscript𝐾0𝑥→superscript𝐻1𝑋superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂subscript𝒦2Z^{2}(X)=H^{0}(X,\bigoplus_{x\in X^{(2)}}j_{*}K_{0}(x)~{})\to H^{1}(X,K_{2}^{% \eta}/{\mathcal{K}_{2}})𝑍²⁢(𝑋)=𝐻⁰⁢(𝑋,⨁┬{𝑥∈𝑋⁽²⁾}{𝑗_∗⁢𝐾₀⁢(𝑥)})→𝐻¹⁢(𝑋,𝐾₂^𝜂/𝒦₂)

attaches to any cycle its Cartier cycle. Lemma 2.4 provides the following succinct description of 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼:

it is the gerbe of liftings (to a K2ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂K_{2}^{\eta}K₂^η-torsor) of the (K2η/𝒦2)superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂subscript𝒦2(K_{2}^{\eta}/{\mathcal{K}_{2}})(K₂^η/𝒦₂)-torsor determined by α𝛼\alphaα.

(iii) The proof of Theorem 5.4 provides a canonical trivialization101010This uses (5.3.3). ηαsubscript𝜂𝛼\eta_{\alpha}𝜂_𝛼 of the gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 on the complement of the support of α𝛼\alpha𝛼.

(iv) The pushforward of 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 along 𝒦2→Ω2→subscript𝒦2superscriptΩ2\mathcal{K}_{2}\to\Omega^{2}𝒦₂→Ω² produces a Ω2superscriptΩ2\Omega^{2}Ω²-gerbe which manifests the cycle class of α𝛼\alpha𝛼 in de Rham cohomology H2⁢(X,Ω2)superscript𝐻2𝑋superscriptΩ2H^{2}(X,\Omega^{2})𝐻²⁢(𝑋,Ω²). If α𝛼\alpha𝛼 is homologically equivalent to zero, then this latter gerbe is trivial, i.e., it is the Picard stack of Ω2superscriptΩ2\Omega^{2}Ω²-torsors. ∎

Remark 5.6.

It may be instructive to compare the 𝔾msubscript𝔾𝑚\mathbb{G}_{m}𝔾_𝑚-torsor LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷 attached to a divisor D𝐷D𝐷 of X𝑋X𝑋 and the 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 attached to a codimension-two cycle. Let U𝑈U𝑈 be any open set of X𝑋X𝑋. This goes, roughly speaking, as follows.

  • •

    LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷: The set of divisors on U𝑈U𝑈 rationally equivalent to zero is exactly the image of d𝑑d𝑑 over U𝑈U𝑈 in (5.2.1). So, the set of sections of LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷 over U𝑈U𝑈 is non-empty if D=0𝐷0D=0𝐷=0 in C⁢H1⁢(U)𝐶superscript𝐻1𝑈CH^{1}(U)𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝑈). The sections of LDsubscript𝐿𝐷L_{D}𝐿_𝐷 over U𝑈U𝑈 are given by rational functions f𝑓f𝑓 on U𝑈U𝑈 whose divisor is D|Uevaluated-at𝐷𝑈D|_{U}{𝐷|}_𝑈. In other words, the sections are rational equivalences between the divisor D𝐷D𝐷 and the empty divisor. The set LD⁢(U)subscript𝐿𝐷𝑈L_{D}(U)𝐿_𝐷⁢(𝑈) is a torsor over 𝔾m⁢(U)subscript𝔾𝑚𝑈\mathbb{G}_{m}(U)𝔾_𝑚⁢(𝑈).

  • •

    𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼: We observe that the image of δ𝛿\delta𝛿 in (5.3.1) consists of codimension-two cycles rationally equivalent to zero. So 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 is non-empty if α=0𝛼0\alpha=0𝛼=0 in C⁢H2⁢(U)𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑈CH^{2}(U)𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑈). Each rational equivalence between α𝛼\alpha𝛼 and the empty codimension-two cycle gives an object of 𝒞α⁢(U)subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈). The sheaf of morphisms between two objects is a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-torsor. ∎

The Bloch-Quillen formula (5.1.3) states that equivalence classes of 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbes are in bijection with codimension-two cycles (modulo rational equivalence) on X𝑋X𝑋. We have seen that a codimension-two cycle determines a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe (an actual gerbe, not just one up to equivalence). It is natural to ask whether the converse holds: (see Proposition 5.8 in this regard)

Question 5.7.

Does a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe on X𝑋XX determine an actual codimension-two cycle?

Since the analogous question in codimension one has a negative answer (a 𝔾msubscript𝔾𝑚\mathbb{G}_{m}𝔾_𝑚-torsor does not determine a codimension-one cycle), one expects a negative answer to Question 5.7. A proof that the answer is negative can be based on the non-existence of a canonical intersection of Weil divisors: Consider the 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} attached to a pair of divisors D,D′𝐷superscript𝐷′D,D^{\prime}𝐷,𝐷' on X𝑋X𝑋. If 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} determines an actual codimension-two cycle, then any pair D,D′𝐷superscript𝐷′D,D^{\prime}𝐷,𝐷' of divisors determines a canonical codimension-two cycle on X𝑋X𝑋 which contradicts the non-existence of a canonical intersection of Weil divisors.

5.4. Gerbes and cohomology with support

Let F𝐹F𝐹 be an abelian sheaf on a site 𝖢𝖢\mathsf{C}𝖢. Recall that (see e.g. [29, §5.1]) H1⁢(F)superscript𝐻1𝐹H^{1}(F)𝐻¹⁢(𝐹) is the set of isomorphism classes of auto-equivalences of the trivial gerbe Tors⁡(F)Tors𝐹\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(F)"Tors"(𝐹) with band F𝐹F𝐹; more generally, given gerbes 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 and 𝒢′superscript𝒢′\mathcal{G}^{\prime}𝒢' with band F𝐹F𝐹, then the set Hom𝖢⁡(𝒢,𝒢′)subscriptHom𝖢𝒢superscript𝒢′\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{C}}(\mathcal{G},\mathcal{G}^{\prime})Hom_𝖢(𝒢,𝒢') (assumed non-empty) of maps of gerbes is a torsor for H1⁢(F)superscript𝐻1𝐹H^{1}(F)𝐻¹⁢(𝐹).

Recall also that, for any sheaf F𝐹F𝐹 on a scheme V𝑉V𝑉, the cohomology HZ∗⁢(V,F)subscriptsuperscript𝐻𝑍𝑉𝐹H^{*}_{Z}(V,F)𝐻^∗_𝑍⁢(𝑉,𝐹) with support in a a closed subscheme Z𝑍Z𝑍 of V𝑉V𝑉 fits into an exact sequence [3, §5]

(5.4.1) ⋯⟶HZi⁢(V,F)⟶Hi⁢(V,F)⟶Hi⁢(V−Z,F)⟶HZi+1⁢(V,F)⟶⋯;⟶⋯subscriptsuperscript𝐻𝑖𝑍𝑉𝐹⟶superscript𝐻𝑖𝑉𝐹⟶superscript𝐻𝑖𝑉𝑍𝐹⟶subscriptsuperscript𝐻𝑖1𝑍𝑉𝐹⟶⋯\cdots\longrightarrow H^{i}_{Z}(V,F)\longrightarrow H^{i}(V,F)\longrightarrow H% ^{i}(V-Z,F)\longrightarrow H^{i+1}_{Z}(V,F)\longrightarrow\cdots;⋯⟶𝐻^𝑖_𝑍⁢(𝑉,𝐹)⟶𝐻^𝑖⁢(𝑉,𝐹)⟶𝐻^𝑖⁢(𝑉-𝑍,𝐹)⟶𝐻^{𝑖+1}_𝑍⁢(𝑉,𝐹)⟶⋯;

the exactness of

H1⁢(V,F)⟶H1⁢(V−Z,F)⟶HZ2⁢(V,F)⟶H2⁢(V,F)⟶H2⁢(V−Z,F)⟶superscript𝐻1𝑉𝐹superscript𝐻1𝑉𝑍𝐹⟶subscriptsuperscript𝐻2𝑍𝑉𝐹⟶superscript𝐻2𝑉𝐹⟶superscript𝐻2𝑉𝑍𝐹H^{1}(V,F)\longrightarrow H^{1}(V-Z,F)\longrightarrow H^{2}_{Z}(V,F)% \longrightarrow H^{2}(V,F)\longrightarrow H^{2}(V-Z,F)𝐻¹⁢(𝑉,𝐹)⟶𝐻¹⁢(𝑉-𝑍,𝐹)⟶𝐻²_𝑍⁢(𝑉,𝐹)⟶𝐻²⁢(𝑉,𝐹)⟶𝐻²⁢(𝑉-𝑍,𝐹)

leads to an interpretation of the group HZ2⁢(V,F)subscriptsuperscript𝐻2𝑍𝑉𝐹H^{2}_{Z}(V,F)𝐻²_𝑍⁢(𝑉,𝐹): it classifies isomorphism classes of pairs (𝒢,ϕ)𝒢italic-ϕ(\mathcal{G},\phi)(𝒢,ϕ) consisting of a gerbe 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 with band F𝐹F𝐹 on V𝑉V𝑉 and a trivialization ϕitalic-ϕ\phiϕ of 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 on V−Z𝑉𝑍V-Z𝑉-𝑍, i.e., ϕitalic-ϕ\phiϕ is an equivalence of 𝒢|V−Zevaluated-at𝒢𝑉𝑍\mathcal{G}|_{V-Z}{𝒢|}_{𝑉-𝑍} with Tors⁡(F|V−Z)Torsevaluated-at𝐹𝑉𝑍\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(F|_{V-Z})"Tors"({𝐹|}_{𝑉-𝑍}).

5.5. Geometric interpretation of some results of Bloch

Bloch [3] has proved that:

  1. (i)

    [3, Proposition 5.3] Any codimension-two cycle α𝛼\alpha𝛼 on X𝑋X𝑋 has a canonical cycle class [α]∈HZ2⁢(X,𝒦2)delimited-[]𝛼subscriptsuperscript𝐻2𝑍𝑋subscript𝒦2[\alpha]\in H^{2}_{Z}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})[𝛼]∈𝐻²_𝑍⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂); here Z𝑍Z𝑍 is the support of α𝛼\alpha𝛼.

  2. (ii)

    [3, Theorem 5.11] If D𝐷D𝐷 is a smooth divisor of X𝑋X𝑋, then Pic⁢(D)=H1⁢(D,𝒦1)Pic𝐷superscript𝐻1𝐷subscript𝒦1\mathrm{Pic}(D)=H^{1}(D,\mathcal{K}_{1})Pic⁢(𝐷)=𝐻¹⁢(𝐷,𝒦₁) is a direct summand of HD2⁢(X,𝒦2)subscriptsuperscript𝐻2𝐷𝑋subscript𝒦2H^{2}_{D}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝐻²_𝐷⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂).

For (1), we note that, by Remark 5.5, the gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 has a trivialization ηαsubscript𝜂𝛼\eta_{\alpha}𝜂_𝛼 on X−Z𝑋𝑍X-Z𝑋-𝑍. By the above interpretation of H2superscript𝐻2H^{2}𝐻² with support, the pair (𝒞α,ηα)subscript𝒞𝛼subscript𝜂𝛼(\mathcal{C}_{\alpha},\eta_{\alpha})(𝒞_𝛼,𝜂_𝛼) defines an element of HZ2⁢(X,𝒦2)subscriptsuperscript𝐻2𝑍𝑋subscript𝒦2H^{2}_{Z}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝐻²_𝑍⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂); this is the canonical class [α]delimited-[]𝛼[\alpha][𝛼].

For (2), recall that Bloch constructed maps a:Pic⁢(D)→HD2⁢(X,𝒦2):𝑎→Pic𝐷subscriptsuperscript𝐻2𝐷𝑋subscript𝒦2a:\mathrm{Pic}(D)\to H^{2}_{D}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝑎:Pic⁢(𝐷)→𝐻²_𝐷⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂) and b:HD2⁢(X,𝒦2)→Pic⁢(D):𝑏→subscriptsuperscript𝐻2𝐷𝑋subscript𝒦2Pic𝐷b:H^{2}_{D}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})\to\mathrm{Pic}(D)𝑏:𝐻²_𝐷⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂)→Pic⁢(𝐷) with b∘a𝑏𝑎b\circ a𝑏∘𝑎 the identity on Pic⁢(D)Pic𝐷\mathrm{Pic}(D)Pic⁢(𝐷). We can interpret the map a𝑎a𝑎 as follows. Note that any divisor E𝐸E𝐸 of D𝐷D𝐷 is a codimension-two cycle α𝛼\alpha𝛼 on X𝑋X𝑋. The 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 on X𝑋X𝑋 has a canonical trivialization ηαsubscript𝜂𝛼\eta_{\alpha}𝜂_𝛼 on X−E𝑋𝐸X-E𝑋-𝐸 (and so also on the smaller X−D𝑋𝐷X-D𝑋-𝐷). The association E↦(𝒞α,ηα)maps-to𝐸subscript𝒞𝛼subscript𝜂𝛼E\mapsto(\mathcal{C}_{\alpha},\eta_{\alpha})𝐸↦(𝒞_𝛼,𝜂_𝛼) gives the homomorphism a:Pic⁢(D)→HD2⁢(X,𝒦2):𝑎→Pic𝐷subscriptsuperscript𝐻2𝐷𝑋subscript𝒦2a:\mathrm{Pic}(D)\to H^{2}_{D}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝑎:Pic⁢(𝐷)→𝐻²_𝐷⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂).

These results of Bloch provide a partial answer to Question 5.7 summarized in the following

Proposition 5.8.

Let 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 be a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe on X𝑋XX and let β∈C⁢H2⁢(X)𝛽𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋\beta\in CH^{2}(X)β∈C⁢H²⁢(X) correspond to 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 in the Bloch-Quillen formula (5.1.3). Let ϕitalic-ϕ\phiϕ be a trivialization of 𝒢𝒢\mathcal{G}𝒢 on the complement X−D𝑋𝐷X-DX-D of a smooth divisor D𝐷DD of X𝑋XX. Then, β𝛽\betaβ can be represented by a divisor of D𝐷DD (unique up to rational equivalence on D𝐷DD).

Note that the data of ϕitalic-ϕ\phiϕ is crucial: the map Pic⁢(D)→C⁢H2⁢(X)→Pic𝐷𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋\mathrm{Pic}(D)\to CH^{2}(X)Pic⁢(𝐷)→𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋) is not injective in general [4, (iii), p. 269].

Proposition 5.9.

Let i:D→X:𝑖→𝐷𝑋i\colon D\to Xi:D→X and j:U=X−D→X:𝑗𝑈𝑋𝐷→𝑋j\colon U=X-D\to Xj:{U=X-D→X} be the inclusion maps. We have the following short exact sequence 111111For the notion of short exact sequence of Picard 2-stacks see, e.g. [2]. Briefly, we say that the diagram 𝔅⁢⟶ı⁢𝔈⁢⟶𝑝⁢𝔄𝔅italic-ı⟶𝔈𝑝⟶𝔄\mathfrak{B}\overset{\imath}{\longrightarrow}\mathfrak{E}\overset{p}{% \longrightarrow}\mathfrak{A}𝔅⁢⟶┬ı⁢𝔈⁢⟶┬p⁢𝔄 is a short exact sequence if: (1) the composition p∘ı𝑝italic-ıp\circ\imathp∘ı is naturally isomorphic to the zero morphism; (2) p𝑝pp is full and essentially surjective, and (3) ıitalic-ı\imathı is an equivalence between 𝔅𝔅\mathfrak{B}𝔅 and so-called “homotopy kernel” of p𝑝pp, consisting of those objects whose image by p𝑝pp is equivalent in 𝔄𝔄\mathfrak{A}𝔄 to the zero object. The meaning of “full” must be qualified: we want p𝑝pp to induce full and essentially surjective functors at the level of morphism categories (see loc. cit. for details). of Picard 2-stacks

Tors⁡(i∗⁢𝒦1D)⟶Gerbes⁡(𝒦2X)⟶Gerbes⁡(j∗⁢𝒦2U).⟶Torssubscript𝑖superscriptsubscript𝒦1𝐷Gerbessuperscriptsubscript𝒦2𝑋⟶Gerbessubscript𝑗superscriptsubscript𝒦2𝑈\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(i_{*}\mathcal{K}_{1}^{D})\longrightarrow% \operatorname{\textsc{Gerbes}}(\mathcal{K}_{2}^{X})\longrightarrow% \operatorname{\textsc{Gerbes}}(j_{*}\mathcal{K}_{2}^{U})."Tors"(𝑖_∗⁢𝒦₁^𝐷)⟶"Gerbes"(𝒦₂^𝑋)⟶"Gerbes"(𝑗_∗⁢𝒦₂^𝑈).
Proof.

Analyzing the Gersten sequence (5.1.1), (5.1.2) for 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂ on X𝑋X𝑋 and U𝑈U𝑈, we get the short exact sequence:

0⟶i∗⁢G1D⟶G2X⟶j∗⁢G2U⟶0.⟶0subscript𝑖superscriptsubscript𝐺1𝐷⟶superscriptsubscript𝐺2𝑋⟶subscript𝑗superscriptsubscript𝐺2𝑈⟶00\longrightarrow i_{*}G_{1}^{D}\longrightarrow G_{2}^{X}\longrightarrow j_{*}G% _{2}^{U}\longrightarrow 0.0⟶𝑖_∗⁢𝐺₁^𝐷⟶𝐺₂^𝑋⟶𝑗_∗⁢𝐺₂^𝑈⟶0.

This gives a short exact sequence of Picard 2-stacks, then use (2.3.3). Note that Tors⁡(i∗⁢𝒦1D)Torssubscript𝑖superscriptsubscript𝒦1𝐷\operatorname{\textsc{Tors}}(i_{*}\mathcal{K}_{1}^{D})"Tors"(𝑖_∗⁢𝒦₁^𝐷) is considered as a Picard 2-stack with no nontrivial 2-morphisms. ∎

The global long exact cohomology sequence arising from the exact sequence in the proposition gives part of the localization sequence for higher Chow groups

⋯⟶C⁢H1⁢(D,1)⟶C⁢H2⁢(X,1)⟶C⁢H2⁢(X−D,1)⟶Pic⁢(D)⟶C⁢H2⁢(X)⟶C⁢H2⁢(X−D)⟶0.⟶⋯𝐶superscript𝐻1𝐷1⟶𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋1⟶𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋𝐷1⟶Pic𝐷⟶𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋⟶𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋𝐷⟶0\cdots\longrightarrow CH^{1}(D,1)\longrightarrow CH^{2}(X,1)\longrightarrow CH% ^{2}(X-D,1)\longrightarrow\mathrm{Pic}(D)\longrightarrow CH^{2}(X)% \longrightarrow CH^{2}(X-D)\longrightarrow 0.⋯⟶𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝐷,1)⟶𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋,1)⟶𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋-𝐷,1)⟶Pic⁢(𝐷)⟶𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋)⟶𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋-𝐷)⟶0.

This uses the fact that C⁢H1⁢(D,0)=Pic⁢(D)𝐶superscript𝐻1𝐷0Pic𝐷CH^{1}(D,0)=\mathrm{Pic}(D)𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝐷,0)=Pic⁢(𝐷), that C⁢H1⁢(D,1)=H0⁢(D,𝒪∗)𝐶superscript𝐻1𝐷1superscript𝐻0𝐷superscript𝒪CH^{1}(D,1)=H^{0}(D,\mathcal{O}^{*})𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝐷,1)=𝐻⁰⁢(𝐷,𝒪^∗) and C⁢H1⁢(D,j)𝐶superscript𝐻1𝐷𝑗CH^{1}(D,j)𝐶⁢𝐻¹⁢(𝐷,𝑗) is zero for j>1𝑗1j>1𝑗>1 [4, (viii), p. 269].

5.6. The two gerbes associated with an intersection of divisors

For a codimension-two cycle α𝛼\alpha𝛼 of X𝑋X𝑋 presented as the intersection of divisors

α=D.D′,formulae-sequence𝛼𝐷superscript𝐷′\alpha=D.D^{\prime},𝛼=𝐷.𝐷',

we know that the 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbes

(i) the Gersten gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 in Theorem 5.4 and

(ii) the Heisenberg gerbe GD,D′subscript𝐺𝐷superscript𝐷′G_{D,D^{\prime}}𝐺_{𝐷,𝐷'} in §4.4 using Theorem 1.4

are equivalent (as their classes in H2⁢(X,𝒦2)superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂) corresponds to the class of the codimension-two cycle in C⁢H2⁢(X)𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋CH^{2}(X)𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋) via (5.1.3)). We now construct an actual equivalence between them.

Theorem 5.10.

Suppose that the codimension-two cycle α𝛼\alphaα is the intersection D.D′formulae-sequence𝐷superscript𝐷′D.D^{\prime}D.D' of divisors D𝐷DD and D′superscript𝐷′D^{\prime}D' on X𝑋XX. There is a natural equivalence121212By §5.4. the set of such equivalences is a torsor under the action of the group H1⁢(X,𝒦2)=C⁢H2⁢(X,1)superscript𝐻1𝑋subscript𝒦2𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋1H^{1}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})=CH^{2}(X,1)H¹⁢(X,𝒦₂)=C⁢H²⁢(X,1) [34, §2.1].

Θ:𝒞α→𝒢D,D′:Θ→subscript𝒞𝛼subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\Theta:\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}\to\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}Θ:𝒞_𝛼→𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'}

of 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbes on X𝑋XX.

Proof.

By Theorem 1.4 and Theorem 5.4, the classes of the gerbes 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} and 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 in H2⁢(X,𝒦2)superscript𝐻2𝑋subscript𝒦2H^{2}(X,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝐻²⁢(𝑋,𝒦₂) both correspond to the class of α𝛼\alpha𝛼 in C⁢H2⁢(X)𝐶superscript𝐻2𝑋CH^{2}(X)𝐶⁢𝐻²⁢(𝑋). This shows that they are equivalent.

Let us exhibit an actual equivalence. We will construct a functor ΘU:𝒞α⁢(U)→𝒢D,D′⁢(U):subscriptΘ𝑈→subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′𝑈\Theta_{U}:\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)\to\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}(U)Θ_𝑈:𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈)→𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'}⁢(𝑈), compatible with restriction maps V⊂U⊂X𝑉𝑈𝑋V\subset U\subset X𝑉⊂𝑈⊂𝑋. We will need the Heisenberg group H𝐻H𝐻 from (4.4.1).

Consider an object r∈𝒞α⁢(U)𝑟subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈r\in\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝑟∈𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈). We want to attach to r𝑟r𝑟 a H𝐻H𝐻-torsor ΘU⁢(r)subscriptΘ𝑈𝑟\Theta_{U}(r)Θ_𝑈⁢(𝑟) on U𝑈U𝑈 in a functorial manner. Each ΘU⁢(r)subscriptΘ𝑈𝑟\Theta_{U}(r)Θ_𝑈⁢(𝑟) is a H𝐻H𝐻-torsor which lifts the 𝒦1×𝒦1subscript𝒦1subscript𝒦1\mathcal{K}_{1}\times\mathcal{K}_{1}𝒦₁×𝒦₁-torsor 𝒪D×𝒪D′subscript𝒪𝐷subscript𝒪superscript𝐷′\mathcal{O}_{D}\times\mathcal{O}_{D^{\prime}}𝒪_𝐷×𝒪_𝐷' on U𝑈U𝑈. We will describe ΘU⁢(r)subscriptΘ𝑈𝑟\Theta_{U}(r)Θ_𝑈⁢(𝑟) by means of Čech cocycles. Fix an open covering {Ui}subscript𝑈𝑖\{U_{i}\}{𝑈_𝑖} of U𝑈U𝑈 and write 𝒞n⁢(A)superscript𝒞𝑛𝐴\mathcal{C}^{n}(A)𝒞^𝑛⁢(𝐴) for Čech n𝑛n𝑛-cochains with values in the sheaf A𝐴A𝐴 with respect to this covering.

Step 1.

Let a={ai,j}𝑎subscript𝑎𝑖𝑗a=\{a_{i,j}\}𝑎={𝑎_{𝑖,𝑗}} and b={bi,j}𝑏subscript𝑏𝑖𝑗b=\{b_{i,j}\}𝑏={𝑏_{𝑖,𝑗}} with a,b∈𝒞1⁢(O∗)𝑎𝑏superscript𝒞1superscript𝑂a,b\in\mathcal{C}^{1}(O^{*}){𝑎,𝑏}∈𝒞¹⁢(𝑂^∗) be Čech 1-cocycles for 𝒪Dsubscript𝒪𝐷\mathcal{O}_{D}𝒪_𝐷 and 𝒪D′subscript𝒪superscript𝐷′\mathcal{O}_{D^{\prime}}𝒪_𝐷'. Pick h={hi,j}∈𝒞1⁢(H)ℎsubscriptℎ𝑖𝑗superscript𝒞1𝐻h=\{h_{i,j}\}\in\mathcal{C}^{1}(H)ℎ={ℎ_{𝑖,𝑗}}∈𝒞¹⁢(𝐻) of the form

hi,j=(ai,j,bi,j,ci,j)∈H⁢(Ui∩Uj).subscriptℎ𝑖𝑗subscript𝑎𝑖𝑗subscript𝑏𝑖𝑗subscript𝑐𝑖𝑗𝐻subscript𝑈𝑖subscript𝑈𝑗h_{i,j}=(a_{i,j},b_{i,j},c_{i,j})\in H(U_{i}\cap U_{j}).ℎ_{𝑖,𝑗}=(𝑎_{𝑖,𝑗},𝑏_{𝑖,𝑗},𝑐_{𝑖,𝑗})∈𝐻⁢(𝑈_𝑖∩𝑈_𝑗).

We need ci,j∈K2⁢(Ui∩Uj)subscript𝑐𝑖𝑗subscript𝐾2subscript𝑈𝑖subscript𝑈𝑗c_{i,j}\in K_{2}(U_{i}\cap U_{j})𝑐_{𝑖,𝑗}∈𝐾₂⁢(𝑈_𝑖∩𝑈_𝑗) such that hℎhℎ is a Čech 1-cocycle (for ΘU⁢(r)subscriptΘ𝑈𝑟\Theta_{U}(r)Θ_𝑈⁢(𝑟), the putative H𝐻H𝐻-torsor). Since a,b𝑎𝑏a,b𝑎,𝑏 are Čech cocycles, the Čech boundary ∂hℎ\partial h∂ℎ is of the form

∂h={(1,1,yi,j,k)}ℎ11subscript𝑦𝑖𝑗𝑘\partial h=\{(1,1,y_{i,j,k})\}∂ℎ={(1,1,𝑦_{𝑖,𝑗,𝑘})}

with y={yi,j,k}∈𝒞2⁢(𝒦2)𝑦subscript𝑦𝑖𝑗𝑘superscript𝒞2subscript𝒦2y=\{y_{i,j,k}\}\in\mathcal{C}^{2}(\mathcal{K}_{2})𝑦={𝑦_{𝑖,𝑗,𝑘}}∈𝒞²⁢(𝒦₂) a Čech 2-cocycle. This cocycle y𝑦y𝑦 represents the gerbe 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} on U𝑈U𝑈.

Step 2.

Recall that 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 is the associated stack of the prestack U↦𝒞α⁢(U)maps-to𝑈subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈U\mapsto\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝑈↦𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈) where the category 𝒞α⁢(U)subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈) has objects u∈⊕x∈X1j∗⁢K1⁢(x)𝑢subscriptdirect-sum𝑥superscript𝑋1subscript𝑗subscript𝐾1𝑥u\in\oplus_{x\in X^{1}}j_{*}K_{1}(x)𝑢∈⊕_{𝑥∈𝑋¹}𝑗_∗⁢𝐾₁⁢(𝑥) with δ⁢u=ϕ⁢(1)𝛿𝑢italic-ϕ1\delta{u}=\phi(1)𝛿⁢𝑢=ϕ⁢(1) and morphisms from u𝑢u𝑢 to v𝑣v𝑣 are elements a∈K2η𝑎superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜂a\in K_{2}^{\eta}𝑎∈𝐾₂^𝜂 with ν⁢(a)=v−u𝜈𝑎𝑣𝑢\nu(a)=v-u𝜈⁢(𝑎)=𝑣-𝑢. Since the category 𝒞α⁢(U)subscript𝒞𝛼𝑈\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(U)𝒞_𝛼⁢(𝑈) is non-empty, the class of the gerbe 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 (restricted to U𝑈U𝑈) in H2⁢(U,𝒦2)superscript𝐻2𝑈subscript𝒦2H^{2}(U,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝐻²⁢(𝑈,𝒦₂) is zero. Since 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼 and 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} are equivalent, so the class of 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} in H2⁢(U,𝒦2)superscript𝐻2𝑈subscript𝒦2H^{2}(U,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝐻²⁢(𝑈,𝒦₂) is also zero.

Step 3.

Consider the case in which the chosen object r𝑟r𝑟 is given by a pair (C,g)𝐶𝑔(C,g)(𝐶,𝑔) where C𝐶C𝐶 is a divisor on X𝑋X𝑋 and g𝑔g𝑔 is a rational function on C𝐶C𝐶. The condition δ⁢(r)=ϕ⁢(1)𝛿𝑟italic-ϕ1\delta(r)=\phi(1)𝛿⁢(𝑟)=ϕ⁢(1) says α∩U𝛼𝑈\alpha\cap U𝛼∩𝑈 is the intersection of U𝑈U𝑈 with the zero locus of g𝑔g𝑔. Assume g∈𝒪C⁢(C∩U)𝑔subscript𝒪𝐶𝐶𝑈g\in\mathcal{O}_{C}(C\cap U)𝑔∈𝒪_𝐶⁢(𝐶∩𝑈). Given any lifting g~∈𝒪X⁢(U)~𝑔subscript𝒪𝑋𝑈\tilde{g}\in\mathcal{O}_{X}(U)𝑔̃∈𝒪_𝑋⁢(𝑈) of g𝑔g𝑔, let C′superscript𝐶′C^{\prime}𝐶' be its divisor on U𝑈U𝑈. Notice that the codimension-two cycle α∩U𝛼𝑈\alpha\cap U𝛼∩𝑈 is the intersection of the divisors C∩U𝐶𝑈C\cap U𝐶∩𝑈 and the (principal) divisor C′superscript𝐶′C^{\prime}𝐶' in U𝑈U𝑈. So we can apply the results in §4.4 to the codimension-two cycle

α∩U=(C∩U).C′formulae-sequence𝛼𝑈𝐶𝑈superscript𝐶′\alpha\cap U=(C\cap U).C^{\prime}𝛼∩𝑈=(𝐶∩𝑈).𝐶'

on the smooth variety U𝑈U𝑈; we obtain a 𝒦2subscript𝒦2\mathcal{K}_{2}𝒦₂-gerbe 𝒢C∩U,C′subscript𝒢𝐶𝑈superscript𝐶′\mathcal{G}_{C\cap U,C^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐶∩𝑈,𝐶'} on U𝑈U𝑈. As C′superscript𝐶′C^{\prime}𝐶' is a principal divisor, its class in H1⁢(U,𝒦1)superscript𝐻1𝑈subscript𝒦1H^{1}(U,\mathcal{K}_{1})𝐻¹⁢(𝑈,𝒦₁) is zero; also zero is the class of 𝒢C∩U,C′subscript𝒢𝐶𝑈superscript𝐶′\mathcal{G}_{C\cap U,C^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐶∩𝑈,𝐶'} in H2⁢(U,𝒦2)superscript𝐻2𝑈subscript𝒦2H^{2}(U,\mathcal{K}_{2})𝐻²⁢(𝑈,𝒦₂) because it is the image of the cup product of the classes of C∩U𝐶𝑈C\cap U𝐶∩𝑈 and C′superscript𝐶′C^{\prime}𝐶' in H1⁢(U,𝒦1)superscript𝐻1𝑈subscript𝒦1H^{1}(U,\mathcal{K}_{1})𝐻¹⁢(𝑈,𝒦₁).

Step 4.

Let z={zi,j,k}∈𝒞2⁢(𝒦2)𝑧subscript𝑧𝑖𝑗𝑘superscript𝒞2subscript𝒦2z=\{z_{i,j,k}\}\in\mathcal{C}^{2}(\mathcal{K}_{2})𝑧={𝑧_{𝑖,𝑗,𝑘}}∈𝒞²⁢(𝒦₂) be a Čech 2-cocycle for 𝒢C∩U,C′subscript𝒢𝐶𝑈superscript𝐶′\mathcal{G}_{C\cap U,C^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐶∩𝑈,𝐶'};

So z=∂w𝑧𝑤z=\partial w𝑧=∂𝑤 is the boundary of a Čech cochain w={wi,j}∈𝒞1⁢(𝒦2)𝑤subscript𝑤𝑖𝑗superscript𝒞1subscript𝒦2w=\{w_{i,j}\}\in\mathcal{C}^{1}(\mathcal{K}_{2})𝑤={𝑤_{𝑖,𝑗}}∈𝒞¹⁢(𝒦₂). Note that y−z=∂v𝑦𝑧𝑣y-z=\partial v𝑦-𝑧=∂𝑣 for a 1-cochain v𝑣v𝑣 since 𝒢C∩U,C′subscript𝒢𝐶𝑈superscript𝐶′\mathcal{G}_{C\cap U,C^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐶∩𝑈,𝐶'} and 𝒢D,D′subscript𝒢𝐷superscript𝐷′\mathcal{G}_{D,D^{\prime}}𝒢_{𝐷,𝐷'} are equivalent as gerbes on U𝑈U𝑈: both are trivial on U𝑈U𝑈!

The Čech cochain h′={hi,j′}∈𝒞1⁢(H)superscriptℎ′subscriptsuperscriptℎ′𝑖𝑗superscript𝒞1𝐻h^{\prime}=\{h^{\prime}_{i,j}\}\in\mathcal{C}^{1}(H)ℎ'={ℎ'_{𝑖,𝑗}}∈𝒞¹⁢(𝐻) with

hi,j′=(ai,j,bi,j,ci,j)⁢(1,1,−wi,j)⁢(1,1,−vi,j)subscriptsuperscriptℎ′𝑖𝑗subscript𝑎𝑖𝑗subscript𝑏𝑖𝑗subscript𝑐𝑖𝑗11subscript𝑤𝑖𝑗11subscript𝑣𝑖𝑗h^{\prime}_{i,j}=(a_{i,j},b_{i,j},c_{i,j})(1,1,-w_{i,j})(1,1,-v_{i,j})ℎ'_{𝑖,𝑗}=(𝑎_{𝑖,𝑗},𝑏_{𝑖,𝑗},𝑐_{𝑖,𝑗})⁢(1,1,-𝑤_{𝑖,𝑗})⁢(1,1,-𝑣_{𝑖,𝑗})

is a Čech cocycle and represents the required H𝐻H𝐻-torsor ΘU⁢(r)subscriptΘ𝑈𝑟\Theta_{U}(r)Θ_𝑈⁢(𝑟) on U𝑈U𝑈.

Step 5.

The same argument with simple modifications works for a general object of 𝒞αsubscript𝒞𝛼\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}𝒞_𝛼. It is easy to check that ΘUsubscriptΘ𝑈\Theta_{U}Θ_𝑈 is a functor, compatible with restriction maps V⊂U⊂X𝑉𝑈𝑋V\subset U\subset X𝑉⊂𝑈⊂𝑋, and that the induced morphism of gerbes is an equivalence. ∎

  • [1] Théorie des topos et cohomologie étale des schémas. Tome 2. Lecture Notes in Mathematics, Vol. 270. Springer-Verlag, Berlin-New York, 1972. Séminaire de Géométrie Algébrique du Bois-Marie 1963–1964 (SGA 4), Dirigé par M. Artin, A. Grothendieck et J. L. Verdier. Avec la collaboration de N. Bourbaki, P. Deligne et B. Saint-Donat.
  • [2] Cristiana Bertolin and A. Emin Tatar. Extensions of Picard 2-stacks and the cohomology groups ExtisuperscriptExt𝑖\mathrm{Ext}^{i}Ext^𝑖 of length 3 complexes. Ann. Mat. Pura ed Applicata, 193:291–315, 2013

  • [4] Spencer Bloch. Algebraic cycles and higher K𝐾K𝐾-theory. Adv. in Math., 61(3):267–304, 1986.
  • [5] Lawrence Breen. Extensions du groupe additif. Inst. Hautes Études Sci. Publ. Math., (48):39–125, 1978.
  • [6] Lawrence Breen. Bitorseurs et cohomologie non abélienne. In The Grothendieck Festschrift, Vol. I, volume 86 of Progr. Math., pages 401–476. Birkhäuser Boston, Boston, MA, 1990.
  • [7] Lawrence Breen. On the classification of 2222-gerbes and 2222-stacks. Astérisque, (225):160, 1994.
  • [8] Lawrence Breen. Monoidal categories and multiextensions. Compositio Math., 117(3):295–335, 1999.
  • [9] Jean-Luc Brylinski. Loop spaces, characteristic classes and geometric quantization, volume 107 of Progress in Mathematics. Birkhäuser Boston, Inc., Boston, MA, 1993.
  • [10] Jean-Luc Brylinski. Holomorphic gerbes and the Beĭlinson regulator. Astérisque, (226):8, 145–174, 1994. K𝐾K𝐾-theory (Strasbourg, 1992).
  • [11] Ph. Cassou-Noguès, T. Chinburg, B. Morin, and M. J. Taylor. The classifying topos of a group scheme and invariants of symmetric bundles J. Proc. Lond. Math. Soc., (109):3 1093–1136, 2014.
  • [12] Jean-Luc Brylinski and Pierre Deligne. Central extensions of reductive groups by 𝕂2subscript𝕂2\mathbb{K}_{2}𝕂₂. Publ. Math. Inst. Hautes Études Sci., (94):5–85, 2001.
  • [13] P. Deligne, La formule de dualité globale, Exposé XVIII in Théorie des topos et cohomologie étale des schémas. Tome 3. Lecture Notes in Mathematics, Vol. 305. Springer-Verlag, Berlin-New York, 1973. Séminaire de Géométrie Algébrique du Bois-Marie 1963–1964 (SGA 4), Dirigé par M. Artin, A. Grothendieck et J. L. Verdier. Avec la collaboration de P. Deligne et B. Saint-Donat.

  • [15] P. Deligne, Le déterminant de la cohomologie. In Current trends in arithmetical algebraic geometry (Arcata, Calif., 1985), volume 67 of Contemp. Math., pages 93–177. Amer. Math. Soc., Providence, RI, 1987.

  • [19] Pawel Gajer. Geometry of higher connections. J. Reine Angew. Math. 522 (2000), 143–159.
